+# External Protocol: WhisperKit Integration
+
+**Contract Type:** External dependency protocol (third-party Swift package)
+**Redesignable:** NO -- WhisperKit API surface is imposed by the package; we consume it.
+**Package source:** `https://github.com/argmaxinc/WhisperKit.git`, minimum version 0.16.0
+
+---
+
+## 1. Consumed API Surface
+
+### 1.1 Model Download
+
+| API | Usage | Context |
+|-----|-------|---------|
+| `WhisperKit.download(variant:downloadBase:useBackgroundSession:)` | Downloads Whisper model files to disk | Called during model loading when model is not cached |
+
+The download reports progress as a fraction (0.0 to 1.0). Progress is cleared after download completes.
+
+### 1.2 Engine Initialization
+
+| API | Usage | Parameters |
+|-----|-------|------------|
+| `WhisperKit(WhisperKitConfig(...))` | Creates and initializes the Whisper inference engine | verbose: false, logLevel: error-only, prewarm: false, load: true |
+
+### 1.3 Transcription
+
+| API | Usage | Input | Output |
+|-----|-------|-------|--------|
+| `whisperKit.transcribe(audioArray:)` | Runs speech-to-text inference on raw audio | `[Float]` at 16 kHz mono | Array of result segments, each with a text property |
+
+Result segments are joined with spaces, trimmed, and then passed through artifact removal.
+
+---
+
+## 2. Model Cache Path Convention
+
+WhisperKit models are cached under `~/Library/Application Support/GhostPepper/whisper-models/`. The system ensures this directory exists before downloading. The model descriptor carries cache path components that determine the subdirectory structure (e.g., `models/openai/whisper-small.en`).
+
+### 2.1 Cache Check
+
+To determine whether a model is already cached on disk (without downloading), the system checks for the existence of the directory constructed from the model's cache path components under the whisper models root.
+
+---
+
+## 3. Model Catalog (WhisperKit Variants)
+
+Three Whisper models are consumed from WhisperKit:
+
+| App Model ID | WhisperKit Variant | Size | Language |
+|-------------|-------------------|------|----------|
+| `openai_whisper-tiny.en` | `tiny.en` | ~75 MB | English only |
+| `openai_whisper-small.en` | `small.en` | ~466 MB | English only |
+| `openai_whisper-small` | `small` | ~466 MB | Multilingual |
+
+None of these models support speaker filtering (speaker filtering is a FluidAudio-only capability).
+
+---
+
+## 4. Error Handling Contract
+
+| Condition | Behavior |
+|-----------|----------|
+| Transcription error from WhisperKit | Caught, logged, return nil (no retry) |
+| Download timeout | Retry once after 500ms delay |
+| Non-timeout download/load failure | Transition to error state immediately |
+| Retry also fails | Transition to error state |
+
+---
+
+## 5. Artifact Removal (Post-WhisperKit Processing)
+
+Seven hallucination artifact strings are removed from WhisperKit transcription output (NOT applied to FluidAudio output):
+
+1. `[BLANK_AUDIO]`
+2. `[NO_SPEECH]`
+3. `(blank audio)`
+4. `(no speech)`
+5. `[MUSIC]`
+6. `[APPLAUSE]`
+7. `[LAUGHTER]`
+
+Each occurrence is replaced with empty string, then leading/trailing whitespace is trimmed.

@@ -0,0 +1,596 @@
+# Core Pipeline
+
+This specification describes the end-to-end behavior of a voice-to-text pipeline that captures microphone audio, converts speech to text, optionally refines the text, and delivers the result to the active application. It also covers audio device management, audio data formats, and sound effect feedback.
+
+---
+
+## 1. Pipeline Overview
+
+The core pipeline is the central behavioral thread of the application. A single session proceeds through a fixed sequence: hotkey activation, audio capture, speech-to-text conversion, optional text refinement, delivery to the target application, and optional post-delivery learning. Every session follows exclusive ownership rules that prevent concurrent use.
+
+---
+
+## 2. Session Lifecycle
+
+### 2.1 Successful Session Sequence
+
+A successful session proceeds through these stages in strict order:
+
+1. The global hotkey is detected (push-to-talk press or toggle-to-talk press).
+2. A performance trace is created with a unique session identifier and the hotkey detection timestamp.
+3. Exclusive pipeline ownership is acquired. If the pipeline is already in use, the session is silently abandoned.
+4. If "ignore other speakers" is enabled and the active speech model supports speaker filtering, a speaker filtering session is prepared to receive audio chunks in real time.
+5. If text refinement is enabled, a refinement model is available, and frontmost-window context capture is enabled, a background screen capture and text recognition task is launched. Otherwise, any pending capture task is cancelled.
+6. Audio recording begins: the sample buffer is cleared, the microphone tap is installed, and the audio engine starts.
+7. A start-recording sound effect plays (if sounds are enabled).
+8. The floating overlay appears showing "Recording..." with a pulsing red indicator.
+9. The application status transitions to "Recording".
+10. The user releases the push-to-talk keys or presses the toggle key a second time.
+11. The hotkey release timestamp is recorded on the performance trace.
+12. Audio recording stops after a 200-millisecond flush delay. The microphone tap is removed, the engine stops, and the accumulated audio buffer is returned.
+13. A stop-recording sound effect plays.
+14. The application status transitions to "Transcribing" and the overlay updates to show "Transcribing..." with a blue indicator.
+15. The transcription start timestamp is recorded on the performance trace.
+16. If frontmost-window context capture was launched, its result is awaited and its duration is recorded on the trace.
+17. The audio buffer is transcribed. If speaker filtering is active, the speaker timeline is resolved first: the target speaker's audio segments are extracted and transcribed. If speaker filtering fails or is inactive, the full buffer is transcribed.
+18. The transcription end timestamp is recorded on the performance trace.
+19. If text refinement is enabled and a refinement model is available, the status transitions to "Cleaning up" and the overlay updates to show "Cleaning up..." with a blue indicator.
+20. The transcribed text is refined: a prompt is assembled (optionally including screen context and correction hints), the language model runs inference, and the output undergoes sanitization and post-processing corrections.
+21. The refinement end timestamp is recorded on the performance trace.
+22. The session is archived: the audio buffer, screen context, raw transcription, refined transcription, and speaker analysis summary are stored in the experiment archive.
+23. If the debug log viewer is open, a debug snapshot is recorded containing the raw transcription, refinement configuration, window context summary, and final output.
+24. The final text is delivered to the target application via clipboard manipulation and a simulated paste keystroke. If no suitable paste target is detected, the text is placed on the clipboard and a fallback notification is shown.
+25. The performance trace is completed: the paste-end timestamp is recorded, and the full trace summary is written to the debug log. The trace is then cleared.
+26. The overlay is dismissed.
+27. The application status returns to "Ready".
+28. Exclusive pipeline ownership is released.
+
+### 2.2 Sequencing Rules
+
+- The pipeline must acquire exclusive ownership before recording begins. Recording must not start if acquisition fails.
+- The screen capture prefetch must launch before audio recording starts so that capture time overlaps with the recording duration. The prefetch must not be resolved until after recording stops.
+- Transcription must complete before text refinement begins. These stages are sequential.
+- If a speaker filtering session is active, it must be finalized before fallback full-buffer transcription is attempted. Filtered transcription takes priority.
+- The pipeline must release ownership after all processing completes, regardless of whether the session produced any text.
+
+---
+
+## 3. Application Status
+
+### 3.1 Status Values
+
+The application maintains a single observable status with six possible values:
+
+| Status | Display Text | Meaning |
+|--------|-------------|---------|
+| Loading | "Loading model..." | Speech model is downloading or initializing |
+| Ready | "Ready" | Idle; waiting for the user to begin recording |
+| Recording | "Recording..." | Microphone is actively capturing audio |
+| Transcribing | "Transcribing..." | Audio is being converted to text |
+| Cleaning Up | "Cleaning up..." | Transcribed text is being refined by the language model |
+| Error | "Error" | A failure condition requiring user attention |
+
+### 3.2 Status Transitions
+
+```
+                             +-----------+
+                             |  Loading   |
+                             +-----+-----+
+                                   |
+                     model loaded  | model load failed
+                     successfully  |
+                          |        v
+                          |   +---------+
+                          +-->|  Error  |<----+
+                          |   +----+----+     |
+                          |        |          |
+                          |  retry/|          |  recording
+                          |  model |          |  start fails
+                          |  loads |          |
+                          v        v          |
+                       +--------+             |
+          +----------->| Ready  +-------------+
+          |            +---+----+
+          |                |
+          |       hotkey   |
+          |       press    |
+          |                v
+          |          +-----------+
+          |          | Recording |
+          |          +-----+-----+
+          |                |
+          |       hotkey   |
+          |       release  |
+          |                v
+          |        +--------------+
+          |        | Transcribing |
+          |        +------+-------+
+          |               |
+          |    +----------+----------+
+          |    |                     |
+          |    | refinement     no refinement
+          |    | available      or unavailable
+          |    v                     |
+          | +------------+           |
+          | | Cleaning Up|           |
+          | +------+-----+           |
+          |        |                 |
+          +--------+-----------------+
+              pipeline complete
+```
+
+- The initial status must be "Loading".
+- The status must transition from "Loading" to "Ready" only when the speech model has loaded successfully and the hotkey monitor has started.
+- The status must transition from "Ready" to "Recording" when a recording successfully starts.
+- The status must transition from "Recording" to "Transcribing" when recording stops and transcription begins.
+- The status must transition from "Transcribing" to "Cleaning Up" only if text refinement is enabled and a refinement model is available.
+- If refinement is not available, the status must remain "Transcribing" until delivery completes, then transition directly to "Ready".
+- The status must return to "Ready" after the pipeline completes, regardless of whether a transcript was produced.
+- The status must transition to "Error" if recording fails to start (audio engine error) or if required permissions are missing.
+- When the status is "Loading" and the user triggers a recording, the overlay must show a "Loading models..." message for 1.5 seconds and the recording attempt must be silently dropped.
+
+### 3.3 Menu Bar Icon
+
+| Status | Icon Style |
+|--------|-----------|
+| Recording | Custom red icon |
+| Loading | Ellipsis circle, orange |
+| Error | Warning triangle, yellow |
+| Ready, Transcribing, Cleaning Up | Custom icon adapting to system theme |
+
+---
+
+## 4. Pipeline Exclusivity
+
+### 4.1 Ownership Model
+
+The pipeline uses a simple ownership model to prevent concurrent access. Two consumers may request ownership: the live recording session and the experiment archive replayer. Ownership is tracked as a single optional value; when unset, the pipeline is free.
+
+### 4.2 Acquiring Ownership
+
+- Acquiring the pipeline must succeed only if no owner currently holds it. On success, the caller becomes the owner. On failure, the pipeline remains unchanged.
+- When acquisition fails for a live recording, the attempt must be silently skipped with a debug log entry and the performance trace must be abandoned.
+- When acquisition fails for an experiment archive operation, a "pipeline busy" error must be reported to the caller.
+
+### 4.3 Releasing Ownership
+
+- Releasing the pipeline must succeed only if the caller is the current owner. Release by a non-owner must be silently ignored.
+- The pipeline must be released at the end of every session, whether it succeeded or failed. There is no automatic timeout or release mechanism.
+
+---
+
+## 5. Re-Entrancy Protection
+
+### 5.1 Transcription Guard
+
+A flag prevents the stop-and-transcribe path from executing concurrently:
+
+- If transcription is already in progress, a second stop-and-transcribe request must be silently ignored.
+- The flag must be cleared when transcription completes, regardless of success or failure. Cleanup on exit ensures the flag resets even if transcription returns early.
+
+### 5.2 Recording Start Guard
+
+- Recording must not start unless the application status is "Ready". Attempts from any other status must be silently ignored (with the model-loading overlay shown if the status is "Loading").
+
+---
+
+## 6. Empty Transcription Handling
+
+### 6.1 Disposition Based on Recording Duration
+
+When transcription produces no text, the behavior depends on how long the recording lasted:
+
+- If the audio buffer contains fewer than 80,000 samples (approximately 5 seconds at 16 kHz), the recording is silently cancelled with no user-visible feedback beyond dismissing the overlay.
+- If the audio buffer contains 80,000 or more samples, the overlay shows "No sound detected" with an orange indicator.
+
+### 6.2 Pipeline Behavior for Empty Results
+
+- When no transcript is produced, the recording must still be archived (if the audio buffer is non-empty), with empty values for raw and refined transcription.
+- The performance trace must be completed at the point where the empty result is detected (not after delivery, since there is nothing to deliver).
+- The transcription end timestamp must still be recorded on the performance trace even when the result is empty.
+
+### 6.3 "No Sound Detected" Overlay
+
+- The "No sound detected" message must auto-dismiss after 3 seconds.
+- It must show the text "No sound detected" with no secondary text.
+
+---
+
+## 7. Performance Tracing
+
+### 7.1 Trace Creation
+
+- A new performance trace must be created when a push-to-talk or toggle-to-talk hotkey is first pressed. It must contain a unique session identifier and the hotkey detection timestamp.
+- If recording starts without an active trace (edge case: direct invocation bypassing the hotkey path), a trace must be created as a fallback before pipeline acquisition.
+
+### 7.2 Milestone Timestamps
+
+The following timestamps are recorded on the active trace during a session:
+
+| Milestone | When Recorded |
+|-----------|--------------|
+| Hotkey detected | At trace creation |
+| Microphone live | When the audio engine starts capturing |
+| Hotkey lifted | When push-to-talk keys are released or toggle is pressed again |
+| Microphone cold | When the audio engine stops capturing |
+| Transcription start | Just before transcription begins |
+| Screen capture duration | From the prefetch result's elapsed time |
+| Transcription end | When transcription completes (both empty and non-empty paths) |
+| Refinement start | When text refinement begins (if attempted) |
+| Refinement end | When text refinement completes |
+| Prompt build duration | Time spent assembling the refinement prompt |
+| Model inference duration | Time spent in language model inference |
+| Post-processing duration | Time spent in output post-processing |
+| Delivery start | When text delivery begins |
+| Delivery end | When text delivery completes |
+
+### 7.3 Trace Completion
+
+- The trace must be completed after text delivery finishes or when an empty transcription is detected.
+- On completion, if the delivery end timestamp was not already recorded (e.g., empty transcription path), the current time must be used as a fallback.
+- On completion, the trace summary must be written to the debug log as a performance entry. The summary includes the speech model identifier, refinement backend, and millisecond-formatted durations for each stage.
+- After completion, the active trace must be cleared and the refinement-attempted flag must be reset.
+- On completion, any pending screen capture prefetch must be cancelled.
+
+### 7.4 Trace Abandonment
+
+- If pipeline acquisition fails, the trace must be silently abandoned without writing a summary.
+- If recording start fails (audio engine error), the trace must be silently abandoned.
+
+### 7.5 Trace Summary Format
+
+The trace summary is a single space-separated string of key=value pairs:
+
+- `session=<id>` -- unique session identifier
+- `speechModel=<id>` -- active speech model identifier
+- `cleanupBackend=<value>` -- refinement backend setting
+- `hotkey_to_mic_live=<N>ms` -- latency from hotkey detection to microphone active
+- `hotkey_lift_to_mic_cold=<N>ms` -- latency from hotkey release to microphone stopped
+- `transcription=<N>ms` -- transcription duration
+- `cleanup=<N>ms` or `cleanup=skipped` -- refinement duration or "skipped" if not attempted
+- `ocr=<N>ms` -- screen capture duration (if refinement was attempted)
+- `prompt_build=<N>ms` -- prompt assembly duration (if refinement was attempted)
+- `model_call=<N>ms` -- language model inference duration (if refinement was attempted)
+- `post_process=<N>ms` -- post-processing duration (if refinement was attempted)
+- `paste=<N>ms` -- text delivery duration
+- `total=<N>ms` -- total pipeline duration from trace start to delivery end
+
+Missing values display as `n/a`.
+
+---
+
+## 8. Audio Capture
+
+### 8.1 Pre-Warming
+
+The audio engine supports an optional pre-warm operation that forces initialization of the input processing chain and prepares the engine. This reduces latency on the first recording start. Pre-warming is not required before recording; it is a performance optimization only.
+
+### 8.2 Starting a Recording
+
+When recording starts, the system must perform these steps in order:
+
+1. Clear the sample buffer, discarding any previously accumulated audio data.
+2. Query the audio format from the engine's input on bus 0.
+3. Log the input device's sample rate and channel count.
+4. Validate the input format: sample rate must be greater than zero and channel count must be greater than zero. If either fails, report a "no input available" error.
+5. Create a format converter from the input format to the target format (16 kHz, mono, 32-bit float). If creation fails, report a "converter creation failed" error.
+6. Calculate the tap buffer size as approximately 100 milliseconds of audio at the input device's sample rate (input sample rate multiplied by 0.1, truncated to an integer frame count).
+7. Install an audio tap on the input bus using the calculated buffer size and the input format. The tap feeds each buffer through the format conversion pipeline.
+8. Start the audio engine.
+9. Fire the recording-started notification.
+
+### 8.3 Stopping a Recording
+
+When recording stops, the system must perform these steps in order:
+
+1. Wait 200 milliseconds (the flush delay) to allow remaining audio buffers to drain through the engine's processing pipeline. This wait is asynchronous and cancellable; if cancelled, the remaining steps proceed immediately.
+2. Remove the audio tap from the input bus.
+3. Stop the engine.
+4. Fire the recording-stopped notification.
+5. Copy the accumulated sample buffer under lock and return the copy.
+6. Log the total sample count, equivalent duration in seconds (sample count divided by 16,000), and the maximum absolute amplitude across all samples if the buffer is non-empty.
+
+### 8.4 Flush Delay
+
+The 200-millisecond flush delay between the stop command and engine teardown prevents loss of the final fraction of speech. It is the primary source of latency between the user releasing the hotkey and transcription beginning. The performance trace captures this via the "hotkey lift to mic cold" measurement.
+
+---
+
+## 9. Audio Format Conversion
+
+### 9.1 Target Format
+
+All captured audio is converted to the following format for compatibility with the downstream speech-to-text engine:
+
+| Parameter | Value |
+|-----------|-------|
+| Sample rate | 16,000 Hz |
+| Channel count | 1 (mono) |
+| Sample type | 32-bit floating point |
+| Layout | Non-interleaved |
+
+### 9.2 Conversion Steps
+
+For each buffer delivered by the microphone tap:
+
+1. Calculate the output frame count from the ratio of the target sample rate to the input sample rate, applied to the input buffer's frame length, plus 1 to prevent rounding down to zero.
+2. Allocate an output buffer in the target format with the calculated capacity. If allocation fails, silently skip this buffer.
+3. Convert samples using the platform audio converter with a data-provider callback. The callback provides the input buffer on its first invocation and signals "no data" on all subsequent invocations, ensuring the input is consumed exactly once.
+4. If the converter reports an error, log the error and silently drop the buffer. Recording continues with subsequent buffers.
+5. Read the float channel data from channel 0 of the converted buffer. If no data is available or the frame length is zero, silently skip.
+6. Append the extracted frames to the accumulation buffer using the thread-safe append operation.
+
+---
+
+## 10. Audio Buffer Management
+
+### 10.1 Buffer Storage
+
+The audio buffer stores an array of 32-bit floating-point samples. A newly created recorder must have an empty buffer.
+
+### 10.2 Thread Safety
+
+All reads and writes to the audio buffer must be protected by a mutual exclusion lock. The lock is held only for the duration of the memory operation (no I/O or callbacks inside the critical section).
+
+### 10.3 Buffer Operations
+
+- **Reset:** Acquire the lock, set the buffer to empty, release the lock.
+- **Append:** Acquire the lock, append new frames, release the lock, then fire the converted-audio-chunk notification with the newly appended frames. The notification must fire outside the lock to avoid holding it during potentially long notification processing.
+- **Read (on stop):** Acquire the lock, copy the entire buffer, release the lock. Return the copy.
+
+### 10.4 Dual Delivery
+
+Each converted audio chunk is both delivered via the real-time notification and accumulated in the buffer. The notification delivers incremental chunks; the buffer accumulates the complete recording.
+
+---
+
+## 11. Audio Device Management
+
+Device management is stateless. All operations are independent queries or mutations against the operating system's audio hardware service.
+
+### 11.1 Device Identity
+
+Each input device is represented by a numeric platform device identifier (used as the identity key) and a human-readable name string.
+
+### 11.2 Listing Input Devices
+
+The system must enumerate all audio devices from the operating system's hardware service and filter to include only devices that have at least one input channel and a resolvable human-readable name. Devices failing either condition are excluded. If any hardware query fails, the system returns an empty list.
+
+### 11.3 Default Input Device
+
+- Querying the default input device returns the current system default, or nothing if the query fails.
+- Setting the default input device writes the specified device identifier to the system property and returns a success or failure indicator.
+
+### 11.4 Input Channel Detection
+
+To determine whether a device has input channels, the system queries the device's stream configuration in the input scope and sums the channel count across all audio streams. A device has input channels if and only if the total count is greater than zero.
+
+### 11.5 Device Name Resolution
+
+To resolve a device name, the system queries the device's name property in the global scope. If the query fails, the name is unresolvable and the device is excluded from the input device list.
+
+---
+
+## 12. Audio Serialization
+
+The system supports two serialization formats for audio data: a raw binary format for exact sample preservation, and a WAV format for playable archive files.
+
+### 12.1 Raw Float32 Format
+
+- Serialization converts the float sample array directly to a byte buffer via memory copy.
+- Deserialization validates that the data length is an exact multiple of 4 bytes (the size of a 32-bit float). If validation fails, an "invalid serialized audio data" error is reported.
+- Raw serialization must round-trip losslessly.
+
+### 12.2 WAV Format
+
+WAV serialization produces a complete, valid WAV file in memory with these parameters:
+
+| Parameter | Value |
+|-----------|-------|
+| Sample rate | 16,000 Hz |
+| Channel count | 1 (mono) |
+| Bits per sample | 16 (signed integer PCM) |
+| Audio format code | 1 (uncompressed PCM) |
+
+The WAV file must follow the RIFF/WAVE standard:
+
+1. "RIFF" chunk header (4 bytes), followed by total chunk size (4 bytes little-endian). The chunk size is 36 plus the data payload size.
+2. "WAVE" format identifier (4 bytes).
+3. "fmt " sub-chunk (4 bytes) with a 16-byte payload containing: format code (1 = PCM), channel count, sample rate, byte rate, block alignment, and bits per sample. All multi-byte values in little-endian byte order.
+4. "data" sub-chunk (4 bytes) with the sample data payload.
+
+Float-to-integer conversion for WAV samples must: clamp each sample to [-1.0, 1.0], scale by 32,767, round to the nearest integer, and write as a 16-bit signed integer in little-endian byte order.
+
+WAV round-trip must preserve sample values within a tolerance of 0.0001 (exact equality is not required due to the lossy float-to-int16-to-float conversion).
+
+### 12.3 WAV Deserialization
+
+The deserialization entry point must auto-detect the format:
+- If the data begins with the bytes "RIFF", parse as WAV.
+- Otherwise, parse as raw Float32.
+
+WAV deserialization must validate:
+- The data is at least 44 bytes long (minimum WAV header size).
+- The data starts with "RIFF".
+- Bytes 8 through 11 are "WAVE".
+
+Sub-chunk parsing must begin at byte offset 12 and iterate through chunks. For each sub-chunk, a 4-byte ASCII identifier and 4-byte little-endian size are read. If the chunk data extends beyond the end of the input, an error is reported. Odd-sized chunks must be followed by one padding byte (RIFF word-alignment rule).
+
+When the "fmt " sub-chunk is encountered, it must be at least 16 bytes. The system extracts the audio format code, channel count, and bits per sample.
+
+After parsing, the system validates: format code is 1 (PCM), channel count is 1, bits per sample is 16, and the sample data byte count is even. Failure on any condition reports an "invalid serialized audio data" error.
+
+Integer-to-float conversion divides each little-endian signed 16-bit integer sample by 32,767 to produce a float in approximately the range [-1.0, 1.0].
+
+---
+
+## 13. Sound Effects
+
+### 13.1 Sound Inventory
+
+The system loads two sounds at initialization:
+- A start-recording sound (system sound "Tink").
+- A stop-recording sound (system sound "Pop").
+
+If a named system sound does not exist on the platform, the reference is absent and playback silently does nothing.
+
+### 13.2 Enable/Disable Gate
+
+Every playback request must first check an enable/disable gate. If the gate returns "disabled", the request is silently ignored. The gate defaults to "enabled" if no custom gate is provided.
+
+### 13.3 Playback Behavior
+
+When playing a sound effect, any currently playing instance of that sound must be stopped before starting playback. This allows rapid re-triggering without waiting for the previous playback to complete.
+
+### 13.4 Testing Support
+
+The system accepts optional custom implementations for the enable/disable gate, the start-recording player, and the stop-recording player. These injection points enable testing without actual audio playback.
+
+---
+
+## 14. Overlay Messages
+
+### 14.1 Message Types
+
+The overlay supports seven message types during pipeline operation:
+
+| Message | Primary Text | Secondary Text | Indicator | Size | Auto-Dismiss |
+|---------|-------------|---------------|-----------|------|--------------|
+| Recording | "Recording..." | none | Pulsing red dot | 300x60 | No |
+| Model Loading | "Loading models..." | none | Spinner (orange) | 300x60 | No |
+| Transcribing | "Transcribing..." | none | Pulsing blue dot | 300x60 | No |
+| Cleaning Up | "Cleaning up..." | none | Pulsing blue dot | 300x60 | No |
+| No Sound Detected | "No sound detected" | none | Pulsing orange dot | 300x60 | 3 seconds |
+| Clipboard Fallback | "Copied to clipboard" | "Cmd+V to paste" | Pulsing blue dot | 420x84 | 3 seconds |
+| Learned Correction | "Learned correction" | "<wrong> -> <right>" | Green checkmark | 420x84 | 3 seconds |
+
+### 14.2 Overlay Transitions During a Successful Session
+
+1. "Recording..." is shown when recording starts.
+2. "Transcribing..." replaces it when recording stops and transcription begins.
+3. "Cleaning up..." replaces it when text refinement begins (conditional on refinement availability).
+4. The overlay is dismissed after successful text delivery.
+
+### 14.3 Conditional Overlay Transitions
+
+- If text refinement is unavailable, the overlay transitions directly from "Transcribing..." to dismissed.
+- If the delivery mechanism falls back to clipboard (no editable paste target found), the "Clipboard Fallback" message is shown. The pipeline also schedules its own 2.5-second dismiss timer in addition to the overlay's built-in 3-second auto-dismiss.
+- If no transcript is produced, the overlay is either dismissed immediately (short recording cancelled) or replaced with "No Sound Detected" (long recording with no speech).
+
+### 14.4 Message Replacement
+
+- Showing a new message must replace the currently displayed message. Any pending auto-dismiss timer is cancelled, the content updates, the panel resizes if the new message requires a different size, and a new auto-dismiss timer is scheduled if the new message type requires one.
+- The conditional dismiss operation must only dismiss the overlay if the currently displayed message matches the specified message. If a different message is showing, the dismiss request must be ignored.
+
+---
+
+## 15. Push-to-Talk Upgrade to Toggle
+
+When the user holds the push-to-talk key chord and then adds the additional key needed for the toggle-to-talk chord, the recording mode upgrades:
+
+- The system must trigger a recording restart when a push-to-talk chord is upgraded to a toggle-to-talk chord mid-recording.
+- On restart, if fewer than 16,000 samples (approximately 1 second at 16 kHz) have been accumulated, the audio buffer is cleared and the discarded sample count is logged. If 16,000 or more samples have accumulated, the buffer is preserved and the log notes that existing audio is being kept.
+
+---
+
+## 16. Error Handling
+
+### 16.1 Recording Start Failure
+
+If the audio engine fails to start (no input device available, format converter creation failed, or the engine throws an error), the system must: cancel the screen capture prefetch, release pipeline ownership, abandon the performance trace, set an error message describing the failure, and transition the status to "Error".
+
+### 16.2 Transcription Failure
+
+If speech-to-text conversion returns no result (transcription error or empty audio), this is not treated as an application error. The pipeline continues through the empty transcription disposition path (see section 6).
+
+### 16.3 Refinement Failure
+
+- If the refinement model is unavailable, the refinement stage must fall back to deterministic corrections only (preferred transcription normalization and commonly misheard replacements).
+- If the refinement model produces unusable output (empty result, only internal reasoning content, or an error), the system must fall back to deterministic corrections.
+- Refinement failures must not prevent text delivery. The pipeline must always proceed to delivery with whatever text is available (original transcription with deterministic corrections as the worst case).
+
+### 16.4 Pipeline Busy
+
+When the pipeline is busy (owned by another session), a recording attempt must be silently skipped with a debug log entry. No error message is shown to the user and no status change occurs.
+
+### 16.5 Archive Failure
+
+If archiving the recording to the experiment archive fails, the error must be logged but must not interrupt the pipeline or affect text delivery.
+
+### 16.6 Permission Failures
+
+- If microphone access is denied during initialization, the status must transition to "Error" with the message "Microphone access required". The pipeline must not be available for recording.
+- If the hotkey monitor fails to start (accessibility permission not granted), the status must transition to "Error" with a message prompting the user to grant accessibility access. The system must also trigger an accessibility permission prompt.
+
+### 16.7 Audio Capture Errors
+
+- A "no input available" error is reported when the input device reports a sample rate of zero or a channel count of zero during recording start. User-visible message: "No audio input device available."
+- A "converter creation failed" error is reported when the audio format converter cannot be created from the input format to the target format. User-visible message: "Failed to create audio format converter."
+- An "invalid serialized audio data" error is reported when audio deserialization encounters malformed data (wrong byte count for raw format, or any structural violation for WAV format).
+- Errors during real-time audio format conversion (within the microphone tap) must be logged but must not stop the recording. The affected buffer is silently dropped and recording continues with subsequent buffers.
+- Device management operations must not produce errors visible to the pipeline. List operations return an empty list on failure, default device queries return nothing on failure, and default device mutations return a success or failure indicator.
+
+---
+
+## 17. Event Wiring
+
+The pipeline connects subsystem events via notifications:
+
+| Event | Source | Pipeline Response |
+|-------|--------|------------------|
+| Recording started | Audio capture | Record microphone-live timestamp on performance trace |
+| Recording stopped | Audio capture | Record microphone-cold timestamp on performance trace |
+| Converted audio chunk | Audio capture | Feed samples to speaker filtering session (for diarization) |
+| Delivery started | Text delivery | Record delivery-start timestamp on performance trace |
+| Delivery completed | Text delivery | Record delivery-end timestamp, complete the performance trace |
+| Text delivered | Text delivery | Trigger post-delivery learning |
+| Learned correction | Post-delivery learning | Show learned-correction overlay message |
+| Push-to-talk start | Hotkey monitor | Create performance trace, start recording |
+| Push-to-talk stop | Hotkey monitor | Record hotkey-lifted timestamp, stop and transcribe |
+| Toggle-to-talk start | Hotkey monitor | Create performance trace, start recording |
+| Toggle-to-talk stop | Hotkey monitor | Record hotkey-lifted timestamp, stop and transcribe |
+| Recording restart | Hotkey monitor | Conditionally clear or keep audio buffer |
+
+All event registrations must prevent circular ownership between the event source and the pipeline, ensuring event registrations do not prevent the pipeline from being released.
+
+---
+
+## 18. Application Lifecycle
+
+### 18.1 Initialization
+
+On first appearance after onboarding, the system must: check microphone permission, check accessibility and input monitoring permissions (opening system settings if either is missing), pre-warm the audio engine, start paste target tracking, load the speech model (showing a model-loading overlay), start the hotkey monitor, and refresh the refinement model state.
+
+If onboarding has not been completed, the onboarding flow must be shown first. Initialization must proceed only after onboarding completes.
+
+### 18.2 Termination
+
+On application termination, the system must cancel the screen capture prefetch and shut down the refinement backend.
+
+### 18.3 Automatic Termination Prevention
+
+Automatic termination must be disabled at application startup to prevent the operating system from terminating the menu bar application when it appears idle. It must be re-enabled at termination to allow clean shutdown.
+
+---
+
+## 19. Numeric Constants Reference
+
+| Constant | Value | Purpose |
+|----------|-------|---------|
+| Flush delay | 200 ms | Wait for remaining audio data to drain before tearing down the audio engine after stop |
+| Tap buffer interval | ~100 ms at input sample rate | Size of each audio buffer delivered by the microphone tap |
+| Target sample rate | 16,000 Hz | Output sample rate for all captured audio |
+| Target channel count | 1 (mono) | Output channel layout |
+| Target bit depth | 32-bit float | Output sample type |
+| Short recording threshold | 80,000 samples (~5 seconds at 16 kHz) | Below this, empty transcriptions are silently cancelled; at or above, "No sound detected" is shown |
+| Buffer keep threshold | 16,000 samples (~1 second at 16 kHz) | On push-to-talk upgrade, buffers below this are discarded; at or above, they are preserved |
+| No sound detected dismiss | 3 seconds | Auto-dismiss timer for the "No sound detected" overlay |
+| Clipboard fallback dismiss (pipeline) | 2.5 seconds | Pipeline-scheduled dismiss timer for clipboard fallback overlay |
+| Clipboard fallback dismiss (overlay) | 3 seconds | Overlay's built-in auto-dismiss timer for clipboard fallback |
+| Model loading overlay | 1.5 seconds | Duration the "Loading models..." overlay is shown when recording is attempted during model load |
+| WAV header size | 44 bytes | Minimum valid WAV file size |
+| WAV bits per sample | 16 | Signed integer PCM for WAV serialization |
+| WAV max amplitude scale | 32,767 | Maximum 16-bit signed integer value used for float-to-int and int-to-float conversion |
+| WAV round-trip tolerance | 0.0001 | Maximum acceptable error after WAV serialize/deserialize round-trip |
+| Overlay size (standard) | 300x60 | Dimensions for Recording, Model Loading, Transcribing, Cleaning Up, No Sound Detected messages |
+| Overlay size (extended) | 420x84 | Dimensions for Clipboard Fallback and Learned Correction messages |

+# Input Domain
+
+This domain covers global keyboard monitoring, chord recognition, recording activation, keyboard shortcut configuration, and automated paste delivery of transcribed text.
+
+---
+
+## 1. Global Keyboard Monitoring
+
+### 1.1 Event Tap
+
+The application listens for keyboard events system-wide by installing a CGEvent tap at the session level. The tap is appended to the existing event chain in listen-only (passive) mode. It captures three categories of events: modifier flag changes, key-down events, and key-up events.
+
+The event tap requires macOS Accessibility permission. If the system refuses to create the tap, startup fails. Attempting to start monitoring when it is already active succeeds immediately with no side effects.
+
+The tap runs on a dedicated background thread with its own run loop. The caller blocks until the background thread is ready. The run loop is kept alive to prevent it from terminating when idle.
+
+### 1.2 Tap Auto-Recovery
+
+macOS may disable an event tap if it takes too long to process events. When this occurs, the tap callback detects the disabled state and immediately re-enables it.
+
+### 1.3 Event Snapshots
+
+Each raw keyboard event is converted into a snapshot containing the event type, the hardware key code, and the current modifier flags. Only modifier flag changes, key-down events, and key-up events produce snapshots; all other event types are discarded.
+
+Snapshots are forwarded from the tap thread to a serial processing queue, decoupling tap callback execution from chord evaluation.
+
+### 1.4 Non-Modifier Event Gating
+
+To minimize processing overhead during normal typing, non-modifier key events (key-down and key-up) are filtered before reaching chord evaluation. A non-modifier event passes the filter only when the current modifier flags match the modifier portion of at least one configured chord that includes non-modifier keys.
+
+If every configured chord consists entirely of modifier keys, all non-modifier key events are dropped with zero processing cost.
+
+### 1.5 Modifier State Derivation
+
+When a modifier flag change event arrives, the set of currently held modifiers is derived by inspecting the event's flag bitmask rather than tracking individual press/release toggles. Each recognized modifier key has a known bitmask combining both the device-specific and generic modifier flags. A modifier counts as pressed when its combined bitmask is present in the current flags.
+
+Recognized modifier key codes and their identities:
+
+| Key Code | Key |
+|----------|-----|
+| 54 | Right Command |
+| 55 | Left Command |
+| 56 | Left Shift |
+| 57 | Caps Lock |
+| 58 | Left Option |
+| 59 | Left Control |
+| 60 | Right Shift |
+| 61 | Right Option |
+| 62 | Right Control |
+| 63 | Fn / Globe |
+
+### 1.6 Pressed Key Computation
+
+For each incoming event, an authoritative set of currently pressed keys is computed by combining tracked non-modifier keys with modifiers derived from the event's flag bitmask:
+
+- **Modifier flag change**: union of tracked non-modifier keys and modifiers from the new flags.
+- **Key-down**: union of tracked non-modifier keys, modifiers from flags, and the newly pressed key.
+- **Key-up**: tracked non-modifier keys minus the released key, unioned with modifiers from flags.
+
+### 1.7 Polling-Based Recovery
+
+When chord evaluation produces no effects, the system polls the physical state of all monitored keys using CGEvent source key state queries. If polling reveals pressed keys that are not currently tracked, the chord engine re-synchronizes with the union of tracked and polled keys.
+
+This recovery only adds keys; it never removes keys based on polling alone, avoiding false releases from noisy partial reads. It also requires that the current event's key agrees between event-based and polled state before applying corrections, preventing stale poll data from overriding a more recent event.
+
+### 1.8 Post-Stop Reset
+
+When a stop effect fires and no physical keys remain pressed, all chord engine state is fully cleared. This prevents stale key tracking from persisting after the user has released everything.
+
+---
+
+## 2. Chord Recognition
+
+### 2.1 Engine State
+
+The chord engine tracks two pieces of state:
+
+- **Pressed keys**: the set of physical keys currently considered held.
+- **Active recording mode**: one of push-to-talk, toggle-to-talk, or idle.
+
+### 2.2 Event Processing
+
+The engine accepts three event types:
+
+- **Modifier flag change**: toggles the specified key in the pressed set (adds if absent, removes if present).
+- **Key-down**: adds the key to the pressed set. Ignored if already present.
+- **Key-up**: removes the key from the pressed set. Ignored if not present.
+
+An event that does not change the pressed key set produces no effects.
+
+### 2.3 Matching
+
+After each pressed-set update, the engine checks the current pressed keys against all configured chord bindings:
+
+- **Exact match**: the pressed keys are identical to a binding's key set. Takes priority over prefix matches.
+- **Prefix match**: the pressed keys are a proper subset of some binding's key set, meaning the user may still be building toward a longer chord.
+- **No match**: neither condition holds.
+
+An empty pressed set always evaluates to no match.
+
+### 2.4 Transitions
+
+**From idle:**
+
+| Match Result | Effect | New State |
+|-------------|--------|-----------|
+| Exact match for any action | Start recording | Active = matched action |
+| Prefix match | None | Idle |
+| No match | None | Idle |
+
+**From push-to-talk active:**
+
+| Condition | Effect | New State |
+|-----------|--------|-----------|
+| Exact match for toggle-to-talk | Restart recording | Active = toggle-to-talk |
+| Push chord keys no longer all held | Stop recording | Idle |
+| Push chord keys still held, no toggle match | None | Push-to-talk active |
+
+**From toggle-to-talk active:**
+
+| Condition | Effect | New State |
+|-----------|--------|-----------|
+| Exact match for toggle-to-talk | Stop recording | Idle |
+| Any other event | None | Toggle-to-talk active |
+
+### 2.5 External Synchronization
+
+The pressed key set can be replaced wholesale from an external authoritative source (used by polling-based recovery). If the replacement differs from the current set, transitions are re-evaluated.
+
+### 2.6 Reset
+
+The engine can be fully reset, clearing both the pressed key set and the active recording mode. This happens when monitoring stops, when bindings change, and when suspension state changes.
+
+---
+
+## 3. Push-to-Talk
+
+Recording begins when the user presses and holds every key in the push-to-talk chord simultaneously. Recording continues as long as all chord keys remain held. Releasing any single chord key immediately stops recording.
+
+The user may press additional keys beyond the chord without affecting the recording; only releasing a chord key terminates it.
+
+---
+
+## 4. Toggle-to-Talk
+
+Recording begins when the user presses the complete toggle-to-talk chord. Once active, the user may freely release keys and type normally without affecting the recording. Recording stops only when the exact toggle chord is pressed a second time.
+
+Between the start and stop activations, all key events that do not produce an exact toggle chord match are ignored.
+
+---
+
+## 5. Push-to-Toggle Upgrade
+
+While push-to-talk is active, if the user presses additional keys to complete the toggle-to-talk chord, the recording mode upgrades from push-to-talk to toggle-to-talk. This produces a restart effect: the current recording buffer is discarded and a fresh recording begins in toggle mode.
+
+This enables a workflow where the default push-to-talk chord (Right Command + Right Option) is a subset of the toggle-to-talk chord (Right Command + Right Option + Space). The user holds the modifier keys for push-to-talk, then taps Space to upgrade to toggle mode mid-recording.
+
+---
+
+## 6. Recording Callbacks
+
+When the chord engine produces effects, they are dispatched as callbacks:
+
+- **Start**: dispatched to the action-specific start handler (push-to-talk start or toggle-to-talk start) when available, otherwise to a generic recording start handler.
+- **Stop**: dispatched to the action-specific stop handler, otherwise to a generic recording stop handler.
+- **Restart**: always dispatched to the recording restart handler.
+
+When a single event produces multiple effects, they are dispatched in order: start, then restart, then stop. A single event produces at most one of each.
+
+All callbacks are invoked outside any lock, after state evaluation completes. This prevents deadlocks if a callback re-enters the monitoring system.
+
+---
+
+## 7. Shortcut Configuration
+
+### 7.1 Persistence
+
+Chord-to-action bindings are persisted in the macOS user defaults system. Each action's binding is stored under a key formed by the prefix "chordBinding." followed by the action name (e.g., "chordBinding.pushToTalk", "chordBinding.toggleToTalk"). Bindings are serialized as JSON.
+
+### 7.2 Duplicate Prevention
+
+When saving a new binding, the system checks all other actions for a chord conflict. If another action already uses the same chord, the save is rejected with a duplicate error. This prevents two actions from sharing one chord.
+
+### 7.3 Removal
+
+Clearing a binding for an action removes its entry from user defaults entirely.
+
+### 7.4 Read Behavior
+
+Reading a binding that does not exist or that contains corrupted data returns nothing. Decode failures are silently ignored.
+
+### 7.5 Empty Chord Protection
+
+A chord must contain at least one key. Attempting to create a chord with no keys fails. Deserializing a chord with an empty key set raises a decoding error. This guards against corrupted stored bindings.
+
+### 7.6 Default Bindings
+
+| Action | Chord | Key Codes |
+|--------|-------|-----------|
+| Push-to-talk | Right Command + Right Option | 54, 61 |
+| Toggle-to-talk | Right Command + Right Option + Space | 54, 61, 49 |
+
+### 7.7 Update Flow
+
+When the user changes a shortcut, the system attempts to persist the new binding. If the save is rejected due to a duplicate conflict, the change is rolled back to the previous values and an error is displayed.
+
+After a successful binding update, the monitoring system receives the new binding map and rebuilds its tracked key set and chord engine.
+
+---
+
+## 8. Physical Key Catalog
+
+### 8.1 Key Representation
+
+Each physical key is identified by a 16-bit unsigned integer hardware key code (the macOS virtual key code). Keys support equality comparison, hashing, and JSON serialization.
+
+### 8.2 Display Names
+
+| Key Code | Display Name |
+|----------|-------------|
+| 36 | Return |
+| 48 | Tab |
+| 49 | Space |
+| 51 | Delete |
+| 53 | Escape |
+| 54 | Right Command |
+| 55 | Left Command |
+| 56 | Left Shift |
+| 57 | Caps Lock |
+| 58 | Left Option |
+| 59 | Left Control |
+| 60 | Right Shift |
+| 61 | Right Option |
+| 62 | Right Control |
+| 63 | Fn / Globe |
+
+Keys without a recognized code display as "Key Code N" where N is the numeric value.
+
+### 8.3 Display Sort Order
+
+When keys appear as part of a chord display, they are sorted in canonical order: Command keys first, then Option, Control, Shift, Fn, and all other keys last. Ties within a category are broken alphabetically by display name, then by key code.
+
+### 8.4 Compact Symbol Display
+
+For the shortcut recorder interface, modifier keys use compact symbols with left/right suffixes (e.g., a command symbol with a right-arrow superscript for Right Command). Non-modifier keys use their standard display name.
+
+---
+
+## 9. Shortcut Capture
+
+### 9.1 Capture Tracking
+
+During a shortcut capture session, two key sets are maintained:
+
+- **Currently pressed**: keys physically held at this moment.
+- **Captured**: all keys pressed at any point during the session (the high-water mark).
+
+### 9.2 Event Handling
+
+- Modifier flag changes toggle the key in the pressed set. On press, the key is also added to the captured set.
+- Key-down events add the key to both pressed and captured sets.
+- Key-up events remove the key from the pressed set only.
+
+### 9.3 Completion
+
+When all keys are released (the pressed set becomes empty), capture completes. A chord is constructed from all captured keys, both sets are cleared, and the chord is returned. If any key remains held, the session continues.
+
+### 9.4 Monitor Suspension During Capture
+
+When shortcut capture begins, chord monitoring is suspended. While suspended, keyboard events are logged but produce no chord effects. The chord engine is reset on suspension to prevent stale state.
+
+### 9.5 Post-Capture Key Release Guard
+
+When monitoring resumes after capture, if keys are still physically held, chord matching remains disabled until either:
+
+- All physical keys are released, or
+- A fresh single-key press occurs (a modifier change or key-down where the only pressed key is the new one).
+
+This prevents the just-captured chord from immediately triggering a recording action.
+
+---
+
+## 10. Monitor Lifecycle
+
+### 10.1 Suspension
+
+Monitoring can be externally suspended and resumed. Suspension is used during shortcut capture.
+
+### 10.2 Teardown
+
+Stopping the monitor removes the event tap, halts the background thread's run loop, and resets all state including the chord engine, suspension flag, and key release guard.
+
+### 10.3 Idempotent Start
+
+Starting the monitor when it is already active returns success immediately without reinstalling.
+
+---
+
+## 11. Thread Safety
+
+All mutable monitoring state is protected by a single lock. The lock is held during state evaluation, start, stop, binding updates, and suspension changes. Callbacks are dispatched outside the lock to avoid deadlocks.
+
+Events from the tap callback are forwarded to a serial processing queue, keeping chord evaluation off the tap thread.
+
+The chord engine and shortcut capture state have no internal synchronization; their thread safety comes from the monitor's lock.
+
+---
+
+## 12. Clipboard Preservation
+
+### 12.1 Saving
+
+Before pasting, the system saves the full contents of the macOS general pasteboard (NSPasteboard). The save iterates every pasteboard item and reads every type representation as raw data, preserving rich content such as images, formatted text, and file references.
+
+If the pasteboard is empty, no save is recorded and no restoration will occur.
+
+### 12.2 Restoring
+
+After the paste completes, the saved pasteboard state is restored. The pasteboard is cleared, all items are reconstructed with their original type representations, and they are written in a single batch.
+
+This save/restore cycle makes the paste operation transparent to the user's clipboard contents. The transcribed text occupies the pasteboard only during the brief paste window.
+
+---
+
+## 13. Simulated Paste
+
+### 13.1 Paste Flow
+
+The paste operation proceeds through these steps:
+
+1. Signal paste start via callback.
+2. Save the current pasteboard state.
+3. Clear the pasteboard and write the transcribed text as plain text.
+4. Check whether the focused element can accept a paste.
+5. Prepare the simulated keystroke.
+6. If either check or preparation fails, signal paste end and report that text was left on the clipboard (fallback path).
+7. Wait 50 milliseconds (pre-keystroke delay).
+8. Simulate a Command+V keystroke.
+9. Wait 100 milliseconds (post-keystroke delay).
+10. Capture a paste session snapshot.
+11. Invoke the paste session callback.
+12. Restore the saved pasteboard state.
+13. Signal paste end.
+
+### 13.2 Keystroke Simulation
+
+The simulated paste keystroke is built from a CGEvent source of HID system state type. Both a key-down and key-up event are created using virtual key code 0x09 (the V key) with the Command modifier flag set. These events are posted to the HID event tap location.
+
+If CGEvent creation fails (which can happen without Accessibility permission), the paste falls back to the clipboard fallback path.
+
+### 13.3 Timing Constants
+
+| Constant | Value | Purpose |
+|----------|-------|---------|
+| Pre-keystroke delay | 50 ms | Allows the system to register new pasteboard contents before the simulated keystroke |
+| Post-keystroke delay | 100 ms | Allows the target application to read the pasteboard during paste handling before contents are restored |
+
+---
+
+## 14. Paste Target Detection
+
+### 14.1 Strategy
+
+Before simulating a paste, the system determines whether the focused UI element can accept text. This uses the macOS Accessibility API (AXUIElement) to inspect the frontmost application's focused element.
+
+Detection has three layers:
+
+1. **Direct query**: Ask the frontmost application for its focused UI element via the Accessibility API and evaluate it directly.
+2. **Cached observation fallback**: If the direct query yields nothing, use a cached result from the continuous paste target monitor.
+3. **Recursive tree walk**: When evaluating a candidate element, descend into its accessibility children (up to 12 levels deep) searching for a suitable paste target, checking focused children first.
+
+### 14.2 Priority Logic
+
+1. If a direct query returns a focused element that is a paste target, accept it immediately.
+2. If a direct query returns a focused element that is NOT a paste target, reject immediately. The direct query is trusted over cached data.
+3. If no direct focused element is available, accept the cached observation only if its process ID and window ID match the current frontmost application and the cached status is editable.
+
+### 14.3 Element Evaluation Heuristics
+
+An element is evaluated as a potential paste target using these rules:
+
+**Disqualification**: Disabled elements are never paste targets.
+
+**Without focus context** (no ancestor in the current walk has been identified as focused):
+- The element must have both a selected text range attribute and a settable value attribute.
+
+**With focus context** (the element or an ancestor is focused):
+- Editable attribute is true: accepted.
+- Value attribute is settable: accepted.
+- Has a text entry role (text field, text area, or combo box) AND has a selected text range: accepted.
+- Has a text entry role alone: accepted.
+
+### 14.4 Text Entry Roles
+
+Three Accessibility roles qualify as text entry roles: text field, text area, and combo box.
+
+### 14.5 Recursive Tree Walk
+
+The accessibility tree is walked depth-first to a maximum depth of 12 levels. At each level, focused children are checked before non-focused children. Focus context propagates downward: once any ancestor is identified as focused, all descendants are evaluated with the more relaxed focused heuristics.
+
+---
+
+## 15. Continuous Paste Target Monitoring
+
+### 15.1 Purpose
+
+A background monitor continuously tracks whether the frontmost application's focused element is an editable paste target. This cached observation serves as a fallback when the direct Accessibility API query returns no focused element.
+
+### 15.2 Application Activation Tracking
+
+The monitor observes NSWorkspace application activation notifications. When a different application becomes frontmost, the monitor tears down the previous accessibility observer and installs a new one for the newly active application.
+
+### 15.3 Accessibility Observer
+
+For each tracked application, the monitor creates an AXObserver watching for two notification types: focused UI element changed and focused window changed. The observer is attached to the main run loop. On installation, the monitor enables enhanced accessibility on the application element to ensure accessibility data is available.
+
+### 15.4 Observation Recording
+
+When a focus change notification fires, the monitor evaluates the notified element. If the element is a paste target, the status is recorded as editable. If the notification indicates a focus or window change but the element is not a paste target, the status is recorded as non-editable. Other notification types are ignored.
+
+Each observation records the process ID and, when available, the window ID. This allows the paste decision logic to verify the cached observation applies to the current frontmost window.
+
+---
+
+## 16. Clipboard Fallback
+
+When paste target detection determines that no suitable text input is focused, or when keystroke simulation cannot be prepared, the system does not simulate a keystroke. Instead:
+
+1. The transcribed text remains on the system pasteboard (it was written there in step 3 of the paste flow).
+2. The original pasteboard contents are not restored.
+3. The system reports a "copied to clipboard" result.
+4. An overlay notification informs the user that text has been copied for manual pasting.
+
+---
+
+## 17. Paste Session Capture
+
+### 17.1 Session Data
+
+After a successful paste (the keystroke was simulated), the system captures a snapshot of the paste context containing:
+
+- The pasted text.
+- The timestamp of the paste.
+- The frontmost application's bundle identifier.
+- The frontmost window's identifier and frame.
+- The focused element's frame and text content.
+
+### 17.2 Capture Timing
+
+The snapshot is captured during the post-keystroke delay, 100 milliseconds after the simulated keystroke. This timing allows the target application to process the paste before the system reads back the element state.
+
+### 17.3 Downstream Use
+
+The paste session snapshot is delivered to the post-paste learning system, which compares the focused element's text content against the pasted text to detect corrections made by the target application. This enables automatic learning of commonly misheard words.
+
+---
+
+## 18. Paste Lifecycle Callbacks
+
+Three callbacks mark the paste lifecycle:
+
+- **Paste start**: fired at the beginning of every paste attempt, before any pasteboard manipulation.
+- **Paste end**: fired when the paste flow completes, whether via the normal path (after pasteboard restoration) or the fallback path (after determining no paste target exists).
+- **Paste session**: fired after a successful simulated paste, carrying the session snapshot.
+
+These callbacks are used to stamp performance trace milestones for paste timing measurement.
+
+---
+
+## 19. Chord Data Model
+
+A chord must contain at least one key. Constructing a chord with an empty key set fails. Deserializing an empty key set raises a decoding error.
+
+Chords are displayed as key names joined by " + " separators, sorted in canonical display order (Command, Option, Control, Shift, Fn, then others).
+
+A compact symbolic display format is available for the shortcut recorder interface.
+
+---
+
+## 20. Recording Actions
+
+Two recording actions exist:
+
+- **Push-to-talk**: recording is active only while the chord keys are held.
+- **Toggle-to-talk**: recording starts on first chord activation and stops on second chord activation.
+
+These are the only two action types.
+
+---
+
+## 21. Debug Logging
+
+The monitoring system emits structured debug log entries in the "hotkey" category covering:
+
+- Binding updates with the new binding descriptions.
+- Suspension state changes.
+- Monitor start, already-started skip, start failure, and stop events.
+- Each processed keyboard event with event type, pressed keys, active action, and effects.
+- Key release guard state transitions.
+- Event tap disable and re-enable events.
+- Suppressed events during suspension.
