+# Speech Domain
+
+This specification describes the behaviors of the speech recognition domain: model management, transcription, speaker diarization, and speaker-filtered transcription.
+
+---
+
+## 1. Speech Model Catalog
+
+### 1.1 Available Models
+
+The system offers exactly four speech recognition models.
+
+| User-Facing Title | Variant Label | Approximate Size | Backend |
+|---|---|---|---|
+| Speed | tiny.en | ~75 MB | Whisper |
+| Accuracy | small.en | ~466 MB | Whisper |
+| Multilingual | small | ~466 MB | Whisper |
+| Parakeet v3 | 25 languages | ~1.4 GB | FluidAudio |
+
+### 1.2 Default Model
+
+When no user preference has been stored, the system defaults to the "Accuracy" model (the Whisper small.en variant).
+
+### 1.3 Backends
+
+The system supports two distinct speech engine backends:
+
+- **Whisper**: Powers the Speed, Accuracy, and Multilingual models. These models vary in size and speed but share a common transcription interface provided by the WhisperKit framework.
+- **FluidAudio**: Powers the Parakeet v3 model. This backend provides speaker diarization capabilities in addition to transcription.
+
+### 1.4 Speaker Filtering Support
+
+Only models using the FluidAudio backend support speaker filtering. All Whisper-backend models do not support speaker filtering.
+
+### 1.5 Model Attributes
+
+Each model entry carries the following attributes:
+
+- A unique identifier used as the lookup and persistence key
+- A user-facing title shown in the model picker (e.g., "Speed", "Accuracy")
+- A technical variant label (e.g., "tiny.en", "25 languages")
+- A human-readable size description (e.g., "~466 MB")
+- A backend type indicator (Whisper or FluidAudio)
+- Information needed to locate the model in the on-disk cache
+- For FluidAudio models only, a variant identifier consumed by the FluidAudio SDK
+
+### 1.6 Model Lookup
+
+The system provides a lookup mechanism that finds a model by its unique identifier. When no model matches the given identifier, the lookup returns nothing.
+
+---
+
+## 2. Model Lifecycle
+
+### 2.1 State Machine
+
+The model management subsystem follows a four-state machine:
+
+```
+States: idle, loading, ready, error
+
+Transitions:
+  idle    --[load requested]--> loading
+  error   --[load requested]--> loading
+  loading --[load succeeds]---> ready
+  loading --[load fails, retryable]--> loading (retry once after 500ms)
+  loading --[retry fails / non-retryable error]--> error
+  ready   --[same model requested]--> ready (no-op)
+  ready   --[different model requested]--> idle (reset) --> loading
+```
+
+### 2.2 Initial State
+
+At creation, the model subsystem is in the idle state with no model loaded, no error present, and readiness reported as false.
+
+### 2.3 Loading Sequence
+
+When a model load is requested:
+
+1. If no specific model is named, the system uses the currently configured model.
+2. If the requested identifier does not match any catalog entry, the system transitions to the error state with a "not found" error and does not attempt a download.
+3. If the system is already in the ready state with the same model, the request is a no-op.
+4. If the system is already in the ready state with a different model, the system clears all loaded engine instances and resets to idle before loading the new model.
+5. If the system is currently in the loading state, the request is silently ignored to prevent concurrent loads.
+6. The system transitions to the loading state, clears any prior error, and logs the attempt.
+7. On success, the system transitions to the ready state.
+8. On failure, the system checks retry eligibility (see section 2.6). If not retryable, the system transitions to the error state and records the error.
+
+### 2.4 Whisper Model Download and Initialization
+
+For Whisper-backend models:
+
+1. The system ensures a whisper models directory exists under the application support folder at the path "GhostPepper/whisper-models/".
+2. If the model is not already cached on disk, the system downloads it via the WhisperKit framework. Download progress is reported as a fraction from 0.0 to 1.0, and the progress indicator is cleared once download completes.
+3. After download (or cache hit), the system initializes the WhisperKit engine with verbose output disabled, error-only logging, pre-warming disabled, and loading enabled.
+
+### 2.5 FluidAudio Model Download and Initialization
+
+For FluidAudio-backend models:
+
+1. The model entry must carry a FluidAudio variant identifier. If this identifier is absent, the system fails with a configuration error.
+2. The system downloads and loads the speech recognition models via the FluidAudio SDK, reporting progress during download.
+3. After download, the system creates a FluidAudio speech recognition manager with default configuration and initializes it with the loaded models.
+
+### 2.6 Retry on Timeout
+
+When a model load fails, the system inspects the error to decide whether a retry is warranted:
+
+- An error from the URL loading subsystem with a "timed out" code is retryable.
+- Any error whose human-readable description contains "timed out" (case-insensitive) is retryable.
+- The system recursively inspects wrapped underlying errors for these conditions.
+
+When a retryable error is detected:
+
+1. All loaded engine instances are cleared.
+2. The system waits 500 milliseconds before retrying.
+3. Exactly one retry is attempted. If the retry also fails, the system transitions to the error state normally.
+
+### 2.7 Cache Checking
+
+The system can determine whether a model's files are already present on disk without downloading:
+
+- For Whisper models, the system checks for the existence of the expected directory under the whisper models root.
+- For FluidAudio models, the system delegates the cache check to the FluidAudio framework's own cache directory mechanism.
+
+The system also provides a set of all currently cached model identifiers.
+
+### 2.8 Resetting Loaded Models
+
+When switching to a different model, the system clears all three possible loaded engine instances (Whisper engine, FluidAudio manager, and diarization models) and resets download progress.
+
+---
+
+## 3. Transcription
+
+### 3.1 Dispatching Transcription
+
+When transcription is requested with an audio buffer:
+
+1. If the audio buffer is empty, the system returns no result immediately without invoking any backend.
+2. The system looks up the currently configured model in the catalog. If not found, it returns no result.
+3. The system dispatches to the appropriate backend based on the model's backend type.
+
+### 3.2 Whisper Transcription
+
+When transcribing through the Whisper backend:
+
+1. A loaded WhisperKit engine is required. If none is loaded, the system returns no result.
+2. The engine performs transcription on the raw audio sample array.
+3. All result segments are joined with spaces, leading and trailing whitespace is trimmed, and then artifact removal is applied (see section 5).
+4. If the cleaned result is empty, the system returns no result.
+
+### 3.3 FluidAudio Transcription
+
+When transcribing through the FluidAudio backend:
+
+1. A loaded FluidAudio manager is required. If none is loaded, the system returns no result.
+2. The manager performs transcription on the audio buffer with a microphone source indicator.
+3. Leading and trailing whitespace is trimmed from the result.
+4. If the trimmed result is empty, the system returns no result.
+5. Artifact removal is NOT applied to FluidAudio transcription results.
+
+### 3.4 Transcription Error Handling
+
+If a backend throws an error during transcription, the system catches the error, logs it, and returns no result. Transcription failures are never retried.
+
+### 3.5 Serialized Access
+
+Concurrent transcription requests are serialized so that only one transcription executes at a time, enforced through a serialization mechanism.
+
+The serialization mechanism:
+
+1. Rejects empty audio buffers before entering the serialization gate, returning no result immediately.
+2. Dispatches work onto a serial processing queue.
+3. Acquires exclusive access before invoking the backend.
+4. Releases exclusive access after the backend call completes, ensuring release regardless of success or failure.
+5. Returns the asynchronous result to the caller via a completion callback.
+
+### 3.6 Readiness
+
+The transcription subsystem reports readiness by delegating to the model management subsystem's ready state. Before any model has been loaded, readiness is reported as false.
+
+---
+
+## 4. Speaker Diarization and Target Speaker Selection
+
+### 4.1 Creating a Recording Session Coordinator
+
+A recording session coordinator is created only when all three conditions are met:
+
+1. The currently configured model exists in the catalog.
+2. The model supports speaker filtering (FluidAudio backend only).
+3. The FluidAudio engine is loaded.
+
+If any condition is unmet, no coordinator is produced.
+
+### 4.2 Diarization Model Loading
+
+When creating a recording session coordinator:
+
+1. Diarization models are downloaded from a remote repository. These models are lazily cached in memory: once loaded, subsequent coordinator creations reuse the cached models without re-downloading.
+2. If diarization model loading fails, the system logs the error and returns no coordinator, degrading gracefully to full-buffer transcription.
+
+### 4.3 Coordinator Wiring
+
+When a coordinator is successfully created, it is configured so that:
+
+1. Each audio chunk is simultaneously accumulated in the speech session and fed to the diarizer for real-time speaker analysis.
+2. On finish, the diarizer's timeline is finalized and its segments are converted to diarization spans. These spans are then passed to the speech session's finalization pipeline.
+3. After finalization, diarizer resources are cleaned up.
+
+### 4.4 Per-Chunk Diarization Error Handling
+
+If the diarizer throws an error while processing an individual audio chunk, the system catches and logs the error, then continues processing subsequent chunks. Individual chunk failures do not abort overall diarization.
+
+### 4.5 Diarization Span Conversion
+
+Raw diarizer output (segments grouped by speaker index) is converted to flat diarization spans with:
+
+1. Speaker identifiers synthesized as "Speaker 0", "Speaker 1", etc. (zero-indexed).
+2. The resulting spans sorted by start time, with ties broken by end time.
+
+### 4.6 Dual-Path Finalization
+
+The recording session coordinator supports two finalization paths:
+
+1. **Self-contained finalization**: When a diarizer is wired in, calling finish with no arguments finalizes the diarizer internally and passes its spans to the speech session.
+2. **Externally-supplied spans**: When no diarizer is wired in (session-only mode), calling finish with explicit spans passes them directly to the speech session.
+
+If the self-contained finalization handler is absent, the finish call returns a fallback summary indicating no usable speaker spans.
+
+The coordinator stores the filtered transcript (if any) after finalization for later retrieval.
+
+### 4.7 Pipeline Integration: Speaker Filtering Decision
+
+At recording stop time, the pipeline decides whether to use speaker-filtered results:
+
+1. If a recording session coordinator exists, its finish method is called to finalize diarization.
+2. If finalization succeeded (no fallback used) and a non-empty filtered transcript was produced, that filtered transcript is used as the raw transcription.
+3. If finalization fell back or produced no filtered transcript, the system falls through to full-buffer transcription via the serialized transcriber.
+4. Regardless of whether speaker filtering produced a usable result, the diarization summary is preserved for archival.
+
+---
+
+## 5. Artifact Removal
+
+### 5.1 Hallucination Marker Set
+
+The system recognizes and removes exactly seven hallucination artifact strings from Whisper backend output:
+
+1. `[BLANK_AUDIO]`
+2. `[NO_SPEECH]`
+3. `(blank audio)`
+4. `(no speech)`
+5. `[MUSIC]`
+6. `[APPLAUSE]`
+7. `[LAUGHTER]`
+
+### 5.2 Removal Process
+
+Each occurrence of each artifact string is replaced with an empty string. All leading and trailing whitespace and newlines are then trimmed from the result.
+
+### 5.3 Scope
+
+Artifact removal is applied only to Whisper backend transcription results. It is never applied to FluidAudio backend results.
+
+---
+
+## 6. Speaker-Filtered Transcription Pipeline
+
+### 6.1 Audio Accumulation
+
+During recording, audio samples are accumulated in an internal buffer. Access to this buffer is serialized via a dedicated background thread for thread safety.
+
+- Empty sample arrays are silently ignored.
+- Both writes (appending chunks) and reads (extracting audio for transcription) synchronize through the same mechanism.
+
+### 6.2 Finalization
+
+The finalization method accepts an array of diarization spans and returns a result containing an optional filtered transcript and a diarization summary.
+
+### 6.3 Finalization Steps
+
+The finalization pipeline proceeds through these steps in order. Each step that fails its guard returns a fallback result with a specific reason.
+
+**Step 1 -- Sort spans**: All input spans are sorted by start time, with ties broken by end time.
+
+**Step 2 -- Empty span check**: If no spans are present, return a fallback with reason "no usable speaker spans".
+
+**Step 3 -- Target speaker selection**: Select a target speaker (see section 6.4). If no speaker qualifies, return a fallback with reason "no speaker reached threshold".
+
+**Step 4 -- Mark kept spans**: All spans matching the target speaker are marked as kept. Non-target spans remain unmarked.
+
+**Step 5 -- Duration calculation**: Calculate both the target speaker's total duration (sum of kept span durations) and the merged kept audio duration.
+
+**Step 6 -- Minimum audio check**: If the merged kept audio duration is below 0.75 seconds (the minimum kept audio threshold), return a fallback with reason "insufficient kept audio".
+
+**Step 7 -- Audio extraction**: Extract audio samples corresponding to the merged kept spans (see section 6.6). If extraction fails or produces an empty result, return a fallback with reason "filtered audio extraction failed".
+
+**Step 8 -- Transcription**: Transcribe the extracted audio. If transcription returns nothing or only whitespace, return a fallback with reason "empty filtered transcription".
+
+**Step 9 -- Success**: If all steps pass, return the filtered transcript with fallback marked as not used.
+
+### 6.4 Target Speaker Selection Algorithm
+
+Target speaker selection works as follows:
+
+1. Accumulate total speaking duration per speaker across all spans.
+2. Iterate spans in chronological order (they are pre-sorted by start time).
+3. Return the first speaker encountered whose total accumulated duration meets or exceeds the substantial speaker threshold of 0.5 seconds.
+4. If no speaker qualifies, return nothing.
+
+The target speaker is the chronologically first-appearing speaker who has enough total speaking time. It is not simply the speaker with the most total time; chronological order of first appearance is the tiebreaker.
+
+### 6.5 Span Merging
+
+Kept spans are merged according to these rules:
+
+1. Only spans marked as kept are considered.
+2. Zero-duration spans are skipped.
+3. If the gap between the end of the previous merged span and the start of the current span is within the merge gap tolerance of 0.05 seconds, the spans are merged by extending the previous span's end time to the maximum of the two end times.
+4. Otherwise, a new merged span begins.
+
+### 6.6 Audio Extraction
+
+Audio extraction from merged spans:
+
+1. Reads the accumulated audio buffer under thread-safe synchronization.
+2. Returns nothing if the audio buffer is empty.
+3. For each span, converts start time to a sample index using floor rounding and end time using ceiling rounding, both based on the configured sample rate (default 16,000 Hz).
+4. Clamps indices to valid buffer bounds (start clamped to 0, end clamped to buffer length).
+5. Skips spans where the end index does not exceed the start index.
+6. Concatenates all extracted sample slices into a single output buffer.
+7. Returns nothing if the output buffer is empty.
+
+### 6.7 Configuration Parameters
+
+The speaker-filtered transcription pipeline accepts the following configuration at creation time:
+
+| Parameter | Default | Purpose |
+|---|---|---|
+| Sample rate | 16,000 Hz | Converts time intervals to sample indices |
+| Substantial speaker threshold | 0.5 seconds | Minimum total speaking time to qualify as target speaker |
+| Minimum kept audio duration | 0.75 seconds | Minimum merged kept audio to proceed with transcription |
+| Merge gap tolerance | 0.05 seconds | Maximum gap between spans before they are merged |
+| Transcription callback | (required) | Async function that transcribes a float audio buffer into text |
+
+---
+
+## 7. Diarization Summary
+
+### 7.1 Summary Contents
+
+A diarization summary captures the complete state of a speaker filtering attempt:
+
+- All diarization spans (with speaker attribution and kept/not-kept marking)
+- Merged kept spans (time ranges of audio that was retained)
+- The identified target speaker (if any)
+- The target speaker's total speaking duration
+- The total kept audio duration
+- Whether fallback to full transcription was used
+- The reason for fallback (if applicable)
+
+### 7.2 Span Properties
+
+Each diarization span carries:
+
+- A speaker identifier string
+- Start and end time intervals
+- A kept/not-kept flag (defaulting to not kept)
+- A computed duration equal to the greater of zero and (end minus start)
+
+### 7.3 Merged Span Properties
+
+Each merged span carries start and end time intervals with a computed duration using the same formula as regular spans.
+
+### 7.4 Fallback Reasons
+
+Exactly five fallback reasons are defined:
+
+1. **No usable speaker spans** -- no diarization spans were available
+2. **No speaker reached threshold** -- no speaker's total duration met the substantial speaker threshold
+3. **Insufficient kept audio** -- the target speaker's merged audio was below the minimum kept audio duration
+4. **Filtered audio extraction failed** -- extracting audio samples from the buffer for the kept spans failed
+5. **Empty filtered transcription** -- transcription of the extracted audio returned nothing or only whitespace
+
+### 7.5 Serialization
+
+The diarization summary and all nested types are serializable for persistence (used for archival purposes).
+
+### 7.6 Thread Safety
+
+The diarization summary and all nested types are safe to pass across concurrency boundaries.
+
+---
+
+## 8. Error Handling and Graceful Degradation
+
+### 8.1 Model Loading Errors
+
+| Error Condition | Behavior |
+|---|---|
+| Unknown model identifier requested | Transition to error state with a "not found" error (code 404) |
+| FluidAudio model missing variant identifier | Fail with a configuration error (code 500) |
+| Timeout during model download or load | Retry once after 500ms delay |
+| Non-timeout load failure | Transition to error state immediately |
+| Retry also fails | Transition to error state |
+
+### 8.2 Transcription Errors
+
+All transcription errors from either backend are caught and logged. The system returns no result rather than propagating exceptions. Transcription failures are never retried.
+
+### 8.3 Diarization Errors
+
+- Per-chunk diarizer processing errors are caught and logged individually. Processing continues with subsequent chunks.
+- Diarizer model loading failure results in no coordinator being produced. The system degrades gracefully to full-buffer transcription.
+
+### 8.4 Speaker Filtering Fallback Chain
+
+When speaker filtering is enabled but cannot produce a usable result, the system falls back to full-buffer transcription. The five fallback reasons (section 7.4) cover every stage of the finalization pipeline that can fail. In all fallback cases, the filtered transcript is absent, signaling the caller to use the unfiltered transcription path.
+
+### 8.5 Empty Buffer Handling
+
+The system handles empty audio buffers at multiple levels:
+
+- The serialized transcription gate returns no result for empty buffers without entering the serialization queue.
+- The transcription dispatch returns no result for empty buffers without invoking any backend.
+- The audio accumulator silently ignores empty sample arrays during recording.
+
+---
+
+## 9. Debug Logging
+
+### 9.1 Logged Events
+
+The following events are logged under the "model" category:
+
+- Model loading initiated (with model name)
+- Model loading succeeded (with model name)
+- Model loading timed out and retrying
+- Model loading failed (with error description)
+- Transcription failed (with model name and error description)
+- Diarization chunk processing failed (with error description)
+- Diarization model loading failed (with error description)
+
+---
+
+## Open Questions
+
+### OQ-1: FluidAudio Cache Location
+
+The FluidAudio framework manages its own model cache directory. The exact filesystem path is controlled by the FluidAudio SDK and is not specified in this domain.
+
+### OQ-2: Diarization Model Staleness
+
+Diarization models are lazily loaded and cached in memory for the lifetime of the model management subsystem. There is no specified maximum age or staleness check for cached diarization models.
+
+### OQ-3: Whisper Download Source
+
+Whisper model downloads are delegated to the WhisperKit framework. The exact download source (e.g., a HuggingFace repository) and any download resumption behavior are controlled by WhisperKit and are not specified in this domain.
+
+### OQ-4: Concurrent Coordinator Creation
+
+The model management subsystem is main-thread-confined, preventing concurrent access to its methods. However, coordinator creation is asynchronous and loads diarization models asynchronously. If a second coordinator creation request arrives while the first is suspended awaiting model download, the behavior depends on the main-thread serialization guarantees. This is likely safe due to the single-threaded access pattern but has not been explicitly verified.

+# System Domain Specification
+
+**Status:** DRAFT
+**Application version:** v1.9.0
+
+---
+
+## 1. Privacy Permissions
+
+Ghost Pepper requires four macOS privacy permissions. Two are mandatory for core functionality; two are optional and degrade gracefully when denied.
+
+### 1.1 Permission Categories
+
+**Required permissions** (the application cannot function without these):
+
+- **Microphone** -- Recording voice for transcription depends on this. Denial halts initialization with an error.
+- **Accessibility** -- Global hotkey detection and simulated paste keystrokes depend on this. Denial prevents the hotkey monitor from starting.
+
+**Optional permissions** (features degrade when denied):
+
+- **Input Monitoring** -- Improves reliability of keyboard event detection. When denied, the application falls back to accessibility-only operation.
+- **Screen Recording** -- Enables OCR-based features: reading on-screen text for cleanup context and extracting text after paste for learning. Features gated by this permission show inline recovery prompts when it is denied.
+
+### 1.2 Microphone Permission
+
+The microphone permission has three states: authorized, not determined, and denied.
+
+The application queries the operating system's capture device authorization status for audio and maps the result to this three-state model. "Authorized" maps to authorized, "not determined" maps to not determined, and all other values (denied, restricted, or any future status) map to denied.
+
+When the status is "not determined," the application requests access from the operating system, which presents the standard system permission dialog. The request returns a boolean indicating whether access was granted.
+
+The combined check-and-request flow works as follows:
+1. If already authorized, return success immediately.
+2. If not determined, request access and return the result.
+3. If denied, return failure immediately (no prompt is possible; the user must go to System Settings).
+
+During application initialization (unless permission prompts are being skipped), the application checks microphone access. If access is not granted, the application sets the error message to "Microphone access required," transitions to the error state, and halts initialization.
+
+The recovery path opens the System Settings microphone privacy pane using the URL `x-apple.systempreferences:com.apple.preference.security?Privacy_Microphone`.
+
+The application declares the audio input device entitlement (`com.apple.security.device.audio-input`) and includes the microphone usage description: "Ghost Pepper needs microphone access to record your voice for transcription."
+
+### 1.3 Accessibility Permission
+
+The application provides two accessibility checks:
+
+- A **non-prompting check** that queries whether the current process is trusted for accessibility with the system prompt option set to false. This returns a boolean without displaying any dialog.
+- A **prompting request** that queries the trusted-process status with the prompt option set to true, causing the operating system to show its standard accessibility permission dialog if the application is not yet trusted.
+
+The recovery path opens the System Settings accessibility privacy pane using the URL `x-apple.systempreferences:com.apple.preference.security?Privacy_Accessibility`.
+
+When the hotkey monitor fails to start, the application prompts for accessibility permission, sets the error message to "Accessibility access required -- grant permission then click Retry," and transitions to the error state.
+
+During initialization, if accessibility is not granted, the application opens the settings window to draw the user's attention to the missing permission. Initialization does not halt -- it continues through audio pre-warming, model loading, and hotkey monitor startup.
+
+### 1.4 Input Monitoring Permission
+
+The application checks input monitoring permission by querying the IOKit HID subsystem for listen-event access and comparing the returned access level against the "granted" constant.
+
+To request the permission, the application calls the IOKit HID access request for listen events, which may display a system dialog directing the user to System Settings.
+
+The recovery path opens the System Settings input monitoring privacy pane using the URL `x-apple.systempreferences:com.apple.preference.security?Privacy_ListenEvent`.
+
+If input monitoring is not granted when the hotkey monitor is about to start, the application prompts for the permission but does not block startup. It logs that input monitoring was not granted and attempts to operate with accessibility alone.
+
+During initialization, if input monitoring is not granted, the application opens the settings window alongside the accessibility check.
+
+### 1.5 Screen Recording Permission
+
+The application checks screen recording permission by calling the operating system's screen capture preflight function, which returns a boolean without triggering a prompt.
+
+To request the permission, the application calls the screen capture access request function, which may trigger a system dialog.
+
+The recovery path opens the System Settings screen recording privacy pane using the URL `x-apple.systempreferences:com.apple.preference.security?Privacy_ScreenCapture`.
+
+The screen recording permission is tracked by a dedicated state controller with two observable states: granted and denied. The controller initializes its state by checking the permission at construction time. It provides a refresh operation that re-queries the permission status and a request-access operation that triggers the system request and then immediately refreshes.
+
+During onboarding, when the user taps the "Enable" button for screen recording, the application schedules a deferred relaunch: it spawns a shell process that sleeps for 3 seconds and then opens the application bundle. This compensates for the possibility that macOS terminates the application after the screen recording permission is granted. After scheduling the deferred relaunch, the application calls the screen recording request and then opens the Screen Recording settings pane.
+
+In the settings window, if "Use frontmost window OCR context" or "Learn from manual corrections after paste" is enabled but screen recording permission is not granted, an inline recovery prompt is displayed: "Ghost Pepper needs Screen Recording access. Grant it in System Settings, then return to Ghost Pepper." with an "Open Screen Recording Settings" button.
+
+### 1.6 System Settings URL Scheme Summary
+
+| Permission | URL path fragment |
+|---|---|
+| Accessibility | `com.apple.preference.security?Privacy_Accessibility` |
+| Microphone | `com.apple.preference.security?Privacy_Microphone` |
+| Input Monitoring | `com.apple.preference.security?Privacy_ListenEvent` |
+| Screen Recording | `com.apple.preference.security?Privacy_ScreenCapture` |
+
+All URLs use the `x-apple.systempreferences:` scheme.
+
+---
+
+## 2. Onboarding Permission Flow
+
+Onboarding is a four-step wizard that runs on first launch. Step 2 (Setup) is the primary permission acquisition point.
+
+### 2.1 Onboarding Gate
+
+The onboarding wizard is shown when the `"onboardingCompleted"` UserDefaults key is false (its default on a fresh install). When the wizard is completed, the key is set to true and application initialization proceeds.
+
+During onboarding, the application activates as a regular application (visible in Dock and Cmd+Tab), not as a menu bar accessory, so the onboarding window is prominently visible.
+
+If onboarding has not been completed, the menu bar dropdown shows only a "Show Setup Window" button and a "Quit" button, not the full menu bar interface.
+
+### 2.2 Setup Step (Step 2) Permission Grants
+
+The setup step presents four items: microphone, accessibility, screen recording, and AI models. Each item displays a completion indicator (checkmark icon when granted, action button when not granted).
+
+The **microphone row** has a "Grant" button that invokes the combined check-and-request operation. If the user denies microphone access, the button changes to "Open Settings" which opens the microphone privacy pane. After granting, the application enumerates audio input devices and starts a live microphone level meter.
+
+The microphone level meter starts only if microphone permission is already authorized. It installs an audio tap, computes RMS audio levels, normalizes them to a 0-1 range (multiplied by 10, capped at 1.0), and updates the display.
+
+The **accessibility row** has a "Grant" button that invokes the prompting accessibility check. There is no "Open Settings" fallback -- the system prompt from the operating system is the primary grant mechanism.
+
+The **screen recording row** is labeled as "(optional)" in its title. Its subtitle indicates the purpose as enhancing cleanup by reading on-screen text and notes that data never leaves the computer. Below the row, when permission is not granted, the caption reads: "You can enable this later in Settings."
+
+### 2.3 Setup Step Permission Polling
+
+The setup step starts a repeating timer at 2-second intervals that polls accessibility and screen recording permission status, because the user grants these permissions in System Settings outside the application.
+
+On each poll cycle, the application checks accessibility status and updates the local grant flag if granted. It also refreshes the screen recording permission state.
+
+The polling timer stops when both accessibility and screen recording are granted, or when the setup step disappears from the view hierarchy.
+
+### 2.4 Setup Step Completion Gate
+
+The "Continue" button on the setup step appears only when all three conditions are met: microphone is granted, accessibility is granted, and the speech model is loaded and ready. Screen recording permission is not required to proceed.
+
+### 2.5 On-Appear Initialization
+
+When the setup step appears, the application checks existing microphone and accessibility permission states. If microphone is already authorized, it enumerates devices and starts the level meter immediately. If the speech model is not yet loaded, it begins loading.
+
+### 2.6 Try-It Step (Step 3) Permission Dependency
+
+The try-it step creates a dedicated hotkey monitor that requires accessibility permission. If the monitor fails to start, it retries up to 5 times at 2-second intervals. If all retries fail, it displays the message: "Could not start hotkey monitor. Please verify Accessibility is enabled in System Settings."
+
+---
+
+## 3. Settings Window Permission Display
+
+### 3.1 Permission Status Polling
+
+The settings window tracks three permission states: accessibility granted, input monitoring granted, and screen recording granted. These are refreshed when the settings window appears and when the application becomes active (returns from System Settings).
+
+If either accessibility or input monitoring is not granted, the settings window starts a repeating timer at 2-second intervals to poll both permissions. The timer stops when both are granted or when the settings window disappears.
+
+### 3.2 Permission Status Card
+
+In the Recording settings section, if either accessibility or input monitoring permission is missing, a "Permissions" card is displayed containing a status row for each permission. Each row shows a green checkmark icon when granted or a red X icon when denied, along with a "Grant" button for denied permissions.
+
+The accessibility "Grant" button invokes the prompting accessibility check (which may display the system dialog).
+
+The input monitoring "Grant" button invokes both the input monitoring prompt and opens the Input Monitoring settings pane, since the IOKit prompt may not display a visible dialog on all system versions.
+
+Below the permission status rows, explanatory text reads: "Both permissions are required for hotkeys and pasting to work reliably. If Ghost Pepper doesn't appear in Input Monitoring, click + and select it from Applications."
+
+When both accessibility and input monitoring are granted, the entire "Permissions" card is hidden from the Recording section.
+
+---
+
+## 4. Menu Bar Permission Recovery
+
+When the application has an error message, the menu bar dropdown displays the error text in red. Additionally, it displays context-sensitive recovery buttons based on keyword matching in the error text:
+
+- If the error text contains "Input Monitoring," the system shows an "Open Input Monitoring Settings" button (opening the Input Monitoring privacy pane) and a "Retry" button (which re-attempts starting the hotkey monitor).
+- If the error text contains "Accessibility," the system shows an "Open Accessibility Settings" button (opening the Accessibility privacy pane) and a "Retry" button (which re-attempts starting the hotkey monitor).
+- If the error text contains "Microphone," the system shows an "Open Microphone Settings" button (opening the Microphone privacy pane). No retry button is shown for microphone errors.
+
+---
+
+## 5. Initialization Sequence (Permissions Perspective)
+
+When not skipping permission prompts, the initialization sequence proceeds in this order:
+
+1. Register launch at login (first run only; see Section 8).
+2. Check microphone permission; if denied, halt with error.
+3. Check accessibility and input monitoring; if either is missing, open the settings window. Do not halt.
+4. Pre-warm the audio engine.
+5. Start focus tracking for paste target detection.
+6. Load the speech model.
+7. Start the hotkey monitor (which may prompt for accessibility if it fails, and may prompt for input monitoring if not granted).
+8. Refresh the cleanup model state.
+
+A "skip permission prompts" mode exists that bypasses the microphone check and the accessibility/input monitoring check in steps 2-3. This is used when initialization follows onboarding, where permissions were already acquired.
+
+---
+
+## 6. Data Persistence: User Preferences
+
+All user preferences are stored in UserDefaults. The application uses two access patterns: a declarative property wrapper that provides automatic persistence and UI binding, and manual read/write calls that support first-run nil-check semantics.
+
+### 6.1 Declarative Property Wrapper Keys
+
+| Key | Value type | Default value |
+|-----|-----------|---------------|
+| `"onboardingCompleted"` | Boolean | `false` |
+| `"cleanupEnabled"` | Boolean | `true` |
+| `"cleanupPrompt"` | String | The application's built-in default cleanup prompt |
+| `"speechModel"` | String | `"openai_whisper-small.en"` |
+
+### 6.2 Manual Persistence Keys with Nil-Check Semantics
+
+For each manually-persisted setting, the application checks whether the key exists before reading its boolean value. If the key does not exist, the application applies a specific first-run default rather than the platform's implicit false. After reading each setting during initialization, the application immediately writes the resolved value back to UserDefaults to ensure round-trip consistency. Each setting is also written to UserDefaults on every mutation.
+
+| Key | Value type | First-run default |
+|-----|-----------|-------------------|
+| `"cleanupBackend"` | String (raw value) | `"localModels"` |
+| `"frontmostWindowContextEnabled"` | Boolean | `false` |
+| `"postPasteLearningEnabled"` | Boolean | `true` |
+| `"ignoreOtherSpeakers"` | Boolean | `false` |
+| `"playSounds"` | Boolean | `true` |
+
+### 6.3 One-Shot First-Run Key
+
+| Key | Value type | Default |
+|-----|-----------|---------|
+| `"hasSetLaunchAtLogin"` | Boolean | `false` |
+
+This key guards one-time registration of launch-at-login on first run. Once set to true, launch-at-login registration is never automatically repeated.
+
+### 6.4 Cleanup Model Selection Key
+
+| Key | Value type | Default |
+|-----|-----------|---------|
+| `"selectedCleanupModelKind"` | String (raw value) | `"qwen35_0_8b_q4_k_m"` (the 0.8B model) |
+
+When the stored value is not a recognized model identifier (or the key is absent), the application falls back to the default 0.8B model.
+
+### 6.5 Keyboard Shortcut Binding Keys
+
+Keyboard chord bindings are persisted as JSON-encoded binary data under keys derived from the action name.
+
+| Key pattern | Value type | Default |
+|------------|-----------|---------|
+| `"chordBinding.pushToTalk"` | Binary data (JSON-encoded chord) | Right Command (keycode 54) + Right Option (keycode 61) |
+| `"chordBinding.toggleToTalk"` | Binary data (JSON-encoded chord) | Right Command (54) + Right Option (61) + Space (49) |
+
+The key for each chord binding is constructed by concatenating the prefix `"chordBinding."` with the action's raw string value.
+
+When reading a chord binding, if the stored data cannot be decoded as a valid chord, the application returns a "not configured" indicator and falls back to the hardcoded default chord for that action.
+
+Before writing a new chord binding, the application validates that the chord is not already assigned to a different action. If a conflict is detected, the write is rejected with a duplicate-binding error.
+
+### 6.6 Correction Store Keys
+
+The correction store persists two text drafts as plain strings in UserDefaults.
+
+| Key | Value type | Default |
+|-----|-----------|---------|
+| `"preferredTranscriptionsDraft"` | String | Empty string |
+| `"commonlyMisheardDraft"` | String | Empty string |
+
+### 6.7 Cross-Process Preference Access (Cleanup Model Probe)
+
+The cleanup model probe is a separate command-line executable that reads the main application's UserDefaults by opening the preference domain with the suite name `"com.github.matthartman.ghostpepper"` (the main application's bundle identifier). If the suite cannot be opened, the probe falls back to the standard defaults domain.
+
+The probe reads the following keys from the main application's preference domain:
+
+| Key | Fallback if absent |
+|-----|-------------------|
+| `"cleanupPrompt"` | Uses the built-in default cleanup prompt (via `--prompt` flag or the application's default prompt) |
+| `"preferredTranscriptionsDraft"` | Empty string |
+| `"commonlyMisheardDraft"` | Empty string |
+
+The `--prompt` CLI flag, when provided, takes precedence over the `"cleanupPrompt"` value read from the main application's preferences.
+
+This cross-process preference access is read-only. The probe never writes to the main application's preference domain.
+
+---
+
+## 7. Data Persistence: Application Support Directory Layout
+
+All file-based persistence uses the operating system's per-user Application Support directory as its root. The application creates and manages a single top-level directory.
+
+```
+Root: ~/Library/Application Support/GhostPepper/
+
+  debug-log.json                              -- Debug log entries (Section 7.1)
+  models/                                     -- Cleanup model GGUF files (Section 10.1)
+    <model-filename>.gguf                       (up to 3 files, ~535 MB to ~2.8 GB each)
+  whisper-models/                             -- WhisperKit speech models (Section 10.2)
+    models/
+      openai/
+        whisper-tiny.en/                        (~75 MB)
+        whisper-small.en/                       (~466 MB)
+        whisper-small/                          (~466 MB)
+  transcription-lab/                          -- Transcription Lab data (Section 7.2)
+    transcription-lab-index.json                -- Entry metadata index
+    transcription-lab-timings.json              -- Per-entry timing data
+    audio/                                      -- Archived audio recordings
+      <audio-filename>                          (WAV files, one per entry)
+```
+
+FluidAudio models are stored in the FluidAudio framework's own default cache directory, not under this application's tree.
+
+Each persistence subsystem that writes to this directory creates its required parent directories (including intermediate directories) before writing any file. Directory creation is idempotent.
+
+### 7.1 Debug Log Store
+
+The debug log store maintains a bounded collection of timestamped, categorized log entries. Entries are held in memory for UI observation and simultaneously persisted to a single JSON file on disk.
+
+**Storage file:** `<Application Support>/GhostPepper/debug-log.json` (overridable for testing).
+
+**File format:** A JSON array of entry objects. Each entry contains four fields:
+
+| Field | JSON type | Content |
+|-------|----------|---------|
+| id | String (UUID) | Unique identifier |
+| timestamp | Number (Date) | Creation timestamp |
+| category | String | One of: "Hotkey", "OCR", "Cleanup", "Model", "Performance" |
+| message | String | The log message text |
+
+**Capacity management:** The store enforces a maximum of 250 entries (configurable). When the count exceeds the maximum, the oldest entries are removed until the count equals the maximum. This eviction occurs after every entry is recorded and during initialization when loading persisted entries.
+
+**Write behavior:** The store persists the complete entry array to disk on every record operation and every clear operation. Writes are synchronous on the calling thread. File writes are atomic (write to a temporary file, then rename) to prevent corruption from interrupted writes. The parent directory is created before writing if it does not exist.
+
+**Load behavior:** On initialization, the store attempts to load previously persisted entries from the storage file. If the file does not exist, cannot be read, or cannot be decoded as valid JSON, the store starts with an empty entry list. No error is surfaced.
+
+**Sensitive data gating:** The store maintains a viewer count (initially zero). Sensitive-record operations silently discard the entry when the viewer count is zero. When the viewer count is greater than zero, sensitive entries are recorded normally. Incrementing the viewer count always adds exactly one. Decrementing subtracts one but never goes below zero. Sensitive entries that pass the viewer gate and are recorded are persisted to disk. The privacy boundary is at the recording gate, not the storage layer.
+
+**Clear operation:** Removes all entries from the in-memory collection and immediately persists the empty state to disk.
+
+**Formatted output:** The store provides a text representation of all entries formatted as `[HH:mm:ss] [Category] message`, with entries separated by double newlines.
+
+**Error recovery:** All file I/O operations silently swallow errors. A corrupted or unreadable file results in an empty entry list with no notification. A failed write results in lost persistence with no retry or notification.
+
+### 7.2 Transcription Lab Store
+
+The transcription lab store archives recording sessions for later experimental re-processing. It manages three related files in a dedicated subdirectory.
+
+**Storage directory:** `<Application Support>/GhostPepper/transcription-lab/` (overridable for testing).
+
+**Index file** (`transcription-lab-index.json`): Contains a JSON array of entry objects with these fields:
+
+| Field | JSON key | JSON type | Required |
+|-------|---------|----------|----------|
+| Unique identifier | `id` | String (UUID) | Yes |
+| Creation timestamp | `createdAt` | Number (Date) | Yes |
+| Audio file name | `audioFileName` | String | Yes |
+| Audio duration | `audioDuration` | Number (seconds) | Yes |
+| Window context text | `windowContents` | String or null | No |
+| Raw transcription | `rawTranscription` | String or null | No |
+| Corrected transcription | `correctedTranscription` | String or null | No |
+| Speech model identifier | `speechModelID` | String | Yes |
+| Cleanup model name | `cleanupModelName` | String | Yes |
+| Cleanup used fallback | `cleanupUsedFallback` | Boolean | Yes |
+| Speaker filtering enabled | `speakerFilteringEnabled` | Boolean | Yes |
+| Speaker filtering ran | `speakerFilteringRan` | Boolean | Yes |
+| Speaker filtering used fallback | `speakerFilteringUsedFallback` | Boolean | Yes |
+| Diarization summary | `diarizationSummary` | Object or null | No |
+
+The `windowContents` field uses custom serialization: when encoding, only the plain text content of the OCR context is written as a string. When decoding, a present string value is wrapped back into an OCR context value. A null or absent value decodes as no context.
+
+**Timings file** (`transcription-lab-timings.json`): Contains a JSON dictionary mapping entry UUID strings to timing objects. Each timing object contains two optional duration fields (transcription duration and cleanup duration), both expressed as floating-point seconds. When loading timings, entries with keys that are not valid UUID strings are silently discarded.
+
+**Audio directory** (`audio/`): Each recording is a separate WAV file named by the entry's audio file name field. Audio files are written atomically. The WAV format is 16 kHz, mono, 16-bit signed integer PCM (format code 1).
+
+**Capacity management:** The store enforces a maximum of 50 entries (configurable). On every insert operation, after adding the new entry and sorting all entries by creation timestamp (newest first), entries beyond the maximum are pruned. Pruning deletes the audio file for each pruned entry, removes the timing record, and writes the pruned index and timings files. Audio file deletion during pruning silently ignores errors.
+
+**Insert operation:** Performs these steps in order:
+1. Create the audio directory if it does not exist.
+2. Write the audio data file atomically.
+3. Load the existing entry index from disk.
+4. Remove any existing entry with the same identifier (deduplication).
+5. Append the new entry and sort all entries by creation timestamp, newest first.
+6. Load existing timing data from disk and add the new entry's timing record.
+7. Prune entries exceeding the capacity limit.
+8. Write the pruned index JSON atomically.
+9. Create the lab directory if needed.
+10. Write the pruned timings JSON atomically.
+
+**Load operations:** Loading entries returns an empty list if the index file does not exist. Loaded entries are sorted by creation timestamp, newest first. Loading timings returns an empty dictionary if the timings file does not exist.
+
+**Error recovery:** If JSON decoding fails when loading entries or timings, the store performs a destructive reset: it deletes the index file, the timings file, and the entire audio directory. After the reset, the load operation returns an empty result. All file deletions during reset silently ignore errors.
+
+---
+
+## 8. Launch at Login
+
+### 8.1 First-Run Registration
+
+On the first run, the application registers itself for launch at login using ServiceManagement's app service registration. The first-run detection uses the `"hasSetLaunchAtLogin"` UserDefaults key. If this key is not set to true, the application registers and then sets the key. The registration call suppresses errors silently to accommodate environments where login item registration may not be available.
+
+### 8.2 User Toggle
+
+The settings window's General section provides a "Launch at login" toggle. The toggle state is initialized by querying whether the main app service status is "enabled."
+
+When the user enables the toggle, the application calls the service register function. When the user disables it, the application calls the unregister function. If either operation throws an error, the toggle reverts to its previous state.
+
+---
+
+## 9. Auto-Update System
+
+The application uses the Sparkle framework for automatic update checking and installation.
+
+### 9.1 Updater Lifecycle
+
+The updater is lazily initialized. The application entry point holds a lazy wrapper that defers Sparkle framework initialization until the updater is first accessed (when the full menu bar view is shown after onboarding completes).
+
+On initialization, the updater creates a standard Sparkle updater controller with the updater started immediately, with no updater delegate, and no user driver delegate.
+
+### 9.2 Update Check Configuration
+
+Automatic update checks are enabled by default. The check interval is 86,400 seconds (24 hours).
+
+### 9.3 Update Feed
+
+The update feed URL is configured in the application's Info.plist as `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/matthartman/ghost-pepper/main/appcast.xml`.
+
+Updates are verified using EdDSA signatures. The public EdDSA key `aU72TC8lOwqKtnWlwAkT8z7LhIvB+LRnUNRNNw9DmDQ=` is embedded in the Info.plist under `SUPublicEDKey`.
+
+### 9.4 Manual Update Check
+
+The menu bar dropdown includes a "Check for Updates" button that triggers a manual update check via the Sparkle updater.
+
+### 9.5 Appcast Feed Format
+
+The appcast is a valid RSS 2.0 document using the Sparkle XML namespace (`http://www.andymatuschak.org/xml-namespaces/sparkle`) and the Dublin Core namespace (`http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/`).
+
+The channel title is "Ghost Pepper" and the channel link points to the GitHub repository. The feed currently advertises only a single item (the latest version); historical versions are not retained.
+
+Each appcast item contains:
+- A human-readable title (e.g., "Version 1.9").
+- A Sparkle version element containing the internal build number (e.g., "10"), which is the value compared by Sparkle to determine update availability.
+- A short version string element containing the marketing version (e.g., "1.9").
+- A minimum system version element (e.g., "14.0") matching the deployment target.
+- An enclosure element with the download URL, MIME type `application/octet-stream`, an EdDSA signature in the `sparkle:edSignature` attribute, and the file size in bytes.
+
+---
+
+## 10. Model Caches
+
+The application downloads and caches machine learning model files for both speech-to-text transcription and LLM-based text cleanup. All model caches are permanent -- files are downloaded once and reused indefinitely. There is no automatic cache eviction, expiration, or size management.
+
+### 10.1 Cleanup Model Cache (GGUF)
+
+Cleanup model files are stored in `<Application Support>/GhostPepper/models/`. Three model variants may be present:
+
+| Model variant | Approximate file size | Max token count |
+|--------------|----------------------|-----------------|
+| 0.8B (Very fast) | ~535 MB | 2048 |
+| 2B (Fast) | ~1.3 GB | 2048 |
+| 4B (Full) | ~2.8 GB | 4096 |
+
+Each model file is downloaded from a HuggingFace URL with a progress-reporting download. Model availability is determined by checking whether the expected file exists on disk.
+
+### 10.2 WhisperKit Speech Model Cache
+
+WhisperKit speech model files are stored under `<Application Support>/GhostPepper/whisper-models/`. Each model is cached in a path constructed by appending its cache path components to a `models/` subdirectory:
+
+| Model | Cache path components | Approximate size |
+|-------|-----------------------|-----------------|
+| Whisper tiny.en | `openai/whisper-tiny.en` | ~75 MB |
+| Whisper small.en | `openai/whisper-small.en` | ~466 MB |
+| Whisper small | `openai/whisper-small` | ~466 MB |
+
+Model availability is determined by checking whether the model directory exists at its constructed cache path.
+
+### 10.3 FluidAudio Speech Model Cache
+
+FluidAudio models (Parakeet v3, approximately 1.4 GB) are stored in the FluidAudio framework's own default cache directory. The application does not control or configure this location.
+
+### 10.4 Sortformer Diarization Model Cache
+
+The Sortformer diarization model (used for speaker filtering with FluidAudio) is downloaded from HuggingFace. The caching strategy is managed by the FluidAudio framework.
+
+### 10.5 Total Disk Budget
+
+| Category | Maximum size |
+|----------|-------------|
+| Cleanup models (all three) | ~4.6 GB |
+| WhisperKit models (all three) | ~1.0 GB |
+| FluidAudio Parakeet v3 (outside app directory) | ~1.4 GB |
+| **Worst case total** | **~7.0 GB** |
+| **Typical case (one speech + one cleanup model)** | **~1.0 GB** |
+
+There is no mechanism for the user to clear model caches from within the application. Reclaiming disk space requires manual filesystem deletion.
+
+---
+
+## 11. Correction Store Persistence
+
+The correction store manages two categories of user-defined text corrections. Both are persisted as raw draft text strings in UserDefaults and parsed into structured data on every write.
+
+### 11.1 Preferred Transcriptions
+
+Stored under the key `"preferredTranscriptionsDraft"`. When absent, defaults to an empty string.
+
+The draft text is parsed into a structured list by:
+1. Splitting on newline characters.
+2. Trimming whitespace from each line.
+3. Filtering out empty lines.
+4. Deduplicating while preserving the order of first occurrence.
+
+```
+Format: One term per line. Blank lines are ignored.
+
+Example:
+  Ghost Pepper
+  ChatGPT
+  Kubernetes
+
+Parsed result: ["Ghost Pepper", "ChatGPT", "Kubernetes"]
+```
+
+The parsed preferred transcriptions list is also exposed as a custom word list for the OCR system, with no transformation applied.
+
+### 11.2 Commonly Misheard Replacements
+
+Stored under the key `"commonlyMisheardDraft"`. When absent, defaults to an empty string.
+
+The draft text is parsed into a structured list of replacement pairs by:
+1. Splitting on newline characters.
+2. Trimming whitespace from each line.
+3. Filtering out empty lines.
+4. Splitting each remaining line on the `"->"` separator.
+5. Requiring exactly two parts after splitting. Lines with zero or more than one separator are silently excluded from the parsed result (but preserved in the draft text).
+6. Trimming whitespace from both the "wrong" and "right" parts.
+7. Filtering out pairs where either part is empty after trimming.
+8. Deduplicating while preserving the order of first occurrence.
+
+```
+Format: One replacement per line. Each line contains a "wrong"
+part and a "right" part separated by " -> " (arrow with spaces).
+
+Example:
+  chat gbt -> ChatGPT
+  ghost pepper -> Ghost Pepper
+  cube control -> Kubernetes
+  incomplete line without arrow
+
+Parsed result:
+  {wrong: "chat gbt", right: "ChatGPT"}
+  {wrong: "ghost pepper", right: "Ghost Pepper"}
+  {wrong: "cube control", right: "Kubernetes"}
+
+"incomplete line without arrow" is preserved in the raw
+draft text but is not included in the parsed replacements.
+```
+
+### 11.3 Dual Representation
+
+The store maintains both the raw draft text (for UI binding and editing) and the parsed structured data (for programmatic consumption) simultaneously. Both representations are published for observation by the UI layer.
+
+Every mutation to the draft text immediately re-parses the structured data and persists the updated draft to UserDefaults.
+
+### 11.4 Programmatic Append
+
+The application supports appending a single replacement pair programmatically (used by the post-paste learning system). The append operation:
+1. Skips the append if the replacement's "wrong" or "right" part is empty, or if an identical replacement already exists in the parsed list.
+2. Formats the new entry as `"wrong -> right"`.
+3. Ensures a newline separator exists between the existing draft text and the new entry (if the draft does not already end with a newline, one is prepended).
+4. Triggers the full update cycle (re-parse and persist).
+
+---
+
+## 12. Audio Serialization Formats
+
+The application uses two audio serialization formats.
+
+### 12.1 Raw Float32 Format
+
+A direct memory copy of the floating-point sample array. Each sample is a 32-bit IEEE 754 float. Samples are written contiguously with no header, framing, or metadata.
+
+Deserialization validates that the total byte count is an exact multiple of 4. If not, deserialization fails with an invalid-data error.
+
+This format is used for internal audio buffer passing and is not suitable for playback or archival.
+
+### 12.2 WAV Format (Playable Archive)
+
+Produces a valid RIFF/WAVE file with these parameters:
+
+| Parameter | Value |
+|-----------|-------|
+| Container | RIFF with WAVE form type |
+| Audio format code | 1 (PCM) |
+| Sample rate | 16,000 Hz |
+| Channel count | 1 (mono) |
+| Bits per sample | 16 (signed integer) |
+| Byte rate | 32,000 bytes/second |
+| Block align | 2 bytes |
+
+The WAV file contains exactly two chunks after the RIFF header: a `fmt ` chunk (16 bytes of format data) and a `data` chunk containing the sample data.
+
+Float32 samples are converted to 16-bit signed integers by clamping each sample to [-1.0, 1.0] and scaling by 32767. The result is stored in little-endian byte order. This conversion is lossy; round-tripping preserves sample values within an accuracy tolerance of 0.0001.
+
+### 12.3 Auto-Detection on Deserialization
+
+The archive deserialization operation auto-detects the format by inspecting the first four bytes:
+- If the data starts with the ASCII bytes `"RIFF"`, it is decoded as WAV.
+- Otherwise, it is decoded as raw Float32 data.
+
+### 12.4 WAV Deserialization Validation
+
+WAV deserialization validates:
+1. The data is at least 44 bytes (minimum valid WAV header size).
+2. The first 4 bytes are `"RIFF"`.
+3. Bytes 8-12 are `"WAVE"`.
+4. The audio format code in the `fmt ` chunk is 1 (PCM).
+5. The channel count is 1 (mono).
+6. The bits per sample is 16.
+7. The sample data byte count is an even number.
+
+If any validation fails, deserialization fails with an invalid-data error.
+
+WAV deserialization iterates through chunks using sub-chunk IDs and sizes (supporting arbitrary chunk ordering), handling odd-size padding by advancing to the next even byte boundary.
+
+---
+
+## 13. Application Relaunch
+
+The application supports restarting itself (for example, after certain permission changes or error recovery).
+
+The relaunch operation performs two steps in sequence:
+1. Spawn a new instance of the application by executing `/usr/bin/open` with the `-n` flag (open a new instance) and the current application bundle URL path as the argument.
+2. Terminate the current application.
+
+If the relaunch operation fails (process spawn failure), the error is surfaced as an application error message: "Failed to relaunch Ghost Pepper: [error description]".
+
+---
+
+## 14. Sandbox, Entitlements, and Security
+
+The application sandbox is disabled. This is required because the application uses global keyboard event taps, simulated keystrokes via event posting, and screen capture for OCR -- all capabilities incompatible with the app sandbox.
+
+The hardened runtime is disabled in the build configuration. Apple notarization requires hardened runtime; this setting is expected to be overridden at archive or export time for distribution builds.
+
+The application uses automatic code signing with the "Apple Development" identity. The development team identifier is `BBVMGXR9AY`. Entitlements modification is permitted during the signing process.
+
+The only declared entitlement is audio input device access (`com.apple.security.device.audio-input`).
+
+The application declares itself as a UI element (`LSUIElement: true`) so it runs as a menu bar application without a Dock icon under normal operation.
+
+---
+
+## 15. Build and Distribution
+
+### 15.1 Project Generation
+
+The project uses XcodeGen to generate the Xcode project from a declarative YAML configuration. The generated project file is not checked in directly.
+
+The project is named "GhostPepper." The bundle identifier prefix is `com.github.matthartman`. The deployment target is macOS 14.0 (Sonoma). The Xcode version is 16.0.
+
+### 15.2 Build Targets
+
+The project defines three build targets:
+
+**Main application:** A macOS application with the bundle identifier `com.github.matthartman.ghostpepper`. It depends on four third-party packages: WhisperKit, FluidAudio, the LLM product from LLM.swift, and Sparkle. The asset catalog app icon name is "AppIcon."
+
+**Unit test bundle:** A hosted test bundle for macOS that depends on the main application target. Tests run inside the application process (the test host is the built application binary). The Info.plist is auto-generated.
+
+**Cleanup model probe:** A command-line tool for macOS with the bundle identifier `com.github.matthartman.ghostpepper.cleanup-model-probe`. It depends only on the LLM product from LLM.swift. Its runtime library search paths include the inherited paths and `@executable_path/Frameworks`.
+
+### 15.3 Package Dependencies
+
+| Package | Repository | Version constraint |
+|---------|-----------|-------------------|
+| WhisperKit | `https://github.com/argmaxinc/WhisperKit.git` | From 0.16.0 (semantic version range) |
+| FluidAudio | `https://github.com/FluidInference/FluidAudio.git` | From 0.12.4 (semantic version range) |
+| LLM.swift | `https://github.com/obra/LLM.swift.git` | Branch: `main` (a fork, not the upstream repository) |
+| Sparkle | `https://github.com/sparkle-project/Sparkle.git` | From 2.6.0 (semantic version range) |
+
+WhisperKit provides Whisper speech-to-text inference for Apple Silicon. FluidAudio provides the Parakeet speech model backend and Sortformer speaker diarization. LLM.swift provides the local LLM inference backend for text cleanup. Sparkle provides the auto-update framework with EdDSA signature verification.
+
+The main application target consumes the "LLM" product from the LLM.swift package (not a product with the same name as the package).
+
+Note: The LLM.swift dependency uses branch tracking rather than a version pin, so builds are not reproducible across time. It points to a fork under the project maintainer's control.
+
+### 15.4 DMG Distribution
+
+A build script automates the release build and DMG packaging workflow. It performs these steps in order:
+
+1. Read the version and build number from the Info.plist.
+2. Clean any prior build artifacts.
+3. Build a release configuration using `xcodebuild` with the `-skipMacroValidation` flag.
+4. Verify the built application bundle exists; exit with an error if not.
+5. Copy the application bundle into a staging directory and create an `/Applications` symlink alongside it.
+6. Create a compressed (UDZO format) DMG using `hdiutil` with the project name as the volume name.
+7. Locate the Sparkle `sign_update` tool and generate an EdDSA signature for the DMG. If the tool is not found, print a warning rather than failing.
+8. Clean up the staging directory and derived data.
+9. Print the DMG file path, size in bytes, and next steps.
+
+The build script does not perform notarization or code signing beyond what Xcode applies during the build.
+
+The distribution DMG is hosted as a GitHub release asset at the URL pattern `https://github.com/matthartman/ghost-pepper/releases/download/v{version}/GhostPepper.dmg`.
+
+### 15.5 Notarization
+
+The application is notarized by Apple for distribution. The notarization workflow is not captured in the repository and appears to be performed manually or via Xcode archive export.
+
+### 15.6 Version Numbering
+
+The application uses two version numbers:
+- A marketing version string (e.g., "1.9").
+- A build number (e.g., "10"), which is monotonically increasing and is the value used by Sparkle for update comparison.
+
+Both version numbers are set in the Info.plist and must also be reflected in the appcast for each release. A mismatch between these values causes Sparkle to enter an update loop.
+
+### 15.7 Info.plist Configuration
+
+| Key | Value |
+|-----|-------|
+| Bundle display name | "Ghost Pepper" |
+| Bundle identifier | `com.github.matthartman.ghostpepper` |
+| Marketing version | "1.9" |
+| Build number | "10" |
+| `LSUIElement` | true (menu-bar-only, no Dock icon) |
+| `SUFeedURL` | `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/matthartman/ghost-pepper/main/appcast.xml` |
+| `SUPublicEDKey` | `aU72TC8lOwqKtnWlwAkT8z7LhIvB+LRnUNRNNw9DmDQ=` |
+| `NSMicrophoneUsageDescription` | "Ghost Pepper needs microphone access to record your voice for transcription." |
+
+---
+
+## 16. Deployment Requirements
+
+The application requires macOS 14.0 (Sonoma) or later, enforced by both the deployment target and the appcast minimum system version.
+
+The application requires Apple Silicon (M1 or later). This is not enforced by an explicit architecture constraint in the project configuration; the requirement derives from WhisperKit and FluidAudio, which use Apple Neural Engine and Metal for on-device ML inference.
+
+The release notes state the application requires approximately 2 GB of RAM for both the speech and cleanup models, and approximately 1.5 GB of disk space for model downloads on first launch.
+
+---
+
+## 17. MDM / Enterprise Support
+
+On MDM-managed devices, IT administrators may pre-approve the accessibility permission using a Privacy Preferences Policy Control (PPPC) profile referencing:
+
+| Field | Value |
+|---|---|
+| Bundle identifier | `com.github.matthartman.ghostpepper` |
+| Team identifier | `BBVMGXR9AY` |
+| Permission | Accessibility |
+
+It is unclear whether microphone, input monitoring, or screen recording permissions can also be pre-approved via MDM profiles. The application does not contain MDM-specific logic; pre-approval is a macOS system-level capability that the application benefits from passively.
+
+---
+
+## 18. Release Workflow
+
+The release workflow proceeds as follows:
+
+1. Update the version string and build number in Info.plist.
+2. Run the build script to build, package, and sign the DMG.
+3. Notarize the DMG with Apple (mechanism not captured in repository).
+4. Update appcast.xml with the new version number, build number, DMG size, and EdDSA signature.
+5. Commit and push the appcast changes to the `main` branch.
+6. Create a GitHub release with the DMG attached.
+
+No CI/CD configuration exists in the repository. The release workflow appears to be entirely manual.
+
+---
+
+## 19. Capacity Management Summary
+
+The application uses three distinct strategies for managing persisted data volume:
+
+**Ring buffer (debug log):** 250-entry limit. Oldest entries evicted when the count exceeds the limit. Runs after every record operation and on initialization. Storage is a single JSON file rewritten completely on every operation.
+
+**Oldest-first pruning (transcription lab):** 50-entry limit. Oldest entries by creation timestamp are removed during insert only. Associated audio files are deleted and timing records removed.
+
+**Permanent caching (model files):** No limit. No automatic eviction. Up to ~7 GB if all models are downloaded. Users must manually delete files to reclaim space.
+
+---
+
+## 20. In-Memory-Only Data
+
+The following data exists only in process memory and does not survive application termination:
+
+- Audio sample buffer (cleared on next recording start or explicit reset)
+- Active performance trace (released after completion or abandonment)
+- OCR prefetch result (resolved once per recording session, then released)
+- Diarization data (discarded after finalization)
+- Pipeline owner mutex (released to nil)
+- Pressed key set (maintained by chord engine, reset on stop)
+- Debug log viewer count (incremented/decremented with viewer lifecycle)
+- Loaded LLM model instance (held in memory while active, unloaded on shutdown)
+- Loaded speech model instance (held in memory while active)
+
+---
+
+## 21. Scripts
+
+The project includes three executable shell scripts.
+
+**DMG build script:** Automates the release build and DMG packaging workflow as described in Section 15.4. Uses `bash` with strict error handling (`set -euo pipefail`).
+
+**Cleanup model probe script:** Builds and runs the cleanup model probe command-line tool. Checks whether the compiled binary exists; if not, builds it with code signing disabled and macro validation skipped. Then executes the binary, forwarding all command-line arguments. Supports an environment variable `CLEANUP_MODEL_PROBE_DERIVED_DATA` to override the default derived data location.
+
+**Model download script:** Downloads the `ggml-small.en.bin` Whisper model (~466 MB) from HuggingFace to `Resources/models/`. Skips the download if the file already exists. This script downloads the whisper.cpp format model, while the main application uses WhisperKit format models downloaded at runtime to `~/Library/Application Support/GhostPepper/`.
+
+---
+
+## 22. Testing Infrastructure
+
+The unit test suite is a hosted test bundle that runs inside the main application process, allowing tests to access internal symbols.
+
+The test suite consists of 29 test files covering all major subsystems: audio recording and serialization, keyboard chord engine and binding, the cleanup pipeline (backend, prompt builder, correction store, deterministic corrections, text cleaner, cleanup manager, model probe), context (OCR, focused element), debug logging, hotkey monitoring, input handling (key chords, shortcut capture, text pasting), transcription (speech transcription, model management, recording session coordination, fluid audio speech sessions), transcription lab (controller, runner, store), performance tracing, model inventory, and screen recording permissions.
+
+The testing approach uses fakes, spies, and stubs (not mocks) for dependency injection. Tests exercise real logic with injected dependencies rather than verifying mock interactions.
+
+The default permission behavior during testing returns safe non-interactive defaults: accessibility check returns false, microphone status returns denied, microphone access request returns false, and all settings-opening operations are no-ops. This default is activated automatically when the test host environment variable `XCTestConfigurationFilePath` is detected.
+
+---
+
+## 23. Version History Summary
+
+| Version | Milestone |
+|---|---|
+| v1.0.0 | Initial release |
+| v1.1.0 | Apple notarization; auto-updates via Sparkle |
+| v1.9.0 | Current release (build 10) |
