+# Text Processing
+
+## 1. Purpose
+
+This domain covers the transformation of raw speech-to-text output into polished, human-quality text ready for insertion into applications. It encompasses local language model inference, rule-based corrections, screen context capture for disambiguation, and an adaptive learning system that discovers new corrections from user behavior after paste.
+
+The domain spans four areas of concern:
+
+1. **Cleanup pipeline** -- a multi-stage process that applies deterministic corrections, runs the text through a local language model, sanitizes the model output, and applies a second round of corrections.
+2. **Screen context capture** -- screenshot acquisition, optical character recognition, and background prefetching to provide the language model with on-screen text for domain-term disambiguation.
+3. **Correction management** -- storage and application of user-defined preferred terms and misheard-word replacement rules.
+4. **Post-paste learning** -- observation of user edits after pasting to automatically infer and store new replacement rules.
+
+---
+
+## 2. Cleanup Pipeline
+
+### 2.1 Overall Flow
+
+When cleanup is enabled and a transcription produces non-empty text, the pipeline runs six stages in sequence:
+
+1. **Prompt resolution.** Determine the effective system prompt. If the user has not supplied a custom prompt, the built-in default prompt is used.
+2. **Pre-cleanup deterministic corrections.** Apply the user's preferred transcriptions and misheard replacements to the raw text using the protect-replace-restore algorithm (see section 5.1).
+3. **Input framing.** Wrap the corrected text in `<USER-INPUT>` and `</USER-INPUT>` tags before sending it to the language model.
+4. **Language model inference.** Send the framed input and assembled prompt to the local language model. Inference uses a temperature of 0.1, a thinking mode of "suppressed" (reasoning tokens are generated internally but excluded from output), and a timeout of 15 seconds.
+5. **Output sanitization.** Strip reasoning blocks and orphan reasoning tags from the model output and trim surrounding whitespace (see section 2.4).
+6. **Post-cleanup deterministic corrections.** A reserved stage for future post-model correction logic. Currently returns text unchanged.
+
+The pipeline produces a result containing the final cleaned text, optional timing measurements for model inference and post-processing, an optional diagnostic transcript (prompt, framed input, raw model output), and a flag indicating whether the model was bypassed.
+
+### 2.2 Fallback Strategy
+
+The pipeline MUST always produce output. If the language model is unavailable, produces unusable output, or encounters any error, the system falls back to the deterministically corrected text from stage 2 (with post-cleanup corrections applied). The fallback flag is set to true and no model timing is reported.
+
+Unusable output is defined as model output that, after whitespace trimming, is either empty or consists solely of the string "...".
+
+### 2.3 Prompt Assembly
+
+The system prompt sent to the language model is assembled from up to four sections, joined by double newline separators:
+
+1. **Base prompt.** Either the user's custom prompt or the built-in default.
+2. **Correction hints.** If any preferred transcriptions or misheard replacements exist, they are formatted into a `<CORRECTION-HINTS>` block. Preferred transcriptions appear as bullet items. Misheard replacements appear as arrow-separated pairs (e.g., "- chat gbt -> ChatGPT").
+3. **OCR rules.** When screen context is enabled and available, an `<OCR-RULES>` block instructs the model to treat window text as supporting disambiguation context only -- preferring spoken words, correcting likely misrecognitions of names, commands, files, and jargon visible on screen, and never summarizing or rewriting the window content.
+4. **Window OCR content.** A `<WINDOW-OCR-CONTENT>` block containing the captured screen text, truncated to a maximum of 4000 characters (keeping the first 4000 characters).
+
+When screen context is disabled or unavailable, sections 3 and 4 are omitted entirely.
+
+### 2.4 Output Sanitization
+
+After the language model returns raw output, the system MUST:
+
+1. Remove all matched pairs of reasoning tags (opening tag starting with "think", case-insensitive, with optional attributes, paired with its closing tag) and everything between them.
+2. Remove any orphan opening reasoning tag at the start of the output (with optional leading whitespace) that has no matching closing tag.
+3. Trim leading and trailing whitespace and newline characters from the result.
+
+### 2.5 Built-In Default Prompt
+
+The default prompt instructs the language model to clean up transcribed audio according to these rules:
+
+- Delete filler words: um, uh, like, you know, basically, literally, sort of, kind of.
+- Handle self-correction commands: the exact phrases "scratch that", "never mind", or "no let me start over" delete what the user is correcting.
+- Use screen context and misheard-word information for disambiguation.
+- Fix obvious recognition errors for names, models, commands, files, and jargon when context clearly shows the intended term, but preserve deliberate turns of phrase.
+- Properly punctuate sentences.
+- Honor explicit punctuation or spelling requests from the user.
+- Reproduce the entire transcript of what the user said. Never delete sentences, remove context, or summarize. When uncertain whether to keep or delete something, keep it.
+- The output must read as though competently and professionally written by a human.
+- Do not change the user's word selection unless the transcription is in error.
+
+The prompt includes example input/output pairs demonstrating filler removal, self-correction handling, and complete content preservation.
+
+### 2.6 Cleanup Enable/Disable
+
+Cleanup is controlled by a boolean user preference.
+
+| Setting key | Default | Behavior |
+|---|---|---|
+| `cleanupEnabled` | true | When false, the pipeline is bypassed entirely and raw transcription text is returned unchanged. The language model SHOULD be unloaded from memory when cleanup is disabled and SHOULD NOT be loaded while disabled. |
+
+---
+
+## 3. Language Model Management
+
+### 3.1 Model Catalog
+
+Three Qwen 3.5 models in GGUF format (Q4_K_M quantization) are available:
+
+| Model | Parameters | Download size | Max tokens | Speed label |
+|---|---|---|---|---|
+| Qwen 3.5 0.8B Q4_K_M | 0.8 billion | ~535 MB | 2048 | Very fast |
+| Qwen 3.5 2B Q4_K_M | 2 billion | ~1.3 GB | 2048 | Fast |
+| Qwen 3.5 4B Q4_K_M | 4 billion | ~2.8 GB | 4096 | Full |
+
+The default model is the 0.8B "Very fast" variant.
+
+Models are downloaded from HuggingFace repositories under the "unsloth" organization and stored permanently at `~/Library/Application Support/GhostPepper/models/`.
+
+The user's selected model is persisted under the preference key `selectedCleanupModelKind`. When no stored value exists, the default is the 0.8B model.
+
+For archived recordings that stored model display names, a legacy name mapping resolves old names: "0.8B" maps to the 0.8B model, "2B" or "1.7B" maps to the 2B model, and anything else maps to the 4B model.
+
+### 3.2 Model Lifecycle
+
+The model progresses through five states:
+
+- **Idle.** No model loaded. Initial state and state after explicit unload.
+- **Downloading.** A model file is being fetched from the remote repository. Progress ranges from 0.0 to 1.0.
+- **Loading.** A model file on disk is being initialized into memory on a background task.
+- **Ready.** Model is loaded and available for inference.
+- **Error.** Download or initialization failed. A human-readable error message describes the failure.
+
+State transitions:
+
+| From | Trigger | Condition | To |
+|---|---|---|---|
+| Idle | Load requested | File not on disk | Downloading |
+| Idle | Load requested | File exists on disk | Loading |
+| Idle | Load requested | Availability override is false | Error |
+| Downloading | Download succeeds | File saved to models directory | Loading |
+| Downloading | Download fails | Network or disk error | Error |
+| Loading | Initialization succeeds | Model instance created | Ready |
+| Loading | Initialization fails | Runtime returns no instance | Error |
+| Ready | Load requested | Same model already active | Ready (no-op) |
+| Ready | Load requested | Different model requested | Downloading or Loading |
+| Ready | Unload requested | -- | Idle |
+| Error | Load requested | -- | Downloading or Loading (retry) |
+| Any | Shutdown requested | -- | Idle (release all resources) |
+
+When a load is requested while another load is already in progress, the requester MUST wait (polling at 10 ms intervals) until the in-progress load resolves. If the loaded model matches the request, it proceeds; otherwise a new load begins.
+
+### 3.3 Model Configuration
+
+After loading, the model MUST be configured with:
+
+- Temperature: 0.1
+- Thinking mode: suppressed (for cleanup inference)
+- System prompt: initialized with the default cleanup prompt at load time; overwritten per inference with the active assembled prompt
+
+### 3.4 Inference Serialization
+
+Only one inference operation may execute at a time. Concurrent requests MUST be queued in first-in-first-out order. When the running inference completes, the next queued request proceeds.
+
+### 3.5 Inference Timeout
+
+Each language model inference call MUST be bounded by a 15-second timeout. If the timeout fires before inference completes, the inference is cancelled.
+
+### 3.6 External Dependency: LLM.swift
+
+The language model runtime is provided by LLM.swift (a specific fork, not the upstream version). It provides:
+
+- Model initialization from a local GGUF file path with a maximum token count.
+- System prompt configuration.
+- Conversation history management (clearing between invocations).
+- Inference with a thinking mode parameter.
+- Model parameters: temperature, incremental update callback, postprocess callback.
+- A static backend shutdown method for releasing all resources.
+
+Three thinking modes are supported: none (no reasoning), suppressed (reasoning occurs but excluded from output), and enabled (reasoning included in output).
+
+---
+
+## 4. Screen Context Capture
+
+### 4.1 Window Screenshot Capture
+
+The screenshot capture system acquires a raster image of the single frontmost window. Each capture is stateless and independently identifies the current frontmost window.
+
+**Frontmost window identification:**
+
+1. Query the operating system workspace for the currently active application. If none exists, return no image.
+2. Query the system window list (on-screen only, excluding desktop elements) and select the first window whose owner process matches the active application, whose layer is 0 (normal window layer), and whose alpha is greater than 0.
+3. If no matching window is found, return no image.
+
+**Screenshot acquisition (via ScreenCaptureKit):**
+
+1. Query the screen capture framework for shareable on-screen windows and match by the target window's platform identifier. If no match, return no image.
+2. Capture the window in single-window isolation mode (not as a screen region).
+3. Configure capture at 2x the window's frame dimensions (each dimension clamped to a minimum of 1 pixel) without scaling content. This produces Retina-equivalent detail.
+4. Hide the cursor in the captured image.
+
+### 4.2 Optical Character Recognition (Apple Vision)
+
+The OCR system uses the Apple Vision framework to extract text from the captured screenshot.
+
+**Configuration:**
+
+- Recognition level: accurate (slower, higher-quality mode).
+- Language correction: enabled.
+- Custom vocabulary: the user's preferred transcription terms from the correction store, improving recognition of domain-specific words.
+- Recognition revision: 3.
+
+**Text extraction and ordering:**
+
+1. Collect the top recognition candidate from each text observation.
+2. Sort observations into natural reading order:
+   - Primary: descending vertical midpoint (top-to-bottom in Vision's coordinate system where Y=0 is at the image bottom).
+   - Secondary: ascending horizontal midpoint (left-to-right) for same-line observations.
+   - Vertical tolerance: observations whose vertical midpoints differ by 0.02 normalized units or less are treated as the same line.
+3. Join all sorted candidates with a single space and trim surrounding whitespace.
+4. Compute average confidence across all observations.
+5. If no text is recognized, return no result.
+
+A convenience accessor returns only the text string without the confidence score.
+
+### 4.3 OCR Service Orchestration
+
+The OCR service composes screenshot capture and text recognition into a single operation that produces a screen context result (the recognized text as a string).
+
+Flow:
+
+1. Attempt to capture a screenshot of the frontmost window.
+2. If no screenshot is available, log a diagnostic message and return no context.
+3. Pass the captured image and custom vocabulary to the text recognizer.
+4. If no text is recognized, log a diagnostic message and return no context.
+5. On success, return the recognized text as the screen context.
+
+**Permission handling:**
+
+- Screen recording permission is required for window capture. However, the service MUST NOT gate capture attempts on the permission preflight result. It always attempts capture regardless of reported permission status. Permission status is used only to distinguish permission-related failures from other failures in diagnostic log messages.
+- When the permission provider reports screen recording as unavailable and an error occurs, the log message indicates that screen recording permission appears unavailable. Otherwise, the message reports the error without a permission qualifier.
+
+**Error handling:**
+
+- All errors in the OCR pipeline (capture failure, recognition failure, permission denial) result in no context being returned. The system MUST NOT crash, throw to callers, or halt the recording pipeline due to OCR failures.
+- When screen context is unavailable and the feature is enabled, the cleanup pipeline proceeds without window context. The prompt assembly omits the OCR sections entirely.
+
+**Logging:**
+
+- On successful capture: a non-sensitive log message indicates text was captured; the actual OCR text is logged only through a sensitive log channel.
+- On failure: a diagnostic message with permission context when relevant.
+
+### 4.4 Background OCR Prefetch
+
+To hide OCR latency, capture begins when recording starts so the result is typically ready before transcription completes.
+
+The prefetch operates as a simple three-state lifecycle:
+
+- **Idle.** No capture task exists.
+- **Running.** A capture task is in progress.
+- **Resolved.** The task completed and was consumed.
+
+Transitions:
+
+| From | Trigger | To |
+|---|---|---|
+| Idle | Start | Running |
+| Running | Start again | Running (cancels previous, launches new) |
+| Running | Cancel | Idle |
+| Running | Resolve | Resolved (returns result, then clears to Idle) |
+| Idle | Resolve | Returns nothing |
+
+The prefetch result includes the screen context (which may be absent if capture failed) and the elapsed wall-clock time from capture start to completion.
+
+**Integration with the recording pipeline:**
+
+- The prefetch MUST be started when recording begins, but only when ALL of: text cleanup is enabled, a cleanup backend is available, and the frontmost window context feature is enabled.
+- If any of those conditions are not met, any existing prefetch task MUST be cancelled.
+- Custom vocabulary passed to the prefetch MUST be the user's preferred transcription terms from the correction store.
+- When recording stops and window context is enabled, the system resolves the prefetch and uses its context for prompt assembly. The prefetch duration is recorded in the performance trace.
+- The prefetch MUST be cancelled on application state reset, on prepare-for-termination, and if recording start fails.
+
+### 4.5 Screen Context Feature Gate
+
+The frontmost window context feature is controlled by a user-facing boolean setting. The default value is off (false). When disabled, no OCR capture is attempted and no screen content appears in the cleanup prompt.
+
+---
+
+## 5. Correction Management
+
+### 5.1 Correction Store
+
+The correction store maintains two collections:
+
+**Preferred transcriptions:** A list of strings the user wants preserved exactly in all output (proper nouns, technical terms, product names). These are protected from modification during deterministic correction and hinted to the language model through the prompt.
+
+**Misheard replacements:** A list of "wrong -> right" pairs for words the transcription engine frequently gets wrong. Both sides are trimmed of whitespace.
+
+**Persistence:**
+
+Both collections are stored as raw newline-separated text in user preferences:
+
+| Collection | Preference key |
+|---|---|
+| Preferred transcriptions | `preferredTranscriptionsDraft` |
+| Misheard replacements | `commonlyMisheardDraft` |
+
+**Parsing rules:**
+
+- Preferred transcriptions: split by newlines, trim whitespace per line, discard empty lines, deduplicate preserving first occurrence.
+- Misheard replacements: split by newlines, split each line by "->", trim both sides, discard lines without exactly two non-empty parts after trimming, deduplicate. Lines without a "->" separator are preserved in the raw draft text for UI display but excluded from the parsed replacement list, allowing users to have incomplete lines while editing.
+
+**Programmatic append:** New replacements can be added by the learning system. If either side is empty or the replacement already exists, it is silently ignored. The formatted "wrong -> right" text is appended to the raw draft (with a newline separator if needed), and the full draft is re-parsed to update both the structured list and persisted text.
+
+**OCR vocabulary sharing:** The correction store provides its preferred transcriptions as custom vocabulary to the OCR system, helping the screen text recognizer prefer those terms.
+
+### 5.2 Deterministic Correction Engine
+
+The engine applies corrections using a protect-replace-restore algorithm:
+
+**Phase 1 -- Protect preferred transcriptions:**
+All preferred transcriptions, sorted longest-first, are located in the text using case-insensitive word-boundary matching. Each match is replaced with a unique sentinel token to shield it from subsequent replacement rules.
+
+**Phase 2 -- Apply misheard replacements:**
+All misheard replacements, sorted longest-first by the "wrong" side, are applied using case-insensitive word-boundary matching. Longest-first ordering prevents shorter patterns from matching inside longer ones.
+
+**Phase 3 -- Restore preferred transcriptions:**
+All sentinel tokens are replaced with their original preferred transcription text.
+
+**Word-boundary matching rules:**
+
+- If the phrase starts with an alphanumeric character (Unicode letter or number), a negative lookbehind ensures the match does not occur mid-word.
+- If the phrase ends with an alphanumeric character, a negative lookahead ensures the match does not occur mid-word.
+- The phrase is regex-escaped. Matching is always case-insensitive.
+- Empty or whitespace-only phrases produce no match and are skipped.
+
+---
+
+## 6. Focused Element Inspection
+
+The system uses the macOS accessibility API (ApplicationServices) to inspect the frontmost application's UI element tree. This serves three purposes: determining whether the focused element can accept pasted text, extracting text content from the focused element, and capturing a snapshot of the paste context for post-paste learning.
+
+### 6.1 Frontmost Window Resolution
+
+To identify the frontmost window for a given process, the system queries the system window list (on-screen only, excluding desktop elements) and selects the first window matching the process with layer 0 and alpha greater than 0. The window frame is extracted from the window bounds dictionary. If the bounds cannot be interpreted as a rectangle, the window is skipped.
+
+### 6.2 Paste Target Monitoring
+
+A singleton monitor provides real-time tracking of whether the frontmost application's focused element is editable. It runs for the lifetime of the application.
+
+On start, the monitor registers for application activation notifications from the operating system workspace. When any application activates, the monitor synchronizes to the new frontmost application by:
+
+1. Uninstalling any existing accessibility observer.
+2. Clearing the current paste target observation.
+3. Installing a new accessibility observer for the new application's process.
+
+When installing an observer, the system ensures accessibility information is available from the target application, even for applications that do not natively export it.
+
+The observer subscribes to two accessibility notification types on the application-level element:
+- Focused UI element changed.
+- Focused window changed.
+
+The observer must receive notifications while the application is in any interactive mode, including during modal operations and tracking.
+
+When a notification fires, the notified element is evaluated for paste target eligibility. Focus or window change notifications produce either an "editable" or "non-editable" observation. Other notification types where the element is not a paste target are ignored.
+
+### 6.3 Focused Element Resolution
+
+Finding the focused element uses a two-strategy approach:
+
+1. **Direct query:** Ask the application-level accessibility element for its focused UI element. If this succeeds, use it.
+2. **Descendant search fallback:** If the direct query fails, get the focused window and perform a recursive depth-first search for a focused descendant, stopping at a maximum depth of 12 levels.
+
+### 6.4 Paste Target Eligibility
+
+Eligibility is determined by inspecting a snapshot of the element's accessibility properties: role, enabled state, editable state, focused state, whether a selected text range attribute exists, and whether the value attribute is settable.
+
+The paste target decision follows this priority:
+
+1. If the directly queried focused element is a paste target, return true immediately (and record this observation in the monitor).
+2. If a directly queried focused element exists but is NOT a paste target, return false (the direct query is trusted over background observations).
+3. If no focused element could be queried directly, fall back to the monitor's stored observation: return true only if the observation matches the current process, window, and has an editable status.
+
+The full paste target check sequence:
+
+1. Verify accessibility permission is granted (return false if not).
+2. Identify the frontmost application (return false if none).
+3. Ensure the paste target monitor is started and synchronized.
+4. Resolve the frontmost window identifier.
+5. Query the directly focused element.
+6. Evaluate the element using the priority-based decision logic.
+
+### 6.5 Text Extraction
+
+Text extraction tries two strategies per element, in order:
+
+1. **Direct text:** Read the element's value attribute. Accept both plain strings and attributed strings (extracting the plain text).
+2. **Text marker text:** Read the element's start and end text markers, create a range, and request the string for that range. This handles elements like web content views that use text markers rather than plain value attributes.
+
+If neither strategy produces non-empty text, the system walks up the parent chain trying both strategies at each ancestor, stopping after at most 8 ancestor levels. The first non-empty trimmed text found at any level is returned.
+
+### 6.6 Paste Session Capture
+
+At the time of a paste, the system captures:
+
+- The pasted text and paste timestamp.
+- The frontmost application's bundle identifier.
+- The frontmost window's identifier and frame.
+- The focused element's frame.
+- The focused element's text content (via the text extraction chain).
+
+If no frontmost application exists, the session is not captured.
+
+### 6.7 Permissions
+
+| Permission | Purpose | Behavior when missing |
+|---|---|---|
+| Screen Recording | Window capture and OCR | Optional feature; capture is attempted regardless of preflight status; errors are diagnosed with permission context |
+| Accessibility | Paste target detection and text extraction | Checked at entry; returns false/nil immediately if not granted |
+
+---
+
+## 7. Post-Paste Learning
+
+### 7.1 Overview
+
+After text is pasted into a target application, the system can observe the user's subsequent edits and infer new misheard-word replacement rules. This feature is optional and gated by a learning-enabled setting (default: true).
+
+### 7.2 Timing Constants
+
+| Constant | Value |
+|---|---|
+| Observation window | 15 seconds |
+| Poll interval | 1 second |
+| Quiescence period | 2 seconds |
+| Maximum replacement word count | 2 words per side |
+| Maximum poll count | 16 |
+| Required stable poll count | 2 |
+
+### 7.3 Polling Behavior
+
+1. On paste, if learning is enabled, schedule the first poll immediately.
+2. Each poll reads the current focused element text via the accessibility API.
+3. The observation is eligible only if the user is still in the same application where the paste occurred (matching bundle identifier). Window and element frame drift within the same application is tolerated.
+4. If text is available and no baseline has been captured yet, use it as the baseline.
+5. If text changed from the previous observation, reset the stable count to 0.
+6. If text is unchanged from the previous observation, increment the stable count.
+7. If the stable count reaches the required threshold (2), invoke the replacement inference algorithm.
+8. If the total poll count reaches the maximum (16), stop polling -- the observation window has expired.
+9. Otherwise, schedule the next poll after the poll interval (1 second).
+
+Learning is checked at three points: before scheduling the first poll, during each poll, and before storing a learned correction. If learning becomes disabled at any checkpoint, the operation is abandoned.
+
+### 7.4 Replacement Inference Algorithm
+
+Given a baseline text (before user edit) and an observed text (after user edit), constrained to the originally pasted text:
+
+1. **Validation.** Both texts must be non-empty after trimming and must differ (case-insensitive).
+2. **Word-level diff.** Split both texts into word arrays. Compute a shared prefix (case-insensitive) and shared suffix (excluding prefix). At least one shared prefix or suffix word must exist -- the change must be embedded within existing text.
+3. **Extract the differing segments.** The word segments between the shared prefix and suffix become the "wrong" side and "right" side of a potential replacement.
+4. **Constraint checks:**
+   - Both sides must be non-empty.
+   - They must differ by more than just punctuation (after normalizing non-alphanumeric characters to spaces and comparing lowercased).
+   - Each side must be at most 2 words.
+   - The "wrong" side must appear as a contiguous word sequence in the originally pasted text (case-insensitive).
+5. **Storage.** If all constraints pass, the replacement is stored via the correction store's programmatic append and a callback notifies the UI layer.
+
+### 7.5 Edge Cases
+
+- If the accessibility API cannot read the focused element, the poll is counted but no text is captured.
+- If the user switches applications, observations return nothing until they return to the original application or the window expires.
+- Wholesale rewrites with no shared prefix or suffix produce no learned replacement.
+- Replacements where either side exceeds 2 words are rejected.
+- Punctuation-only edits produce no learned replacement.
+- If the first observation returns nothing (element not yet readable), subsequent observations can still capture the baseline and proceed.
+
+---
+
+## 8. Error Handling Summary
+
+| Failure | System response |
+|---|---|
+| Language model not loaded or not ready | Fall back to deterministic corrections only; set fallback flag |
+| Language model produces unusable output | Fall back to deterministic corrections; include diagnostic transcript |
+| Any other inference error | Fall back to deterministic corrections; set fallback flag |
+| Model download failure | Transition to error state; message: "Failed to download cleanup model: [error]"; user can retry |
+| Model initialization failure | Transition to error state; message: "Failed to load the selected cleanup model."; user can retry |
+| OCR pipeline failure (capture, recognition, permission) | Return no context; pipeline continues without screen context |
+| Accessibility API failure (element query, attribute read) | Return nil for the affected capability; system continues with partial data |
+| Accessibility observer installation failure | Continue without observation; paste target detection falls back to direct query |
+
+---
+
+## 9. Diagnostic Logging
+
+The system uses two log channels:
+
+- **Operational log:** Non-sensitive messages about model availability, loading progress, inference timing. Always recorded.
+- **Sensitive log:** Full transcripts including prompts, raw input, raw model output, and corrected output. Recorded only when a debug viewer is actively open.
+
+---
+
+## 10. Implementation-Defined Behavior
+
+- The exact wording of the default cleanup prompt (rules, examples, formatting) is implementation-defined. The behavioral contract is that it instructs the model to clean up transcribed audio while preserving all content.
+- The sentinel token format used in the protect-replace-restore algorithm is implementation-defined, as long as the chosen format is extremely unlikely to appear in natural text.
+- The 4000-character OCR context truncation limit is implementation-defined and must be documented.
+- Maximum token counts per model are implementation-defined but the smaller models must receive a lower or equal count compared to larger models.
+
+## 11. Unspecified Behavior
+
+- Whether the correction store is read once at pipeline entry or re-read at each stage is unspecified. Any consistent approach is acceptable.
+- The concurrency mechanism for model load serialization (polling, semaphores, etc.) is unspecified.
+- Whether partial downloads are resumed or restarted is unspecified.
+
+## 12. Undefined Behavior
+
+- If a previously valid GGUF model file becomes corrupted on disk, behavior is undefined.
+- If the user edits corrections while a cleanup is in progress, the effect on the in-flight cleanup is undefined.
+- Behavior under system memory pressure during model loading is undefined.

+# User Experience
+
+This specification describes the visual interface, navigation structure, onboarding flow, floating indicators, debug viewing, prompt editing, recording history exploration, and operational logging behaviors of Ghost Pepper.
+
+---
+
+## 1. Application Presence
+
+Ghost Pepper runs as a menu bar application with no Dock icon during normal operation. The sole persistent interface element is a system tray icon with a dropdown panel.
+
+### 1.1 Menu Bar Icon
+
+The menu bar icon reflects the current application status through four visual states.
+
+- While recording, the icon displays a custom red-tinted image rendered in its original colors (not as a system template).
+- While loading, the icon displays an ellipsis-in-circle system symbol colored orange.
+- While in an error state, the icon displays a warning triangle system symbol colored yellow.
+- In all other states (ready, transcribing, cleaning up), the icon displays a custom image rendered as a system template so it adapts automatically to light and dark appearance.
+
+### 1.2 Dropdown Before Onboarding
+
+Before the user completes onboarding, the dropdown contains only two items: "Show Setup Window" (which brings the onboarding window to front) and "Quit".
+
+### 1.3 Dropdown After Onboarding
+
+After onboarding, the dropdown displays the following items in order:
+
+1. A "Settings..." button that opens the settings window.
+2. A "Debug Log..." button that opens the debug log viewer.
+3. A version label reading "Ghost Pepper v{version}" where the version comes from the application bundle. If the bundle key is missing, the version defaults to "1.0".
+4. A status line reflecting application activity:
+   - "Loading..." when loading
+   - "Recording..." when recording
+   - "Transcribing..." when transcribing
+   - "Cleaning up..." when cleaning up
+   - No status line when ready or in an error state
+5. When a cleanup model download is in progress, a linear progress bar showing the download progress.
+6. When an error message is present, the error text displayed in red.
+
+### 1.4 Error Recovery Buttons
+
+Error recovery buttons appear contextually based on the error message text. String matching is case-sensitive, and multiple groups may appear simultaneously if the message matches more than one keyword.
+
+- If the error text contains "Input Monitoring": an "Open Input Monitoring Settings" button (opening the system Input Monitoring privacy pane) and a "Retry" button (re-attempting the hotkey monitor).
+- If the error text contains "Accessibility": an "Open Accessibility Settings" button (opening the system Accessibility privacy pane) and a "Retry" button.
+- If the error text contains "Microphone": an "Open Microphone Settings" button (opening the system Microphone privacy pane).
+
+### 1.5 Footer Items
+
+A divider separates the error area from a "Check for Updates" button. A second divider separates "Check for Updates" from "Quit". The "Quit" button terminates the application and uses the keyboard shortcut Cmd+Q.
+
+---
+
+## 2. Settings Window
+
+The settings window is the primary configuration surface.
+
+### 2.1 Window Properties
+
+- Title: "Ghost Pepper Settings"
+- Initial size: 960 x 720 points
+- Minimum size: 900 x 680 points
+- Style: title bar and close button, no resize control
+- The window is hidden (not destroyed) when closed, and is reused on subsequent show requests
+- Opening settings when the window already exists brings the existing window to front
+
+### 2.2 Layout
+
+The window uses a horizontal split with a sidebar on the left and a scrollable detail area on the right.
+
+The sidebar has a minimum width of 250 points and an ideal/maximum width of 270 points. It uses the system control background color and has a trailing 1-point separator line.
+
+The sidebar lists six sections in the following fixed order. The default selection on window open is Recording.
+
+| Section | Title | Subtitle | Icon |
+|---|---|---|---|
+| Recording | "Recording" | "Shortcuts, microphone input, dictation testing, and sound feedback." | waveform.and.mic |
+| Cleanup | "Cleanup" | "Prompt cleanup, OCR context, and learning behavior." | sparkles |
+| Corrections | "Corrections" | "Words and replacements Ghost Pepper should preserve." | text.badge.checkmark |
+| Models | "Models" | "Speech and cleanup model downloads and runtime status." | brain |
+| History | "History" | "Saved recordings, reruns, and cleanup experiments." | waveform.badge.magnifyingglass |
+| General | "General" | "Startup behavior and app-wide preferences." | gearshape |
+
+Each sidebar item displays a system symbol icon, a title, and a subtitle. The selected section is highlighted with a tinted background and a border stroke.
+
+The detail area for each section shows the section title in a large heading (28pt semibold). For all sections except History, the subtitle is also shown.
+
+### 2.3 Permission Polling
+
+When the settings view appears, the application loads input devices, refreshes screen recording permission status, refreshes accessibility and input monitoring permissions, starts permission polling if any required permission is missing, syncs default values for the recording history panel, and reloads history entries.
+
+Permission polling uses a 2-second timer that checks accessibility and input monitoring permissions. When both are granted, the timer stops automatically. When the settings view disappears, any in-progress test dictation is stopped and the permission timer is cancelled.
+
+### 2.4 Recording Section
+
+The recording section contains four cards.
+
+**Permissions card.** Shown only when accessibility or input monitoring permission is missing. Displays a permission status row for each, with a green checkmark circle icon when granted and a red X-mark circle icon when denied. Denied permissions show a "Grant" button that triggers the system permission prompt. Explanatory text notes that both permissions are required for hotkeys and pasting, and that Input Monitoring may require manually adding the application.
+
+**Shortcuts card.** Contains two shortcut recorder widgets: "Hold to Record" (push-to-talk) and "Toggle Recording" (toggle-to-talk). Changing a shortcut updates the corresponding key binding. Errors from failed shortcut updates are displayed in red.
+
+**Input card.** Contains a microphone selection picker showing available input devices, a "Play sounds" toggle, and an "Ignore other speakers" toggle. The speaker filtering toggle is always visible but disabled when the current speech model does not support speaker filtering.
+
+**Test dictation card.** Provides a start/stop button for testing the recording pipeline within settings. During recording, a red recording indicator dot and "Recording..." text are shown. During transcription, a progress spinner and "Transcribing..." text are shown. On completion, the transcript result is displayed. On error, the error text is shown in red. If the recorder cannot start, the error reads "Could not start recording." If transcription returns no text, the error reads "Ghost Pepper could not transcribe that sample."
+
+### 2.5 Cleanup Section
+
+Three cards.
+
+**Cleanup card.** Contains an "Enable cleanup" toggle. When cleanup is enabled and the cleanup model is in an error state, the error message is displayed in red.
+
+**Cleanup prompt card.** Contains an editable text area bound to the cleanup prompt and a "Reset to Default" button.
+
+**Context card.** Contains a "Use frontmost window OCR context" toggle. When enabled without screen recording permission, a recovery view appears with an "Open Screen Recording Settings" button and red warning text. Also contains a "Learn from manual corrections after paste" toggle with the same screen-recording recovery behavior. Explanatory text describes how OCR context works and that post-paste learning uses high-quality OCR after 15 seconds, keeping only narrow high-confidence corrections.
+
+### 2.6 Corrections Section
+
+A single card with two text editors.
+
+- "Preferred transcriptions" with the prompt "One preferred word or phrase per line"
+- "Commonly misheard" with the prompt "One replacement per line using probably wrong -> probably right"
+
+A divider separates the two editors. Explanatory text notes that preferred transcriptions are preserved during cleanup and forwarded to OCR custom words, and that commonly misheard replacements run deterministically before cleanup.
+
+### 2.7 Models Section
+
+Three cards.
+
+**Speech model card.** A picker listing all available speech models. Changing the selection triggers a model load.
+
+**Cleanup model card.** A picker listing all cleanup models. Changing the selection triggers a model load.
+
+**Runtime models card.** Displays a model inventory card showing the status of all speech and cleanup models. If any model is actively downloading, the active download text (e.g., "Downloading ModelName (XX%)...") is displayed below the inventory.
+
+### 2.8 History Section
+
+The history section operates in two modes: browser and detail.
+
+**Browser mode.** Displays a "Recent recordings" heading and lists all recording history entries. When no entries exist, an empty-state placeholder reads "No Saved Recordings" with a prompt to make some dictations.
+
+Each entry row shows the recording time, date, transcript text (preferring the corrected version over raw), audio duration in seconds, a copy button, and a chevron indicator. Rows highlight on hover.
+
+**Detail mode.** A "Back to recordings" button returns to browser mode. The detail view contains:
+
+- Audio recording section: metadata summary, diarization visualization (if available), play button. The play button is disabled for non-WAV audio files, with a note that playback is available for newly archived recordings.
+- Original transcription section.
+- Re-run transcription section: speech model picker and run button. While running, a progress spinner and "Running..." text are shown. The run button is disabled while any experiment is in progress.
+- Cleanup section: original cleanup output, inline prompt editor, screen context toggle, cleanup model picker and run button. Same progress and disabling behavior as re-run transcription.
+- A "Show full cleanup transcript" button that opens a separate window. Disabled when no cleanup transcript is available.
+- Diff views comparing original to experiment results.
+- Error messages from experiments displayed in red at the bottom of the detail view.
+
+### 2.9 General Section
+
+A single card with a "Launch at login" toggle. Changing the toggle registers or unregisters the application as a login item. If registration fails, the toggle reverts to its previous value.
+
+---
+
+## 3. Onboarding Wizard
+
+A four-step first-run experience that guides the user through permissions, model downloads, and a test recording.
+
+### 3.1 Window Properties
+
+- Title: "Ghost Pepper"
+- Size: 480 x 620 points
+- Style: titled and closable, normal window level
+- Before showing, the application switches to regular activation policy so it appears in the Dock and Cmd+Tab switcher during onboarding
+- Window creation is dispatched asynchronously to allow the activation policy change to take effect
+- Showing the onboarding window dismisses any previously shown instance
+
+### 3.2 Onboarding Gate
+
+A persistent boolean flag controls whether onboarding is shown. When the flag is true, the application initializes directly. When false, onboarding is shown first and the application initializes only after onboarding completes.
+
+### 3.3 Step 1: Welcome
+
+Displays the application icon (128 x 128 points), the title "Ghost Pepper", the tagline "Hold-to-talk speech-to-text for your Mac", a privacy badge reading "100% Private -- Everything runs locally on your Mac. No cloud, no accounts, no data ever leaves your machine." with a green shield icon, and a "Get Started" button.
+
+### 3.4 Step 2: Setup
+
+Heading: "Setup". Subheading: "Grant permissions and download the app models".
+
+Four setup rows in order:
+
+**Microphone.** When not granted and not denied, a "Grant" button triggers the system permission prompt. If granted, the view loads input devices and starts microphone level monitoring. If denied, an "Open Settings" button replaces the grant button. After access is granted, a device picker appears (when more than one device is available) alongside a sound level meter. The level meter shows a colored bar: green normally, orange above 30%, red above 70%. Changing the selected device restarts the level monitor on the new device.
+
+The microphone level monitor starts only if permission is already authorized. It installs an audio tap on the input bus (bus 0, buffer size 1024), computes RMS from float channel data, normalizes the result to 0-1 range by multiplying by 10 and clamping, and publishes the level value. When stopped, it removes the audio tap, stops the engine, and resets the level to zero.
+
+**Accessibility.** A "Grant" button triggers the system accessibility prompt. Status is polled via a 2-second timer.
+
+**Screen Recording (optional).** Labeled as optional with the subtitle "Enhances cleanup by reading on-screen text (never leaves your computer)". When not granted, shows an "Enable" button and the text "You can enable this later in Settings." Pressing the button schedules a relaunch process (via shell with a 3-second delay) to recover if the OS kills the application after permission grant, then requests the screen recording permission and opens the screen recording settings pane.
+
+**AI Models.** Shows a progress indicator when models are loading and a "Retry" button in the error state. Model loading is triggered automatically on appearance if not yet ready. A model summary below shows the selected speech and cleanup models with their download status and the text "You can change models later in Settings."
+
+**Completion gate.** The "Continue" button appears only when all three required conditions are met: microphone access granted, accessibility granted, and models ready. Screen recording is explicitly not a gate. When the continue button is unavailable, a social sharing link is shown instead (a link to tweet about trying Ghost Pepper).
+
+**Permission polling.** A 2-second timer checks accessibility and screen recording status. If both are granted, the timer stops automatically. The timer is cancelled on disappear.
+
+### 3.5 Step 3: Try It
+
+Instructs the user to "Hold Right Command + Right Option and say something" and displays key cap widgets for both keys. The key caps turn red when recording is active.
+
+The try-it step creates its own audio recorder and hotkey monitor configured with the default push-to-talk chord. When the hotkey detects a recording start, the recorder starts. When the hotkey detects a stop, the recorder stops and the audio is transcribed.
+
+If transcription produces text, the result is displayed for 2 seconds with the success message "It works! Your words will be pasted wherever your cursor is." and the wizard auto-advances to the next step. The auto-advance mechanism includes a guard to prevent advancing more than once.
+
+If the hotkey monitor fails to start, the system retries up to 5 times with a 2-second delay between attempts. After exhausting retries, an error message reads "Could not start hotkey monitor. Please verify Accessibility is enabled in System Settings."
+
+"Skip" and "Continue" buttons are always available, allowing the user to bypass the test.
+
+### 3.6 Step 4: Done
+
+Displays a green checkmark icon, the heading "You're All Set!", a menu bar mockup showing where Ghost Pepper lives (with the Ghost Pepper icon highlighted in orange among other mock system tray icons), a bullet list of capabilities:
+
+- Switch your microphone
+- Change your recording shortcuts
+- Toggle text cleanup on/off
+- Edit the cleanup prompt
+- Check for updates
+
+And a "Start Using Ghost Pepper" button that sets the onboarding-completed flag to true and invokes the completion callback.
+

+## 4. Recording Overlay
+
+A non-interactive floating indicator that appears at the bottom center of the main screen during various pipeline stages.
+
+### 4.1 Message Types
+
+| Message | Primary Text | Secondary Text |
+|---|---|---|
+| Recording | "Recording..." | None |
+| Model loading | "Loading models..." | None |
+| Cleaning up | "Cleaning up..." | None |
+| Transcribing | "Transcribing..." | None |
+| Clipboard fallback | "Copied to clipboard" | "(Cmd+V symbol) to paste" |
+| No sound detected | "No sound detected" | None |
+| Learned correction | "Learned correction" | "{wrong} -> {right}" |
+
+### 4.2 Panel Behavior
+
+- Style: non-activating, borderless, transparent background, floating window level, shadow enabled, ignores all mouse events (non-interactive)
+- Appears on all desktops and alongside full-screen applications
+- Positioned at the horizontal center of the main screen, 40 points above the bottom of the visible frame
+
+### 4.3 Sizing
+
+- Clipboard fallback and learned correction messages: 420 x 84 points
+- All other messages: 300 x 60 points
+
+### 4.4 Show and Dismiss
+
+- Showing a message when a panel already exists updates the existing panel rather than creating a new one
+- Showing a message always cancels any pending auto-dismiss timer first
+- Clipboard fallback, learned correction, and no-sound-detected messages auto-dismiss after 3 seconds. All other messages persist until explicitly dismissed.
+- Dismissing cancels any pending timer, hides the panel, and releases all references
+- Conditional dismiss only takes effect if the currently displayed message matches the specified one; otherwise it is a no-op
+
+### 4.5 Visual Design
+
+- Capsule-shaped background filled with black at 85% opacity
+- Status indicators per message type:
+  - Recording: red dot with pulsing animation
+  - Model loading: progress spinner (no dot)
+  - Cleaning up, transcribing, clipboard fallback: blue dot with pulsing animation
+  - No sound detected: orange dot with pulsing animation
+  - Learned correction: green checkmark circle icon (no dot, no pulse)
+- Pulsing animation: oscillates opacity between 0.4 and 1.0, ease-in-out curve, 0.6-second duration, repeating forever with autoreversal
+- Primary text: white, 13pt semibold
+- Secondary text: white at 80% opacity, 12pt medium weight with rounded design, limited to 2 lines
+

+## 5. Debug Log Viewer
+
+A floating utility panel that displays real-time debug events.
+
+### 5.1 Window Properties
+
+- Title: "Ghost Pepper Debug Log"
+- Initial size: 640 x 420 points
+- Style: titled, closable, resizable, utility window style
+- Floating window level, remains visible when the application is deactivated
+- Hidden (not destroyed) when closed; reused on subsequent show requests
+
+### 5.2 Live Viewing Lifecycle
+
+Opening the debug log viewer signals the logging system to begin capturing sensitive log entries. Closing signals the end of sensitive capture. The viewer tracks its own state to avoid redundant begin/end signals.
+
+### 5.3 Toolbar
+
+- "Copy Log" button: copies the formatted log text to the system clipboard. Disabled when no entries exist.
+- "Clear" button: clears all log entries. Disabled when no entries exist.
+
+### 5.4 Log Display
+
+Each entry is formatted as "[HH:mm:ss] [category] message" in a monospaced caption font with text selection enabled. When no entries exist, the placeholder text "No debug events yet." is displayed.
+
+### 5.5 Auto-Scroll
+
+The viewer automatically scrolls to the bottom when new entries arrive, provided the user has not scrolled away from the bottom. The "near bottom" threshold is 32 points. On initial appearance, the viewer scrolls to the bottom. The scroll-to-bottom action is dispatched asynchronously to avoid layout conflicts.

+
+## 8. Shortcut Recorder Widget
+
+A reusable widget for capturing keyboard shortcut chords.
+
+### 8.1 Display States
+
+- Not recording: a button showing the current chord's display string, bordered style with an orange tint.
+- Recording: the button switches to bordered prominent (filled orange) style. The label shows "Press Shortcut" when no keys have been captured yet, or a live preview of the captured keys. Instructional text appears below: "Press the full chord, then release. Press Escape to cancel."
+
+### 8.2 Event Capture
+
+Starting a recording installs a local event monitor for modifier-flags-changed, key-down, and key-up events. All captured events are consumed (not passed to other responders). The widget notifies its parent of recording state changes so the parent can suspend the hotkey monitor during capture.
+
+### 8.3 Event Handling
+
+- Modifier flag changes: the physical key is identified and passed to the capture state machine. If the state machine returns a completed chord, the recording stops with a commit.
+- Key-down: if the key is Escape (key code 53) and no keys have been captured yet, the recording is cancelled. Otherwise, the event is passed to the capture state machine.
+- Key-up: passed to the capture state machine. If a completed chord is returned, the recording stops with a commit.
+
+### 8.4 Commit and Cancel
+
+Committing removes the event monitor, resets capture state, notifies the parent, and invokes the on-change callback with the captured chord.
+
+Cancelling removes the event monitor and resets capture state but does not invoke the on-change callback.
+
+If the view disappears while recording, the recording is cancelled.
+

+## 9. Model Inventory Display
+
+A visual summary of all speech and cleanup model statuses.
+
+### 9.1 Card Layout
+
+The model inventory card displays a vertical list of model rows within a rounded rectangle using the system control background color.
+
+### 9.2 Model Row
+
+Each row displays a status indicator, the model name, a "Selected" badge in orange (when applicable), a status text string, and the model's size description.
+
+Status text values:
+
+| Status | Text |
+|---|---|
+| Loaded | "Loaded" |
+| Loading | "Loading" |
+| Not loaded | "Not loaded" |
+| Downloading (with progress) | "Downloading XX%" (integer percentage) |
+| Downloading (indeterminate) | "Preparing" |
+
+### 9.3 Status Indicators
+
+All indicators are 14 x 14 points.
+
+| Status | Indicator |
+|---|---|
+| Loaded | Green checkmark circle icon |
+| Loading | Mini progress spinner |
+| Not loaded | Hollow circle in quaternary color |
+| Downloading | Pie progress indicator |
+
+### 9.4 Pie Progress Indicator
+
+When progress is known, the pie renders a filled arc proportional to the progress (minimum 0.05, maximum 1.0). The arc starts at the 12 o'clock position (-90 degrees) and sweeps clockwise. When progress is indeterminate, the pie renders a 28% arc that rotates continuously with a 1-second linear animation loop. A background circle stroke at 25% secondary color opacity is always displayed.
+
+### 9.5 Active Download Text
+
+When any model is downloading, a summary line is shown. For determinate progress: "Downloading {name} ({pct}%)...". For indeterminate: "Preparing {name}...". If no model is downloading, this text is absent.
+
+### 9.6 Row Status Logic
+
+A speech model row shows "downloading" (with progress) when the model system is loading and the model is the active one and not yet cached. It shows "loading" when the model system is loading and the model is cached. It shows "loaded" when cached, and "not loaded" otherwise.
+
+A cleanup model row shows "downloading" (with progress) when that specific model variant is being downloaded. It shows "loading" when the model system is in the loading state and the variant matches the selected one. It shows "loaded" when cached, and "not loaded" otherwise.
