+
+## 10. Operational Logging
+
+Ghost Pepper maintains a structured log of operational events with privacy-gated capture of sensitive content.
+
+### 10.1 Log Categories
+
+The system defines exactly five log categories:
+
+| Category | Label | Content |
+|---|---|---|
+| Hotkey | "Hotkey" | Keyboard shortcut detection and recording mode transitions |
+| OCR | "OCR" | Screen context capture status (not captured content) |
+| Cleanup | "Cleanup" | Text cleanup pipeline events including user content (raw transcription, cleanup configuration, context summaries, cleaned output) |
+| Model | "Model" | Model lifecycle events (loading status, empty transcription notices) |
+| Performance | "Performance" | Pipeline timing summaries |
+
+Categories are classified into two sensitivity tiers:
+
+- Non-sensitive (always recorded): Hotkey, OCR, Model, Performance
+- Sensitive (recorded only when the debug viewer is open): Cleanup
+
+### 10.2 Log Entry Structure
+
+Each log entry contains four fields:
+
+1. A unique identifier (auto-generated)
+2. A creation timestamp
+3. A log category
+4. A free-form text message
+
+Entries support JSON serialization for disk persistence and equality comparison for use in reactive UI collections.
+
+### 10.3 Log Store
+
+The log store is a single-instance observable object that publishes changes to its entry list so the debug viewer updates automatically.
+
+**Capacity.** The store enforces a maximum of 250 entries by default. When the count exceeds the maximum, the oldest entries are removed from the beginning of the list until the count equals the maximum. Trimming occurs after every record operation and during initialization.
+
+**Initialization.** The store accepts an optional maximum entry count (default 250) and an optional storage file location. The default storage path is `~/Library/Application Support/GhostPepper/debug-log.json`. On creation, the store loads any previously persisted entries from the file. If the file does not exist, is unreadable, or contains invalid data, the store starts with an empty list silently. After loading, the store trims to capacity.
+
+**Recording.** Recording creates a new entry with the current time, the specified category, and the specified message, appends it to the list, trims to capacity, and persists all entries to disk. This is unconditional and always succeeds.
+
+**Clearing.** Removes all entries and immediately persists the empty state to disk.
+
+**Formatted output.** A computed text representation formats each entry as `[HH:mm:ss] [CategoryLabel] message` using 24-hour time. Entries are separated by double newlines (blank line between each). If no entries exist, the result is an empty string.
+
+### 10.4 Sensitive Data Gate
+
+The store maintains a live viewer count, starting at zero.
+
+- When the count is zero, sensitive recording requests are silently discarded. No entry is created, no disk write occurs.
+- When the count is greater than zero, sensitive recording requests are handled identically to standard recording.
+
+The count is incremented when a viewer begins live viewing and decremented when it ends. The decrement is clamped to zero (never goes negative). Multiple concurrent viewers are supported via the counter.
+
+In practice, four pieces of content are recorded through the sensitive gate (all under the "Cleanup" category): raw transcription text, cleanup configuration details, window context summary, and final cleaned output text.
+
+### 10.5 File Persistence
+
+The store persists the complete entry list as a JSON array on every record and clear operation. Before writing, the parent directory is created if it does not exist. The file write is atomic (write to temporary file, then rename) to prevent corruption. All file I/O is synchronous. All file I/O errors are silently suppressed. Entries survive application restarts via the persisted file.
+
+### 10.6 Privacy Considerations
+
+All entries (both sensitive and non-sensitive) that pass their respective gates are persisted to a JSON file on disk. Non-sensitive operational entries are always written. Sensitive entries captured while the debug viewer was open are also written and remain on disk until they age out of the 250-entry capacity limit or are manually cleared. There is no automatic expiration, scrubbing, or redaction of sensitive entries when the debug viewer is closed.
+
+---
+
+## 11. Performance Tracing
+
+A performance trace captures timing milestones across the full recording-to-paste pipeline.
+
+### 11.1 Trace Structure
+
+A trace is an independent data structure (copied on assignment, not shared by reference) with two fields set at creation: a session identifier (a new unique identifier per trace) and a start timestamp (defaulting to the current time).
+
+### 11.2 Timing Milestones
+
+Ten timestamp milestones (absolute points in time, all initially absent):
+
+| Milestone | Phase |
+|---|---|
+| Hotkey detected | Recording |
+| Microphone live | Recording |
+| Hotkey lifted | Recording |
+| Microphone cold | Recording |
+| Transcription start | Transcription |
+| Transcription end | Transcription |
+| Cleanup start | Cleanup |
+| Cleanup end | Cleanup |
+| Paste start | Paste |
+| Paste end | Paste |
+
+Four pre-computed duration measurements (elapsed intervals, all initially absent):
+
+| Measurement | Description |
+|---|---|
+| OCR capture duration | Time to capture and process screen context |
+| Prompt build duration | Time to assemble the cleanup prompt |
+| Model call duration | Time for the cleanup model inference |
+| Post-process duration | Time for output sanitization after inference |
+
+### 11.3 Summary Format
+
+The trace generates a summary as a single string of space-separated key=value pairs:
+
+1. `session=<id>`
+2. `speechModel=<id>`
+3. `cleanupBackend=<value>`
+4. `hotkey_to_mic_live=<ms>`
+5. `hotkey_lift_to_mic_cold=<ms>`
+6. `transcription=<ms>`
+7. `cleanup=<ms>` or `cleanup=skipped`
+8. `ocr=<ms>` or `ocr=skipped`
+9. `prompt_build=<ms>` or `prompt_build=skipped`
+10. `model_call=<ms>` or `model_call=skipped`
+11. `post_process=<ms>` or `post_process=skipped`
+12. `paste=<ms>`
+13. `total=<ms>`
+
+When cleanup was not attempted, fields 8 through 11 all show "skipped".
+
+### 11.4 Duration Formatting
+
+Duration values are formatted as integer milliseconds followed by "ms" (e.g., "142ms"). Conversion from seconds multiplies by 1000 and rounds to the nearest integer. If either start or end timestamp for a computed duration is absent, the value is "n/a". If a pre-computed duration is absent, the value is "n/a".
+
+### 11.5 Trace Lifecycle
+
+A trace has four states: none (no active trace), active (milestones being stamped), completed (summary recorded), and abandoned (discarded without recording).
+
+- A new trace is created at the start of every recording session. The hotkey-detected milestone is stamped immediately. The cleanup-attempted tracking flag is reset.
+- If recording fails to start (pipeline busy or error), the trace is silently discarded.
+- Milestones are stamped at each corresponding pipeline event as the recording progresses through audio capture, transcription, cleanup, and paste.
+- Completion is triggered after the paste operation completes or when an empty transcription is detected. If the paste-end milestone has not been stamped, it is stamped with the current time as a fallback. The summary is recorded as a "Performance" category log entry. Then the trace is cleared and the cleanup-attempted flag is reset.
+
+---
+
+## 12. Recording History (Transcription Lab)
+
+The recording history system archives recording sessions and enables re-running them with different speech and cleanup models.
+
+### 12.1 History Entry
+
+An entry is an immutable snapshot of a single recording session containing fourteen fields:
+
+1. Unique identifier (auto-generated)
+2. Creation timestamp
+3. Audio file name on disk
+4. Audio recording duration in seconds
+5. Screen context captured at recording time (optional)
+6. Original speech-to-text output (optional)
+7. Cleaned/corrected transcription text (optional)
+8. Speech model identifier used for the original transcription
+9. Display name of the cleanup model used
+10. Whether cleanup fell back to a simpler strategy
+11. Whether speaker filtering was enabled (default: false)
+12. Whether speaker filtering actually executed (default: false)
+13. Whether speaker filtering used a fallback path (default: false)
+14. Summary of speaker diarization results (optional): per-speaker timeline spans, target speaker, kept duration, fallback reason
+
+### 12.2 Entry Serialization
+
+- The screen context field is serialized as a plain string under the key "windowContents" (not as its full structured type). During deserialization, if the key is present, a screen context object is reconstructed from the string. If absent, the screen context is nil.
+- The diarization summary uses conditional serialization: present when non-nil, omitted when nil.
+- Optional text fields (raw and corrected transcription) use conditional encoding: present when non-nil, omitted when nil.
+- The three boolean speaker filtering fields and the cleanup-used-fallback field are always serialized.
+
+### 12.3 History Store
+
+The store persists entries and associated audio files to a configurable directory, defaulting to `~/Library/Application Support/GhostPepper/transcription-lab/`.
+
+Three artifacts are maintained:
+
+| Artifact | Path | Content |
+|---|---|---|
+| Entry index | `transcription-lab-index.json` | JSON array of all entries |
+| Stage timings | `transcription-lab-timings.json` | JSON dictionary mapping entry identifiers to timing records |
+| Audio files | `audio/` subdirectory | One audio file per entry |
+
+Stage timings are stored separately from the entry index. Each timing record contains two optional duration values: one for the transcription stage and one for the cleanup stage.
+
+**Loading entries.** Returns an empty list if the index file does not exist. Sorts results newest-first. If the file exists but cannot be decoded, the destructive archive reset runs and an empty list is returned.
+
+**Loading stage timings.** Returns an empty dictionary if the file does not exist. Silently discards entries with unparseable identifier keys. If decoding fails, the destructive archive reset runs and an empty dictionary is returned.
+
+**Inserting an entry.** Performed as a sequence: create the audio subdirectory if needed, write the audio file atomically, load existing entries, remove any duplicate by identifier, append the new entry, sort newest-first, load existing timings, add the new timing record, enforce the capacity limit by pruning, write the pruned index as JSON atomically, and write updated timings as JSON atomically.
+
+**Capacity management.** The store enforces a maximum of 50 entries by default. When the count exceeds the maximum after an insert, the oldest entries (beyond position N in the newest-first list) are pruned: their audio files are deleted (failures silently ignored) and their timing records are removed.
+
+**Audio URL resolution.** The store resolves a full file system path for a given audio file name by appending it to the audio subdirectory.
+
+**Destructive error recovery.** When a decode error occurs during loading (entries or timings), all three artifacts are deleted: the index file, the timings file, and the entire audio directory. All deletion failures are silently ignored. This is a destructive strategy that favors reliability over data preservation. A corrupt timings file destroys all entries and audio, not just timings.
+
+### 12.4 Experiment Runner
+
+The experiment runner re-executes transcription and cleanup stages against archived audio. All pipeline capabilities are received through injected closures.
+
+**Pipeline mutual exclusion.** Both transcription and cleanup re-runs accept acquisition and release closures. Before executing, the acquisition closure must succeed. If it returns false (pipeline busy), a "pipeline busy" error is thrown. The release closure always runs on exit, regardless of whether the operation succeeded or failed.
+
+**Transcription re-run pipeline:**
+1. Acquire pipeline lock
+2. Load archived audio. If the buffer is empty, throw "missing audio"
+3. Load the requested speech model
+4. Transcribe the audio. If nil, throw "transcription failed"
+5. Return the raw transcription
+
+**Cleanup re-run pipeline:**
+1. Acquire pipeline lock
+2. Build the full prompt from the base prompt, archived screen context, preferred transcriptions, commonly misheard words, and screen context toggle
+3. Execute the cleanup with the raw transcription, assembled prompt, and selected cleanup model
+4. Return the corrected text, a fallback indicator, and a diagnostic transcript
+
+**Diagnostic transcript.** Captures the full prompt sent to the model, the input text the model actually received (which includes pre-cleanup deterministic corrections), and the raw model output (optional).
+
+### 12.5 Experiment State Management
+
+The history panel manages entry selection, experiment execution, timing comparisons, and model synchronization.
+
+**Published state:**
+
+- Entry list (read-only, newest-first)
+- Selected entry identifier (read-write)
+- Selected speech model identifier (read-write, fires synchronizer on change)
+- Selected cleanup model kind (read-write, fires synchronizer on change)
+- Screen context toggle (read-write)
+- Experiment raw transcription (read-only)
+- Experiment corrected transcription (read-only)
+- Experiment transcription duration (read-only)
+- Experiment cleanup duration (read-only)
+- Latest cleanup transcript (read-only)
+- Running stage: idle, transcription, or cleanup
+- Error message (read-only, optional)
+
+**Entry selection.** When an entry is selected: verify it exists, set the identifier, set the screen context toggle based on whether the entry has screen context, and reset all experiment results. Selecting an entry does not change the model selections. Closing the detail view clears the selection and resets all experiment state.
+
+**Display fallbacks.** The displayed experiment transcription returns the experiment result if non-empty, otherwise the selected entry's original transcription. The same pattern applies to corrected transcription. For cleanup input, the active raw transcription prefers any re-run result over the original.
+
+**Original timing comparison.** Original stage timings are loaded from the store, allowing side-by-side comparisons with experiment durations.
+
+**Transcription re-run orchestration:**
+1. Validate an entry is selected (error: "Choose a saved recording first.")
+2. Set running stage to transcription, clear error and durations
+3. Call the transcription runner
+4. On success: store the result, clear corrected transcription and cleanup duration (they are now stale)
+5. On error: map runner errors to user-facing messages:
+   - Pipeline busy: "Ghost Pepper is busy with another recording or lab run."
+   - Missing audio: "This saved recording no longer has playable audio."
+   - Transcription failed: "That model could not produce a transcription for this recording."
+   - Other errors: "The lab rerun failed."
+6. Set running stage to idle
+
+**Cleanup re-run orchestration:**
+1. Validate an entry is selected
+2. Determine raw transcription input; if empty, error: "Run transcription first or choose a recording with a raw transcription."
+3. Set running stage to cleanup, clear cleanup-specific state
+4. Call the cleanup runner
+5. On success: store corrected text, duration, and diagnostic transcript
+6. On error: same mapping pattern as transcription, with generic fallback "The cleanup rerun failed."
+7. Set running stage to idle
+
+**Reload behavior.** Re-reads entries and timings from the store, sorts newest-first. If the selected entry still exists, selection and experiment state are preserved. If it no longer exists, all state is reset. If loading fails, entries and timings are cleared and the error message is set to "Could not load saved recordings."
+
+**Model selection synchronization.** Changing the speech or cleanup model pushes the change back to the broader application state. A batch update operation allows setting both models without firing synchronizer callbacks, used during programmatic initialization.
+
+### 12.6 Archival from Live Recording
+
+Recording sessions are archived after every session, including those that produce no transcription. The two archival points are after a failed transcription (archived with nil raw and corrected text) and after a successful cleanup (archived with full results).
+
+- If the audio buffer is empty, archival is skipped entirely.
+- Audio is serialized in WAV format, named by the entry's identifier with a ".wav" extension.
+- Duration is computed from sample count divided by sample rate (16,000 Hz).
+- Transcription and cleanup durations are extracted from the active performance trace.
+- If cleanup is enabled, the cleanup model's display name is recorded. If disabled, the literal string "Cleanup disabled" is used.
+- The speaker-filtering-used-fallback field is derived from the diarization summary's fallback flag, defaulting to false if no summary exists.
+- Archival errors are logged but never cause the pipeline to fail (fire-and-forget).
+
+### 12.7 Pipeline Locking
+
+The history system and the live recording pipeline share a mutex. The pipeline has two possible owners: live recording and transcription lab.
+
+- Acquisition succeeds and sets the owner only if no other owner holds the pipeline. Otherwise it returns false.
+- Release clears the owner only if the releasing party matches the current owner.
+
+At the application level, the pipeline lock is acquired before calling the experiment runner, and the runner receives no-op acquisition/release closures (always succeeding). The actual locking happens at the application layer. The pipeline is released in both success and failure paths.
+
+After a transcription re-run, the user's preferred speech model is restored if the experiment loaded a different one. This does not occur after cleanup re-runs.
+
+---
+
+## 13. Diarization Visualization
+
+When a history entry includes diarization results, the detail view displays a visualization.
+
+### 13.1 Data Model
+
+The visualization contains: total audio duration, target speaker identifier (optional), total kept audio duration, whether a fallback was used, fallback reason (optional), and a list of timeline spans (each with speaker identifier, start time, end time, and a kept flag). The visualization is absent when no diarization summary exists or no entry is selected.
+
+### 13.2 Rendering
+
+A colored horizontal bar where each span is proportional to its duration relative to the total. Kept spans use the accent color; filtered-out spans use a muted quaternary color. Each segment has a minimum rendered width of 3 points. The total duration for proportional rendering is the maximum of the entry's audio duration and the maximum span end time.
+
+A "Speaker filtering" heading appears with summary text and a caption row showing target speaker, kept duration, and fallback status.
+
+### 13.3 Fallback Reasons
+
+When speaker filtering used a fallback, the reason is displayed as:
+
+| Reason | Display Text |
+|---|---|
+| No usable speaker spans | "no usable speaker spans" |
+| No speaker reached threshold | "no speaker reached the selection threshold" |
+| Insufficient kept audio | "kept audio was too short" |
+| Filtered audio extraction failed | "filtered audio extraction failed" |
+| Empty filtered transcription | "filtered transcription came back empty" |
+
+---
+
+## 14. Text Diff Visualization
+
+The diff engine computes word-level differences between original and experiment text.
+
+### 14.1 Algorithm
+
+Both texts are tokenized by splitting on whitespace. The longest common subsequence is computed between the token lists. Using the LCS, a list of segments is produced, each classified as unchanged, inserted, or removed. Adjacent segments of the same kind are merged with spaces between tokens.
+
+### 14.2 Character-Level Refinement
+
+When a removed segment is immediately followed by an inserted segment and both are single words, a character-level diff is attempted using the same LCS algorithm. The refinement only replaces the word-level pair if the character-level diff contains at least one unchanged segment (partial overlap). Otherwise, the original word-level pair is preserved.
+
+### 14.3 Segment Data
+
+Each segment carries a kind (unchanged, inserted, or removed), text content, and a spacing flag indicating whether a leading space should be rendered. Equality comparison considers only kind and text, not the spacing flag.
+
+### 14.4 Visual Rendering
+
+- Unchanged segments: default text style, no special formatting
+- Inserted segments: green, bold, underlined
+- Removed segments: red, strikethrough
+
+The engine provides a function to reconstruct a plain string from diff segments, inserting leading spaces as indicated by segment spacing flags.
+
+---
+
+## 15. Reusable Visual Components
+
+### 15.1 Settings Card
+
+A container displaying a bold title (title3, semibold) followed by content, spanning the full width.
+
+### 15.2 Settings Field
+
+A labeled wrapper displaying a subheadline-weight title above content.
+
+### 15.3 Screen Recording Recovery View
+
+Red warning text reading "Ghost Pepper needs Screen Recording access. Grant it in System Settings, then return to Ghost Pepper." and an "Open Screen Recording Settings" button.
+
+### 15.4 Bordered Text Editor
+
+A text editor with a rounded rectangle border (12-point corner radius) using separator-color stroke. Height is computed from text content (20 points per line plus 28 points padding), clamped between configurable minimum and maximum heights.
+
+### 15.5 Read-Only Text Pane
+
+Displays text with selection enabled inside a scrollable container using the same bordered styling as the text editor, with the same line-count-based height computation.
+
+### 15.6 Key Cap Widget
+
+Displays a text label inside a rounded rectangle. When highlighted, the background is orange (or red when active/recording) and the text is white semibold. When not highlighted, the background uses the system control background color with secondary text color. The active-state transition uses a 0.2-second ease-in-out animation.
+
+---
+
+## 16. Application Lifecycle
+
+### 16.1 Automatic Termination
+
+The application disables automatic termination on appearance (reason: "Ghost Pepper keeps a persistent menu bar presence.") and re-enables it on the will-terminate notification, preventing the system from killing the process for inactivity.
+
+### 16.2 Single-Initialization Guard
+
+A boolean flag ensures initialization logic runs exactly once, even if the appearance handler fires multiple times.
+
+### 16.3 Update Checking
+
+The auto-update controller is lazily initialized (deferred until first access) to avoid startup cost if never used.
+
+### 16.4 Termination Cleanup
+
+On the will-terminate notification, the application cancels OCR prefetch and shuts down the cleanup backend.
+
+---
+
+## 17. Window Management Patterns
+
+All windows follow a consistent lifecycle.
+
+- Each window manager stores a single optional window reference. Showing reuses an existing window (bringing it to front) or creates a new one. Windows are configured not to be released when closed.
+- Closing hides the window rather than destroying it, keeping it available for reuse.
+- Window display is triggered through the central application state, which holds references to all window managers and exposes show methods.
+- The prompt editor and cleanup transcript windows resign the first responder before hiding, ensuring in-progress text edits are committed.
+- The recording overlay panel uses non-activating and borderless style with mouse-event ignoring. It joins all spaces and participates as full-screen auxiliary.
+- The debug log panel uses utility window style with floating behavior and does not hide on deactivation.
+- The onboarding window sets the activation policy to regular before showing. On completion, the window is closed and the reference released (unlike other windows, it is not preserved for reuse).
+
+---
+
+## 18. Cleanup Model Probe (Command-Line Tool)
+
+A command-line tool for testing the cleanup model pipeline outside the main application.
+
+### 18.1 Arguments
+
+| Flag | Required | Description |
+|---|---|---|
+| `--model` | Yes | Cleanup model variant. Accepts shorthand ("fast", "full") or raw identifiers. |
+| `--input` | No | Input text for one-shot mode. If omitted, enters interactive mode. |
+| `--prompt` | No | Custom cleanup prompt. Defaults to the standard prompt. |
+| `--window-context` | No | Inline screen context text. |
+| `--window-context-file` | No | Path to a file containing screen context text. |
+| `--thinking` | No | Thinking mode: "none" (default), "suppressed", or "enabled". |
+| `--interactive` | No | Explicitly requests interactive mode. |
+
+### 18.2 Validation
+
+The tool rejects: missing values for flags, unknown flags, invalid model identifiers, invalid thinking mode values, both `--window-context` and `--window-context-file` together, empty input text (after trimming), both `--input` and `--interactive` together, and failed file reads for `--window-context-file`. The `--model` flag is required; omitting it produces an error.
+
+### 18.3 Interactive Mode
+
+When `--input` is omitted, the tool enters interactive mode. It exits when the user types ":quit" (after trimming) or provides an end-of-file signal.
+
+### 18.4 Pipeline
+
+For each input, the tool:
+
+1. Resolves the effective prompt (provided or default), applying model-specific adjustments
+2. Builds the final prompt incorporating optional screen context, preferred transcriptions, and commonly misheard replacements
+3. Applies pre-cleanup deterministic corrections to the input text
+4. Wraps the corrected input in the standard cleanup input format
+5. Executes model inference with the formatted input, final prompt, model variant, and thinking mode
+6. Strips thinking blocks and orphan thinking tags from the raw output
+7. Applies post-cleanup deterministic corrections to the sanitized output
+
+### 18.5 Transcript Output
+
+The tool produces a transcript containing labeled sections in order: Model name and kind, Thinking mode, Elapsed time (formatted to two decimal places as seconds), Prompt, User input, Corrected input, Model input, Raw model output, Sanitized model output, Final cleaned output.
+
+### 18.6 Testability
+
+The execution function (model inference) is injectable, enabling testing without a real model. The correction store and prompt builder are also injectable, defaulting to new instances when not provided.

+# User Journey: Application Update
+
+**Journey ID:** J-APP-UPDATE
+**Status:** DRAFT
+
+---
+
+## Pre-conditions
+
+- Onboarding is complete and the application is running.
+- The auto-updater has been lazily initialized (happens when the full menu bar view is first displayed after onboarding).
+- A newer version of Ghost Pepper has been published: the developer has updated the appcast XML on the main branch of the GitHub repository with a new item containing an incremented build number, new DMG URL, and a valid EdDSA signature.
+
+---
+
+## Flow A: Automatic Update Check
+
+### Step 1: Scheduled Check Fires
+
+**System response (background, no user action):**
+1. The auto-updater is configured to check for updates automatically at a 24-hour interval.
+2. It fetches the appcast XML from the configured URL on the GitHub repository.
+3. The RSS 2.0 document is parsed.
+
+### Step 2: Version Comparison
+
+**System response:**
+1. The updater reads the version element (the internal build number) from the appcast item.
+2. It compares this against the running application's build version.
+3. If the appcast build number is higher, an update is available.
+4. The updater verifies the minimum system version from the appcast item matches or is below the current OS version.
+
+### Step 3: User Notified
+
+**System response:**
+1. The updater presents its standard update UI dialog (this is the update framework's built-in user interface, not custom Ghost Pepper UI).
+2. The dialog shows the new version title and the short version string.
+3. The user is offered options to install, skip, or remind later.
+
+### Step 4: Download and Verify
+
+**User action:** Clicks "Install Update" (or equivalent button).
+
+**System response:**
+1. The updater downloads the DMG from the GitHub release asset URL.
+2. It verifies the EdDSA signature on the downloaded DMG using the public key embedded in the application's property list.
+3. If the signature is invalid or the download is corrupted, the update is rejected and an error is shown.
+
+### Step 5: Install and Relaunch
+
+**System response:**
+1. The updater extracts the new application bundle from the DMG.
+2. The old application bundle is replaced with the new one.
+3. The application terminates and relaunches with the updated version.
+
+---
+
+## Flow B: Manual Update Check
+
+### Step 1: User Triggers Manual Check
+
+**User action:** Clicks the Ghost Pepper menu bar icon, then clicks "Check for Updates".
+
+**System response:**
+1. The "Check for Updates" button triggers an immediate update check via the auto-updater.
+2. It fetches the appcast and performs the same version comparison as in Flow A.
+
+### Step 2: Update Available
+
+**System response:**
+1. If an update is available, the update dialog appears (same as Flow A, Step 3).
+2. The user can proceed to download and install.
+
+### Step 3: No Update Available
+
+**System response:**
+1. If no update is available (the current version matches or exceeds the appcast version), the updater displays a "You're up to date" dialog.
+
+---
+
+## Flow C: Developer Release Workflow (Context)
+
+This flow documents how a new version reaches users, providing context for the update mechanism.
+
+### Step 1: Version Bump
+
+**Developer action:** Updates the version string and build number in the application's property list.
+
+### Step 2: Build DMG
+
+**Developer action:** Runs the build-DMG script.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The script reads version and build number from the property list.
+2. Builds a release configuration.
+3. Creates a compressed DMG.
+4. Generates an EdDSA signature using the update framework's signing tool.
+5. Prints the DMG path, size in bytes, and next steps.
+
+### Step 3: Notarize
+
+**Developer action:** Notarizes the DMG with Apple.
+
+### Step 4: Update Appcast
+
+**Developer action:** Updates the appcast XML with the new version number, build number, DMG size, download URL, and EdDSA signature. Commits and pushes to main.
+
+### Step 5: Create GitHub Release
+
+**Developer action:** Creates a GitHub release with the DMG attached using `gh release create`.
+
+### Step 6: Users Receive Update
+
+Running instances of Ghost Pepper will discover the new version on their next 24-hour check cycle or on manual check.
+
+---
+
+## Post-conditions
+
+- The application is running the new version.
+- The build version and marketing version reflect the updated values.
+- The updated application's updater configuration continues to point to the same appcast URL and uses the same EdDSA public key.
+- User preferences, model caches, correction store data, and lab archives persist across the update (they are stored in the application support directory and user defaults, not in the application bundle).
+
+---
+
+## Error Paths / Alternative Flows
+
+### E1: No Internet Connection
+
+If the appcast cannot be fetched:
+1. The scheduled check silently fails and will retry at the next 24-hour interval.
+2. A manual check may show an error dialog.
+
+### E2: EdDSA Signature Mismatch
+
+If the downloaded DMG's signature does not match the embedded public key:
+1. The updater rejects the update and shows an error.
+2. The current version continues running.
+
+### E3: macOS Below Minimum Version
+
+If the user's macOS version is below the appcast's minimum system version:
+1. The updater does not offer the update.
+
+### E4: Application Launched During Update
+
+The relaunch mechanism uses a system open command with the application bundle path to spawn the new instance, then terminates the current instance.

+# User Journey: Clipboard Fallback
+
+**Journey ID:** J-CLIPBOARD-FALLBACK
+**Status:** DRAFT
+
+---
+
+## Pre-conditions
+
+- A dictation session has completed: recording stopped, audio transcribed, text cleaned.
+- The pipeline is at the paste delivery stage.
+- The user's cursor is NOT in a recognized paste target (e.g., the user is focused on a non-editable area, the Finder, a read-only view, or an application whose accessibility tree does not expose an editable element).
+
+---
+
+## Step Sequence
+
+### Step 1: Paste Flow Begins
+
+**System response:**
+1. The paste-start callback fires, stamping the paste-start timestamp on the performance trace.
+2. The current clipboard contents are saved: all clipboard items with all type representations (rich content, images, files, etc.) are read and stored.
+3. The clipboard is cleared and the cleaned transcription text is written as plain text.
+
+### Step 2: Paste Target Detection Fails
+
+**System response:**
+1. The system queries the frontmost application's focused UI element via the accessibility API.
+2. If a focused element is found but it is NOT a paste target (disabled, not editable, no selected text range, value not settable, not a text entry role), the system returns false.
+3. If no focused element can be queried, the system falls back to the continuous paste target monitor's cached observation.
+4. The fallback observation is checked: it must match the current frontmost application's process ID and window ID, and have "editable" status. If it does not match or reports "non-editable", paste target detection returns false.
+
+**Alternative trigger:** Even if paste target detection succeeds, if the simulated keystroke cannot be prepared (e.g., insufficient accessibility permission for event creation), the same fallback path is taken.
+
+### Step 3: Clipboard Fallback Activates
+
+**System response:**
+1. The simulated Command+V keystroke is NOT sent.
+2. The transcribed text REMAINS on the system clipboard (it was written in Step 1).
+3. The original clipboard contents are NOT restored. The user retains the transcribed text on the clipboard for manual pasting.
+4. The system reports a "copied to clipboard" result.
+5. The paste-end callback fires, stamping the paste-end timestamp.
+
+### Step 4: Clipboard Fallback Overlay
+
+**System response:**
+1. The recording overlay shows the "Clipboard Fallback" message: primary text "Copied to clipboard", secondary text with a Command+V symbol followed by "to paste".
+2. The overlay uses a larger panel size (420x84 points) to accommodate the secondary text.
+3. The overlay has an auto-dismiss timer of 3 seconds (from the overlay's own mechanism).
+4. Additionally, the orchestrator schedules a separate 2.5-second auto-dismiss.
+
+### Step 5: User Manually Pastes
+
+**User action:** Switches to (or clicks on) an editable text field and presses Command+V.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The operating system's standard paste mechanism inserts the transcribed text from the clipboard.
+2. The text appears in the user's target application.
+
+### Step 6: Pipeline Completes
+
+**System response:**
+1. The performance trace is completed with the paste-end timestamp.
+2. The trace summary is written to the debug log.
+3. The application status returns to "ready".
+4. The pipeline is released.
+
+---
+
+## Post-conditions
+
+- The transcribed text is on the system clipboard, replacing whatever was there before.
+- The user's original clipboard contents are LOST (they were saved but not restored because the fallback path does not restore).
+- The user can manually paste the text via Command+V in any application.
+- No post-paste learning occurs because no paste session snapshot was captured (the simulated paste did not execute).
+- The recording has been archived to the transcription lab.
+- The application is in "ready" state.
+
+---
+
+## Error Paths / Alternative Flows
+
+### E1: Accessibility Permission Insufficient for Paste Detection
+
+If accessibility permission is not granted at the time of paste target evaluation:
+1. The focused element query returns false immediately.
+2. The clipboard fallback path activates.
+
+### E2: Accessibility Permission Insufficient for Event Creation
+
+If accessibility is granted enough for paste target detection but not for system event posting:
+1. The simulated keystroke creation fails (returns nil).
+2. The clipboard fallback path activates.
+
+### E3: Application with Non-Standard Accessibility Tree
+
+Some applications (e.g., games, custom-rendered UIs) do not expose standard accessibility elements:
+1. The direct focused element query may return nil.
+2. The accessibility tree search (up to depth 12) may find nothing.
+3. The continuous monitor's cached observation may show "non-editable" or have no observation.
+4. The clipboard fallback activates.
+
+### E4: Target Application in Full-Screen
+
+The recording overlay appears even in full-screen (it joins all spaces and is classified as full-screen auxiliary).
+The clipboard fallback behavior itself is unchanged.

+# User Journey: Correction Learning
+
+**Journey ID:** J-CORRECTION-LEARNING
+**Status:** DRAFT
+
+---
+
+## Pre-conditions
+
+- A dictation session has completed and text has been successfully pasted via simulated Command+V into a target application.
+- Post-paste learning is enabled (the post-paste learning setting is enabled; default is true).
+- Screen recording permission is granted (required for accessibility-based text extraction from the focused element).
+- A paste session snapshot was captured: the pasted text, frontmost application bundle identifier, window identifier, focused element frame, and focused element text.
+
+---
+
+## Step Sequence
+
+### Step 1: Post-Paste Learning Session Begins
+
+**System response:**
+1. After the paste completes, the paste session data is delivered to the post-paste learning coordinator via the paste-session callback.
+2. The coordinator captures the paste session's focused element text as the baseline text (the text field content at paste time).
+3. The first poll is scheduled with zero delay.
+
+### Step 2: User Edits the Pasted Text
+
+**User action:** The user notices a transcription error in the pasted text and corrects it. For example, the pasted text reads "I use ghost paper for dictation" and the user changes "ghost paper" to "Ghost Pepper".
+
+### Step 3: Polling Observes the Edit
+
+**System response (repeated up to 16 times over 15 seconds):**
+1. Each poll reads the current focused element text via the accessibility API.
+2. The observation verifies that the user is still in the same application where the paste occurred (comparing the current frontmost application's bundle identifier to the paste session's bundle identifier). If the application has changed, the observation returns nil.
+3. If text is available and the baseline was nil, it is captured as baseline.
+4. If the text changed from the last observation, the stable poll count resets to 0.
+5. If the text is unchanged from the last observation, the stable poll count increments.
+6. If the stable poll count reaches 2 (the required stable count, meaning 2 consecutive unchanged observations at the 1-second poll interval), the learning algorithm is invoked.
+7. If the completed poll count reaches 16 (the maximum for the 15-second window), polling stops and the learning window expires.
+
+### Step 4: Replacement Inference
+
+**System response (when the quiescence threshold is reached):**
+1. Both the baseline text and the observed text must be non-empty after trimming, and must differ (case-insensitive comparison).
+2. Both texts are split into word arrays. A shared prefix (case-insensitive) and shared suffix (excluding prefix) are computed.
+3. At least one shared prefix or suffix word must exist -- the change must be embedded within existing text.
+4. The differing word segments between the shared prefix and suffix are extracted as the "wrong" side and "right" side.
+5. Both sides are validated: neither can exceed the maximum replacement word count (2 words).
+
+### Step 5: Replacement Stored
+
+**System response (when a valid narrow replacement is inferred):**
+1. The replacement pair is stored (e.g., wrong: "ghost paper", right: "Ghost Pepper").
+2. The store checks: if the replacement's wrong or right side is empty, or an identical replacement already exists, it is silently ignored.
+3. The new entry is formatted as "ghost paper -> Ghost Pepper" and appended to the commonly misheard draft text with a newline separator.
+4. The full draft text is re-parsed to update both the structured replacement list and the persisted text in user defaults.
+
+### Step 6: Learned Correction Overlay
+
+**System response:**
+1. The learned-correction callback fires.
+2. The recording overlay shows a "Learned correction" message with a green checkmark: primary text "Learned correction", secondary text "ghost paper -> Ghost Pepper".
+3. The overlay auto-dismisses after 3 seconds.
+
+### Step 7: Subsequent Dictation Uses the Learned Correction
+
+**User action (later):** The user dictates again. The speech model again produces "ghost paper".
+
+**System response:**
+1. During the cleanup pipeline, pre-cleanup deterministic corrections run.
+2. The deterministic correction engine applies corrections:
+   a. Preferred transcriptions are protected from modification.
+   b. Misheard replacements are applied longest-first. "ghost paper" is replaced with "Ghost Pepper" via case-insensitive word-boundary matching.
+   c. Protected preferred transcriptions are restored.
+3. Additionally, if OCR window context is enabled, the preferred transcriptions are passed to the OCR engine as custom words, improving recognition of "Ghost Pepper" in on-screen text.
+4. The correction hints are included in the LLM prompt, providing the model with the misheard replacement mapping.
+5. The corrected text "Ghost Pepper" appears in the final output.
+
+---
+
+## Post-conditions
+
+- A new entry "ghost paper -> Ghost Pepper" exists in the commonly misheard replacements list.
+- The entry is visible in the Settings > Corrections section.
+- Future dictations that produce "ghost paper" will be automatically corrected via deterministic corrections (before and independently of LLM cleanup).
+- The correction is also hinted to the LLM via the prompt's correction hints section.
+
+---
+
+## Error Paths / Alternative Flows
+
+### E1: User Switches Applications During Observation Window
+
+If the user switches to a different application within the 15-second window:
+1. The observation detects the frontmost application has changed (bundle identifiers do not match).
+2. The observation returns nil. No text is captured.
+3. Polling continues but produces no useful observations until the window expires.
+
+### E2: User Makes a Broad Edit (More Than 2 Words Changed)
+
+If the user rewrites a larger section of text:
+1. The word-level diff extracts a "wrong" or "right" segment with more than 2 words.
+2. The replacement exceeds the maximum replacement word count (2).
+3. The replacement is rejected. No correction is learned.
+
+### E3: Edit Has No Shared Context
+
+If the user completely replaces the text (no shared prefix or suffix word):
+1. The algorithm requires at least one shared prefix or suffix word.
+2. The replacement is rejected. No correction is learned.
+
+### E4: Duplicate Replacement Already Exists
+
+If the user triggers a correction that is already in the commonly misheard list:
+1. The store detects the duplicate.
+2. The append is silently ignored. No overlay is shown.
+
+### E5: Screen Recording Permission Not Granted
+
+If screen recording permission was not granted:
+1. The accessibility API may not be able to read the focused element's text.
+2. The observation returns nil on each poll.
+3. The learning window expires without learning.
+4. In Settings, a recovery prompt directs the user to enable Screen Recording.
+
+### E6: User Does Not Edit Within 15 Seconds
+
+If the text remains stable (matching baseline) through all 16 polls:
+1. The quiescence threshold is reached quickly (text unchanged = stable from the start).
+2. The learning algorithm is invoked with baseline and observed being equal (or both matching the pasted text).
+3. Since the texts do not differ, the validation check fails.
+4. No correction is learned.
+
+### E7: Post-Paste Learning Disabled
+
+If the post-paste learning setting is disabled:
+1. The learning coordinator is never invoked after paste.
+2. No polling occurs. No corrections are learned automatically.

+# User Journey: Debug Investigation
+
+**Journey ID:** J-DEBUG-INVESTIGATION
+**Status:** DRAFT
+
+---
+
+## Pre-conditions
+
+- Onboarding is complete and the application is running.
+- The user wants to investigate a performance issue or unexpected behavior during dictation.
+- The debug log may already contain non-sensitive operational entries (hotkey events, model lifecycle, OCR status, performance traces) from previous sessions -- these are always recorded and persisted.
+
+---
+
+## Step Sequence
+
+### Step 1: Open the Debug Log Viewer
+
+**User action:** Clicks the Ghost Pepper menu bar icon, then clicks "Debug Log..."
+
+**System response:**
+1. The debug log viewer window opens.
+2. The viewer increments the live viewer count from 0 to 1.
+3. With the viewer count now > 0, sensitive recording requests will be captured going forward.
+4. The window displays all currently stored log entries. Entries are formatted as `[HH:mm:ss] [Category] message`, separated by double newlines.
+
+### Step 2: View Existing Entries
+
+**System response:**
+1. The viewer shows up to 250 entries (the capacity limit), loaded from the debug log file in the application's support directory.
+2. Entries fall into five categories:
+   - **Hotkey** -- keyboard shortcut detection and recording mode transitions.
+   - **OCR** -- screen context capture status (not content).
+   - **Cleanup** -- sensitive cleanup details (only present if viewer was open during past sessions).
+   - **Model** -- model lifecycle events.
+   - **Performance** -- pipeline timing summaries.
+
+### Step 3: Perform a Dictation While Viewer is Open
+
+**User action:** Holds the push-to-talk chord, speaks, and releases.
+
+**System response:**
+1. Non-sensitive entries are recorded as usual:
+   - Hotkey events: "Recording started via push-to-talk", key events, chord matches.
+   - OCR events: "No frontmost-window OCR context was captured" (or success status).
+   - Model events: "Model loaded", "empty transcription" notices.
+2. Because the live viewer count is > 0, sensitive entries are also captured:
+   - Raw transcription text (the user's speech as transcribed).
+   - Cleanup configuration details (whether cleanup is enabled, attempted, which backend).
+   - Window context summary (whether screen context was captured).
+   - Final cleaned output text (the post-cleanup result).
+3. All entries (sensitive and non-sensitive) are persisted to the debug log file on every record operation.
+4. The live viewer updates in real-time as entries are added.
+
+### Step 4: View Performance Traces
+
+**User action:** Looks for Performance-category entries.
+
+**System response:**
+1. Each completed dictation produces a performance trace summary as a single space-separated key=value line.
+2. The summary contains:
+   - `session=<uuid>` -- unique session identifier.
+   - `speechModel=<id>` -- which speech model was used.
+   - `cleanupBackend=<value>` -- cleanup backend setting.
+   - `hotkey_to_mic_live=<N>ms` -- latency from hotkey detection to microphone active.
+   - `hotkey_lift_to_mic_cold=<N>ms` -- latency from key release to microphone stopped.
+   - `transcription=<N>ms` -- transcription duration.
+   - `cleanup=<N>ms` or `cleanup=skipped` -- cleanup duration.
+   - `ocr=<N>ms` or `ocr=skipped` -- OCR capture duration.
+   - `prompt_build=<N>ms` -- prompt assembly duration.
+   - `model_call=<N>ms` -- LLM inference duration.
+   - `post_process=<N>ms` -- post-processing duration.
+   - `paste=<N>ms` -- paste delivery duration.
+   - `total=<N>ms` -- total pipeline duration.
+3. Missing values display as "n/a".
+
+### Step 5: Identify Performance Bottleneck
+
+**User action:** Examines the trace durations to find which stage is slow. For example:
+- `transcription=850ms` suggests the speech model is slow (consider switching to a faster model).
+- `model_call=3200ms` suggests the cleanup LLM is slow (consider switching to a smaller model).
+- `hotkey_to_mic_live=180ms` suggests audio engine startup latency.
+- `hotkey_lift_to_mic_cold=220ms` includes the 200ms flush delay.
+
+### Step 6: Clear the Debug Log (Optional)
+
+**User action:** Clicks the "Clear" button in the debug log viewer.
+
+**System response:**
+1. All entries are removed from the in-memory collection.
+2. The empty state is immediately persisted to disk (the JSON file is overwritten with an empty array).
+
+### Step 7: Close the Debug Log Viewer
+
+**User action:** Closes the debug log viewer window.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The live viewer count is decremented from 1 to 0.
+2. With the viewer count back at 0, subsequent sensitive recording requests will be silently discarded.
+3. Sensitive entries that were captured while the viewer was open remain in the file on disk until they age out of the 250-entry ring buffer or are manually cleared.
+4. Non-sensitive entries continue to be recorded and persisted to disk regardless of whether the viewer is open.
+
+---
+
+## Post-conditions
+
+- Non-sensitive operational log entries continue to accumulate in the debug log file across sessions.
+- If the user cleared the log, the file contains an empty array.
+- Sensitive entries captured during the viewer session are persisted on disk until evicted by the 250-entry capacity limit.
+- No sensitive content is captured after the viewer is closed.
+
+---
+
+## Error Paths / Alternative Flows
+
+### E1: Debug Log File Corrupted
+
+If the debug log file is corrupted or contains invalid JSON:
+1. On initialization, the store starts with an empty entry list. No error is surfaced.
+
+### E2: File Write Failure
+
+If the debug log file cannot be written (disk full, permission error):
+1. The write failure is silently suppressed. The entry exists in memory but is lost to persistence.
+
+### E3: Privacy Considerations
+
+**Notable behavior:** All recorded entries (including sensitive ones captured while the viewer was open) are persisted to the debug log file in the application's support directory and survive application restarts.
+
+The effective privacy guarantee is that sensitive user content (raw transcriptions, cleanup details, cleaned output) is only captured when the debug viewer window is open. But once captured, it persists on disk.
+
+### E4: No Entries (Fresh Install)
+
+If no debug log file exists:
+1. The store starts empty.
+2. Entries begin accumulating as soon as the application runs (non-sensitive categories are always recorded).

+# User Journey: First Run
+
+**Journey ID:** J-FIRST-RUN
+**Status:** DRAFT
+
+---
+
+## Pre-conditions
+
+- macOS 14.0 (Sonoma) or later on Apple Silicon hardware.
+- Ghost Pepper has been downloaded as a DMG from the GitHub releases page and the application has been dragged to /Applications.
+- The application has not been launched before on this user account (the onboarding-completed preference is false or absent).
+- Approximately 1.5 GB of free disk space is available for initial model downloads.
+
+---
+
+## Step Sequence
+
+### Step 1: Application Launch
+
+**User action:** Double-click Ghost Pepper (or launch via Spotlight/Launchpad).
+
+**System response:**
+1. The application makes itself visible in the Dock and Command+Tab switcher during onboarding.
+2. The system checks the onboarding-completed flag. Since it is false, the onboarding wizard is displayed instead of initializing the full application.
+3. The menu bar icon appears but the dropdown shows only "Show Setup Window" and "Quit" -- not the full menu.
+4. The auto-updater is NOT initialized at this point; it is deferred until the full menu bar view is first displayed after onboarding.
+5. Automatic termination is disabled to prevent macOS from killing the menu bar app while idle.
+
+### Step 2: Welcome Screen (Onboarding Step 1)
+
+**User action:** Views the welcome screen.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The onboarding window appears at 480x620 points with the title "Ghost Pepper".
+2. The screen displays: the application icon (128x128), the title "Ghost Pepper", the tagline "Hold-to-talk speech-to-text for your Mac", a privacy badge reading "100% Private -- Everything runs locally on your Mac. No cloud, no accounts, no data ever leaves your machine." with a green shield icon, and a "Get Started" button.
+
+**User action:** Clicks "Get Started".
+
+**System response:** Advances to Step 2 (Setup).
+
+### Step 3: Setup Screen -- Microphone Permission (Onboarding Step 2)
+
+**User action:** Views the setup screen.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The heading "Setup" with subheading "Grant permissions and download the app models" is displayed.
+2. Four setup rows appear: Microphone, Accessibility, Screen Recording (optional), and AI Models.
+3. On appear, existing permission states are checked. If microphone is already authorized (rare on first run), the level meter starts immediately.
+4. A 2-second polling timer starts for accessibility and screen recording status, since the user may grant those in System Settings outside the app.
+5. If models are not yet ready, model loading begins automatically.
+
+**User action:** Clicks the "Grant" button on the Microphone row.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The system invokes the combined check-and-request operation. Since the status is "not determined" on first launch, the OS presents its standard microphone permission dialog.
+2. The application has declared the microphone usage description: "Ghost Pepper needs microphone access to record your voice for transcription."
+
+**User action:** Clicks "OK" / "Allow" in the system dialog.
+
+**System response:**
+1. Microphone status transitions to "authorized".
+2. Input devices are enumerated and a device picker appears if more than one input is available.
+3. A live microphone level meter starts, showing a colored bar (green for normal, orange above 30%, red above 70%).
+4. A green checkmark appears on the Microphone row.
+
+### Step 4: Setup Screen -- Accessibility Permission
+
+**User action:** Clicks the "Grant" button on the Accessibility row.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The system prompts for accessibility authorization. macOS presents its standard accessibility permission dialog.
+2. The dialog directs the user to System Settings > Privacy & Security > Accessibility.
+
+**User action:** Enables Ghost Pepper in System Settings, then returns to the app.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The 2-second polling timer detects the permission change and updates the accessibility grant flag.
+2. A green checkmark appears on the Accessibility row.
+
+### Step 5: Setup Screen -- Screen Recording Permission (Optional)
+
+**User action:** Clicks the "Enable" button on the Screen Recording row (or skips this step).
+
+**System response (if Enable is clicked):**
+1. The system schedules a deferred relaunch to compensate for macOS potentially killing the app after granting screen recording: a background process waits 3 seconds and then re-opens the application.
+2. The system requests screen recording permission and opens the Screen Recording settings pane.
+
+**User action:** Enables Ghost Pepper in the Screen Recording settings, then returns to the app.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The 2-second polling timer detects the screen recording grant and updates the UI.
+2. If both accessibility and screen recording are now granted, the polling timer self-invalidates.
+3. A green checkmark appears on the Screen Recording row.
+
+**Alternative flow (skip):**
+1. The row displays "You can enable this later in Settings."
+2. Screen recording is NOT a completion gate; the user can proceed without it.
+
+### Step 6: Setup Screen -- Model Downloads
+
+**System response (concurrent with permission steps):**
+1. On appearing, if the speech model is not yet loaded, loading begins automatically.
+2. The default speech model (~466 MB, "Accuracy" tier) begins downloading if not cached.
+3. The default cleanup model (~535 MB, 0.8B parameter tier) begins downloading if cleanup is enabled (default: true).
+4. Download progress is shown in the AI Models row. An onboarding model summary displays the selected speech and cleanup model rows with their download status.
+5. Models are stored under the application's support directory in dedicated subdirectories for speech and cleanup models respectively.
+
+**User observation:** The progress indicator shows download progress. On completion, a green checkmark appears on the AI Models row.
+
+### Step 7: Setup Complete -- Continue
+
+**Pre-condition for Continue:** All three required conditions must be met: microphone granted, accessibility granted, and models ready. Screen recording is NOT required.
+
+**User action:** Clicks "Continue".
+
+**System response:** Advances to Step 3 (Try It).
+
+### Step 8: Try It Screen (Onboarding Step 3)
+
+**System response:**
+1. The screen instructs the user to "Hold Right Command + Right Option and say something" and displays key cap widgets for both keys.
+2. A dedicated hotkey monitor is created, configured with the default push-to-talk chord (Right Command + Right Option).
+3. If the monitor fails to start, it retries up to 5 times at 2-second intervals.
+
+**User action:** Holds Right Command + Right Option and speaks.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The key caps turn red to indicate recording is active.
+2. A fresh audio recorder starts capturing microphone input in 16kHz mono float32 format.
+
+**User action:** Releases the keys.
+
+**System response:**
+1. Recording stops (with the 200ms flush delay).
+2. The audio buffer is transcribed using the loaded speech model.
+3. If transcription produces text, the result is displayed for 2 seconds with a success message: "It works! Your words will be pasted wherever your cursor is."
+4. The step auto-advances to Step 4. A guard prevents advancing more than once.
+
+**Alternative: Skip/Continue buttons are always available.**
+
+### Step 9: Done Screen (Onboarding Step 4)
+
+**System response:**
+1. A green checkmark icon, the heading "You're All Set!", a menu bar mockup showing where Ghost Pepper lives, a capability list, and a "Start Using Ghost Pepper" button are displayed.
+
+**User action:** Clicks "Start Using Ghost Pepper".
+
+**System response:**
+1. The onboarding-completed flag is set to true in user defaults.
+2. The completion callback fires, triggering full application initialization.
+3. The application reverts to menu-bar-only mode (no Dock icon).
+4. The onboarding window is dismissed.
+
+### Step 10: Post-Onboarding Initialization
+
+**System response (automatic):**
+1. Launch-at-login is registered on first run (one-shot, guarded by a preference flag).
+2. Microphone permission is verified (already granted during onboarding); initialization proceeds with permission prompts suppressed since permissions were just acquired.
+3. The audio engine is pre-warmed to reduce first-recording latency.
+4. Paste target focus tracking starts (the continuous paste target monitor begins observing the frontmost application).
+5. The speech model is confirmed loaded (or loading resumes).
+6. The hotkey monitor starts, installing the global event tap.
+7. The cleanup model state is refreshed.
+8. The application status transitions from "loading" to "ready" once both the speech model has loaded and the hotkey monitor has started.
+9. The auto-updater is lazily initialized when the full menu bar view is first displayed.
+10. The menu bar dropdown now shows the full set of controls (Settings, Debug Log, version, Check for Updates, Quit).
+
+---
+
+## Post-conditions
+
+- The onboarding-completed flag is true; subsequent launches skip onboarding.
+- Microphone and accessibility permissions are granted. Screen recording may or may not be granted.
+- The default speech model and default cleanup model are downloaded and cached on disk.
+- Launch-at-login is registered.
+- The application is in the "ready" state, running as a menu bar icon with no Dock presence.
+- The global hotkey event tap is installed and listening for the default chord (Right Command + Right Option for push-to-talk).
+- Automatic update checks are enabled at a 24-hour interval.
+
+---
+
+## Error Paths / Alternative Flows
+
+### E1: Microphone Permission Denied
+
+If the user denies microphone access in the system dialog:
+1. The "Grant" button changes to "Open Settings" which opens the Microphone privacy pane.
+2. The "Continue" button does not appear because microphone is a completion gate.
+
+### E2: Accessibility Permission Not Granted in Time
+
+If the user does not enable accessibility:
+1. The 2-second polling timer continues checking.
+2. The "Continue" button does not appear because accessibility is a completion gate.
+
+### E3: Model Download Failure (Timeout)
+
+If the speech model download times out:
+1. The system checks for timeout retry eligibility and retries once after 500ms.
+2. If the retry also fails, the model transitions to error state. The AI Models row shows a "Retry" button.
+
+### E4: Hotkey Monitor Fails on Try-It Step
+
+If the hotkey monitor cannot start:
+1. It retries up to 5 times with a 2-second delay.
+2. After exhausting retries, the error message reads: "Could not start hotkey monitor. Please verify Accessibility is enabled in System Settings."
+3. Skip and Continue buttons remain available.
+
+### E5: macOS Kills App After Screen Recording Grant
+
+If macOS terminates the application when screen recording is granted:
+1. The deferred relaunch (scheduled before the permission request) fires after 3 seconds and re-opens the application.
+2. On re-launch, the onboarding flag is still false, so the user returns to the onboarding wizard.
+
+### E6: User Closes Onboarding Window
+
+If the user closes the onboarding window without completing:
+1. The menu bar dropdown still shows only "Show Setup Window" and "Quit".
+2. Clicking "Show Setup Window" brings the onboarding window back.
+3. On next application launch, onboarding will show again since the flag is still false.

+# User Journey: Model Management
+
+**Journey ID:** J-MODEL-MANAGEMENT
+**Status:** DRAFT
+
+---
+
+## Pre-conditions
+
+- Onboarding is complete and the application is running.
+- The Settings window can be opened.
+
+---
+
+## Flow A: Downloading a New Speech Model
+
+### Step 1: User Opens Models Section
+
+**User action:** Opens Settings > Models.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The Models section shows three cards: Speech model, Cleanup model, and Runtime models.
+2. The "Runtime models" card displays a model inventory showing the status of all speech and cleanup models (cached/not cached, loaded/not loaded).
+
+### Step 2: User Selects a New Speech Model
+
+**User action:** Changes the speech model picker to a model that is not yet cached (e.g., "Multilingual", ~466 MB).
+
+**System response:**
+1. The model manager resolves the requested model name against the catalog of 4 speech models.
+2. If a different model is currently loaded, the system clears all loaded model instances and resets to idle.
+3. The system transitions to "loading" state and clears any previous error.
+
+### Step 3: Model Download
+
+**System response:**
+1. The speech models directory is ensured to exist under the application's support directory.
+2. The model is not cached on disk (its cache directory does not exist).
+3. Download begins from the remote repository. Progress is reported as a fraction (0.0 to 1.0).
+4. Progress is cleared after download completes.
+
+### Step 4: Model Initialization
+
+**System response:**
+1. The speech engine is initialized with verbose output disabled, error-only logging, pre-warming disabled, and loading enabled.
+2. On success, the model manager transitions to "ready" state.
+3. The speech model user preference is updated.
+4. The inventory display updates to show the new model as cached and loaded.
+
+---
+
+## Flow B: Downloading a New Cleanup Model
+
+### Step 1: User Selects a Larger Cleanup Model
+
+**User action:** Changes the cleanup model picker from "0.8B Very fast" to "4B Full".
+
+**System response:**
+1. The cleanup model state machine checks current state.
+2. If a different model is loaded (the 0.8B), the state machine transitions through: Ready -> Downloading or Loading (for the new model).
+3. The selected cleanup model preference is updated.
+
+### Step 2: Download Phase
+
+**System response:**
+1. The model file existence is checked under the application's models directory (GGUF format).
+2. If not cached, download begins from HuggingFace (~2.8 GB for the 4B model).
+3. The model state transitions to "downloading" with progress from 0.0 to 1.0.
+4. The menu bar dropdown shows a linear progress bar for the cleanup model download.
+5. The Runtime models card shows active download text (e.g., "Downloading ModelName (XX%)...").
+
+### Step 3: Load Phase
+
+**System response:**
+1. After download, the file is moved to the models directory.
+2. The model state transitions to "loading".
+3. Model initialization runs asynchronously in the background.
+4. The model parameters are configured: temperature 0.1, thinking mode suppressed, system prompt set to the default cleanup prompt.
+5. On success, the state transitions to "ready".
+
+---
+
+## Flow C: Viewing Model Inventory
+
+### Step 1: View Runtime Models
+
+**User action:** Views the "Runtime models" card in the Models section.
+
+**System response:**
+1. A model inventory card displays the status of all speech and cleanup models.
+2. For each speech model, the card shows: title, variant label, approximate size, and whether it is cached on disk.
+3. For each cleanup model, the card shows: model name, parameter count, approximate size, max token count, speed label, and whether it is cached.
+4. Speech model cache status is determined by checking for the model directory under the speech models subdirectory.
+5. Cleanup model cache status is determined by checking for the GGUF file under the cleanup models subdirectory.
+6. The third-party speech model cache status is checked via its framework's own cache verification method.
+
+---
+
+## Flow D: Switching Back to a Previously Downloaded Model
+
+### Step 1: Select a Cached Model
+
+**User action:** Switches the speech model to one that was previously downloaded (e.g., back to "Accuracy").
+
+**System response:**
+1. The model manager detects that the model is cached on disk.
+2. No download is needed. The model is loaded directly from disk.
+3. Loading is fast (no network I/O). The status transitions to "ready" quickly.
+4. The user preference is updated.
+
+---
+
+## Flow E: Third-Party Speech Model with Speaker Filtering
+
+### Step 1: Select the Third-Party Speech Model
+
+**User action:** Switches the speech model to the third-party backend option (e.g., "Parakeet v3").
+
+**System response:**
+1. The system verifies the model configuration is valid.
+2. Download and loading follow the third-party framework's pipeline (models are cached in the framework's own default cache directory, not under the application's tree).
+3. A speech recognition manager is created and initialized with the loaded models.
+4. The "Ignore other speakers" toggle in the Recording section becomes functional (it was previously disabled because the default speech models do not support speaker filtering).
+
+### Step 2: Enable Speaker Filtering
+
+**User action:** Toggles "Ignore other speakers" ON in Settings > Recording.
+
+**System response:**
+1. The ignore-other-speakers setting is persisted.
+2. On the next recording, a recording session coordinator will be created with a diarizer wired to receive audio chunks.
+3. Diarization models are lazily downloaded from HuggingFace on first use.
+
+---
+
+## Post-conditions
+
+- All downloaded models are permanently cached on disk. There is no automatic eviction or expiration.
+- The selected speech model and cleanup model preferences are persisted in user defaults.
+- The application uses the newly selected models for all subsequent dictation sessions.
+- Worst-case total disk usage for all model caches is approximately 7 GB.
+
+---
+
+## Error Paths / Alternative Flows
+
+### E1: Model Download Timeout
+
+If a speech model download times out:
+1. The error is checked for retry eligibility: URL timeout code or "timed out" in the description.
+2. One retry is attempted after 500ms.
+3. If the retry fails, the model manager transitions to "error" state.
+
+If a cleanup model download fails:
+1. The cleanup model state transitions to "error" with a message.
+2. The error is displayed in the Cleanup settings card.
+
+### E2: Model Not Found in Catalog
+
+If a stored preference refers to an unknown model identifier:
+1. For speech models: the model manager transitions to error with a "not found" error.
+2. For cleanup models: the system falls back to the default 0.8B model.
+
+### E3: Insufficient Disk Space
+
+The application does not explicitly check disk space before downloading. If the download succeeds but disk is full during write, the platform's file I/O error propagates through the model loading pipeline and transitions the model to error state.
+
+### E4: Cleanup Model Load While Another Load in Progress
+
+If a load is requested while another load is already in progress:
+1. The requester waits until the loading state resolves.
+2. If the loaded model matches the requested model, the requester proceeds.
+3. Otherwise, a new load begins.
+
+### E5: Third-Party Model Configuration Error
+
+If the third-party speech model configuration is invalid or missing required fields:
+1. The system fails with a configuration error.
